summary: Phuket offers excellent internet, abundant coworking options, and beach lifestyle appeal—but the short-stay cost (€32–45/night hotels + €5–10 coworking) and 7-day limit strain Riza's €2,000 budget significantly. The city excels for calls and AI work, weak for classical music and techstart scene. Visa-free 30 days is perfect; best fit is November–February (dry season). Fair overall fit; trade-offs on culture and pace.
remote_work_pitch: Phuket's coworking ecosystem is mature and pro-nomad: Denz (mountain views, 1,000 Mbps fiber), Place Coworking (24/7, 5 floors, phone booths), and Grind Time (gym, pool, community) all support 3+ hour calls without interruption. Fixed fiber runs 100–300 Mbps standard; mobile 5G averages 136 Mbps—both excel for video clients across timezones. But the tourist premium on short stays and limited startup/AI founder scene mean Riza loses network value compared to Bangkok or Chiang Mai.
best_months: November–March (dry, 25–32°C, low humidity, peak season Dec–Jan pricing)

internet.notes: Coworking spaces (Denz, Place, Grind Time) guarantee fiber backbone; mobile backup strong (AIS dominates Rawai, Chalong, Kathu). Call reliability excellent; upload stable for Claude API, n8n workflows. Public WiFi inconsistent—coworking essential for client work.

work_spots.name: Denz Coworking Cafe
work_spots.price_eur: Day pass ~€4–5 (150 THB), week ~€20–25 (700 THB/7 days discounted)
work_spots.best_for: calls, focus, community (French Bulldogs, mountain views, cafe food)
work_spots.notes: Complimentary coworking for cafe customers; 1,000 Mbps business fiber; quiet, vibe matches indie builders; drawback: hilltop location (taxi-only access, no walk from Patong center). Food quality high (Thai + Western). Sunset views inspire. Best for concentrated client calls.

work_spots.name: Place Coworking
work_spots.price_eur: Day ~€6 (200 THB), flexible memberships; week estimate ~€30–35
work_spots.best_for: phone calls, long hours, professional meetings (dedicated desks, meeting rooms)
work_spots.notes: 5-floor, 1,645 sq m space; high-speed fiber, bright workstations, rooftop. Central Chalong location (good transit access). Phone booths dedicated—critical for client calls. Cafe on-site, event spaces. Corporate vibe, less community than Denz, but call infrastructure superior. Affordable for Phuket standard.

work_spots.name: Grind Time Coworking
work_spots.hours: 7 AM–7 PM daily (approx); check for evening access
work_spots.notes: 3 levels, dedicated silent floor, on-site gym, pool table, kitchen. Social events weekly. Water views, Rawai location quiet and residential. Friendly owners. Drawback: no explicit phone booths—call background might include ambient noise. Gym + pool appeal to Riza (lifting, swimming).

work_spots.name: The Glass House Cafe & Space
work_spots.price_eur: Coffee ~€2 (70 THB), expect €5–10 spend for 3+ hour session
work_spots.best_for: long focus work, local vibe, budget laptop sessions
work_spots.notes: Hybrid cafe-coworking in renovated Sino-Portuguese shophouse; excellent for solo focus work. Central Phuket Town; low tourist premium. No phone booths, casual noise—suitable for async work (Claude API, coding), not client calls. Best for breaks from coworking intensity.

stays.name: Beyond Patong Hotel
stays.why: 3-star, 3 min walk to Patong Beach, gym, free parking, pool, no kitchenette but solid value. Best budget mid-range balance in Patong; walkable beach access aligns with car-free preference. Under €365 leaves €1,636 for coworking (€35/week) and food (€35–40/day = €245–280), fitting €2k budget.

stays.name: Denz WorkStay Rooms
stays.why: Purpose-built live-work rooms directly beneath Denz cafe; 1,000 Mbps fiber, work desk, sunset views. Eliminates daily commute friction. Trades location isolation (hilltop) for seamless work setup. €420 + €35 coworking (covered) + €245 food = €700/week total. Tight but works; specialized for Riza's workflow.

stays.name: Tropica Bungalow Beach Hotel
stays.why: Budget 2/3-star, Karon (quieter than Patong), beach access, pool. Lowest nightly rate options in May (off-season pricing). €336 + €50 coworking + €280 food = €666/week. Leaves €334 buffer for transit, gym passes, dining treats. May is low season (cheaper, fewer tourists); trade-off: hotter, some rain.

visa.details: Turkish citizens exempt via visa-free entry (30 days, confirmed via Royal Thai Embassy Ankara + multiple sources). No requirement to present Slovak Pobyt at Thai immigration. Entry requires: valid passport (6+ months), onward travel proof (return flight), funds proof (10,000–20,000 THB per person). Extendable once for +30 days locally (1,900 THB fee). For stays >60 days, tourist or ED visa required; not applicable here. No residency registration for ≤30 days.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: AQI typically 50–80 (Good). Feb–April haze risk from regional burns; May–Sep monsoon air cleaner. Phuket airport area, Patong/Karon: low industrial pollution. Beach areas better than Phuket Town center. No AQI advisories typical.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Solo night walks in Patong/Bangla Road safe but busy, petty theft (bag snatching) rare but possible. Avoid conspicuous valuables. Rawai/Kata/Karon significantly safer, quiet after sunset. Scams: overpriced tuk-tuk rides (use Grab app, set price first), gem shops (tourist trap—avoid), bar girls. Generally safer for solo male/female than many SE Asia cities; Phuket police visible, expat-friendly. Violent crime rare. Motorbike theft: lock well.

weather.conditions: May: hot, humid, monsoon onset (frequent afternoon rain, 8–9 rainy days/month). June–Oct: heavy monsoon (sea rough, diving off). Nov–Feb: dry, sunny, 23–30°C, perfect.

options.name: Patong Beach
options.quality: Sandy, busy, wave currents, lifeguards present. Tourist hub. Monsoon (Jun–Oct) rough water, undertow risk.
options.transit_from_center: Walkable from central Patong hotels (5–10 min); from Kathu/Denz: taxi/songthaew (15 min, 50–80 THB)

options.name: Karon Beach
options.quality: Longer, softer sand, calm bay, fewer tourists than Patong. Good for daily swimming.
options.transit_from_center: From Karon hotels: 10 min walk. From Patong: songthaew south (20 min, 30 THB).

options.name: Grind Time Coworking (on-site pool) + Hotel pools (Beyond Patong, Tropica)
options.quality: Hotel pools: basic but functional. Grind Time: small, refreshing post-work dips.

options.name: Grind Time Coworking (integrated gym)
options.facilities: Cardio machines, free weights (basic), classes. Small but functional.
options.notes: Included with coworking membership (monthly ~€100–130). Community vibe, social. Quiet gym atmosphere post-9 AM. No pool.

options.name: Fit Box Gym Patong
options.facilities: Full weights, cardio, classes (Muay Thai, yoga), air-conditioned. Modern.
options.notes: Popular with expats. Day pass or short-term passes available. Strength training focus suits Riza (lifting). Central Patong location, walkable from Beyond Patong Hotel.

options.name: Kata Fitness Center
options.facilities: Weights, cardio, spinning, yoga classes. Pool adjacent (small).
options.notes: Beach vibe, quieter than Patong. If staying Tropica Bungalow (Karon), very close. Yoga + lifting balance.

gym.running_options: Patong promenade (beachfront 3 km loop, safe, sunrise/sunset ideal). Karon beach flat sand good for morning runs. Hill routes around Kathu/Patong area moderate intensity.
gym.yoga_pilates: Multiple studios: Phuket Yoga Studio (Patong), Ashtanga Yoga Phuket (Kata), Thai Yoga Wellness (Phuket Town). Classes €8–12/drop-in, €60–80 monthly. English widely spoken. Yoga aligns with Riza's fitness + leisure interests.

day_trips.hiking: Khao Phra Thaeo National Park (Phuket north): 20 km from Patong, accessible by songthaew + short walk to Ton Sai Falls, bang waterfall trek (1–2 hrs easy). Similan Islands (via Khuraburi port, 90 min drive from Patong): world-class diving/snorkeling, multi-day trips. Karon Viewpoint: 10 km south, 30-min scenic hike with panoramic vistas (often cited as best hike near Patong). Phang Nga Bay kayaking day trip: scenic, 90 min from Patong.

marinas.name: Yacht Haven Marina
marinas.transit_from_center: From Patong: 20 min songthaew/taxi south (€5–8). From Chalong hotels: walkable/5 min.

marinas.name: Royal Phuket Marina
marinas.transit_from_center: From Patong: 30 min taxi north (€8–12). Full-service marina, upscale.

marinas.name: Ao Po Grand Marina
marinas.transit_from_center: From Patong: 25 min taxi (€10). Alternative charter base, less crowded than Chalong.

sailing.best_season: Nov–Apr (dry, light winds, calm seas). May–Oct monsoon swells, rougher water, fewer charters.
sailing.notes: Phuket's sailing scene robust: Chalong Bay hub for bareboat/crewed charters. Intro lessons €80–120 per half-day. Day charters (sunset, snorkel trips) €150–300/person common via hotels. Similan/Phi Phi Islands multi-day liveaboards €600–1200/person. Strong cruising culture; many charter companies cater to beginners. Riza's sailing interest well-served; easy access from any hotel via marina shuttles or taxis. Wind conditions Nov–Feb ideal; June–Sep challenging.

new_hobbies.venue: Tiger Muay Thai & MMA Training Camp (Patong)
new_hobbies.notes: Phuket world-renowned for Muay Thai. Drop-in classes 50–80 baht (~€1.50–2.50), membership 3,000 THB/month. Combines cardio + strength. Popular with nomads. Fits Riza's gym/cardio interest and Phuket's identity.

new_hobbies.venue: Phuket Dive Center (Chalong) or local dive shops
new_hobbies.notes: PADI Open Water cert (3–4 days, €200–300). Phuket's diving culture strong (Similan Islands, Phi Phi). Freediving courses (SSI, €350–450) 2–3 days. Aligns with swimming passion; excellent stress relief. Intro snorkel trips €40–60/day via hotels.

new_hobbies.venue: Phuket Thai Cooking School (also offers language) or Private tutors (Preply, italki filtered for Phuket-based)
new_hobbies.notes: Group classes 10–15 €/hour, private tutors €10–15/hr. Cooking schools often bundle language + culture. Practical for nomad life; complements B1 German fluency. Enriches local relationships, useful for market/vendor negotiations. Low-cost, high-value addition.

new_hobbies.venue: Phuket Freediving Centers (Chalong/Patong focus on 'apnea' static/dynamic underwater breathing)
new_hobbies.notes: Static apnea (breath-holding focus): distinct from diving certification. 2-day intro courses €150–200. Growing community in Phuket; advanced athletes visit. Combines meditation + physical challenge. Lower equipment cost than SCUBA; pair with swimming habit.

new_hobbies.venue: Dive shops (Phuket Dive Center, Siam Diving) offer underwater photography add-ons. GoPro rental €8–15/day.
new_hobbies.notes: Underwater macro photography guides + snorkel trips with rental camera €80–120/day. Complements diving/snorkeling. Can monetize via travel photography if side interest. Phuket's coral reefs, sea life vivid subjects. Strong nomad photography community in local forums.

coworking_retreats.notes: No major curated coworking retreat operators (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, Sun and Co, NomadX, Mokrin House, Work Wanderers) currently running Phuket-based group experiences. Phuket coworking scene is mature (individual spaces), but lacks the group-living retreat model common in Chiang Mai, Bali, or Portugal. Denz's WorkStay rooms + cafe community come closest to 'live-work' blend, but not a curated retreat. Riza should focus on individual coworking day memberships + hotel accommodation separately.

car_free.walkability: Patong: High (beachfront, shops, restaurants all walkable 10–15 min). Karon: Moderate (beach walkable, inland requires songthaew/taxi). Phuket Town: Low (car-centric, sprawling). Kathu (Denz location): Low (hilltop, isolated—taxi/Grab only).
car_free.public_transport: Songthaew (blue pickup-truck buses): 30–50 THB (~€0.90–1.50) per ride, frequent Patong↔Phuket Town↔southern beaches. Phuket Smart Bus (airport shuttles, limited schedule). Grab app (Uber equivalent) reliable, €2–8 rides within Phuket. No metro/rail. Day pass concept doesn't exist; pay-per-trip. Budget €10–15/week (5–7 rides) for short stay. Monthly: €40–60 realistic for comfort nomad lifestyle. Car-free viable in Patong/Karon; Kathu/Chalong require taxis.
car_free.airport_to_center: Phuket International (HKT) 32 km from Patong: Grab €12–18, Phuket Smart Bus €3–4 (1 hour, limited schedules), taxi meter ~€15–20. Recommend Grab (fast, reliable). No train; songthaew minimal late-night service. 45–60 min typical.

startup_community.scene_summary: Phuket has a growing but thin tech founder/AI community compared to Bangkok or Chiang Mai. Digital nomads abundant (coworking full), but startup density low. Few VC-backed AI companies; mainly freelance developers, ecommerce consultants, lifestyle businesses. Bangkok dominates Thailand's startup ecosystem; Phuket skews lifestyle/tourism-first. For Riza (Claude API, n8n side products), coworking networking possible but unlikely to surface high-caliber AI builders. Best to treat Phuket as productivity retreat, not founder/peer hub.

key_venues.name: Grind Time Coworking
key_venues.what: Weekly networking events, demo nights (occasional). Social community strongest here. Good for casual founder chats.

key_venues.name: Denz Coworking Cafe
key_venues.what: Indie/creative builder vibe (software engineers, digital marketers frequent). Less formal events; 'co-eat' communal table fosters ad hoc networking.

key_venues.name: Phuket Maker Space / Tech Meetups (via Meetup.com, Lu.ma)
key_venues.what: Sparse but existent: check Meetup/Lu.ma for 'Phuket Startups,' 'Digital Nomads Phuket,' 'Blockchain Phuket' (niche). Frequency: 1–2 events/month.

startup_community.recurring_events: No regular demo days or pitch events. Coworking workshop series sporadic. Best strategy: leverage global online communities (IndieHackers, ProductHunt, Twitter/X) while in Phuket; use coworking for accountability partners, not investor/co-founder sourcing.

bonus.flight_from_vie: Vienna (VIE) → Phuket (HKT): 11–13 hrs (1 stop, typically via Dubai/Bangkok). €350–550 one-way mid-season (May low, Dec–Jan high). Direct flights rare; common route VIE→BKK (11 hrs, €300–400) + domestic BKK→HKT (2 hrs, €30–100). Total ~14–16 hrs door-to-door.

timezones.vs_new_york: +12 hours (Phuket ahead; 12 noon Phuket = midnight prior day NYC)

bonus.classical_music: Weak scene. Occasional concerts at Phuket Town Cultural Center or hotel events; not a hub. Annual Phuket International Music Festival (dates vary, spring typical) but small scale. Bangkok Philharmonic Orchestra (Bangkok, 800 km) is nearest serious classical venue. Riza should arrange classical music listening privately (Apple Music, Spotify) or pursue via Bangkok day trip if needed. Low priority in Phuket decision.
bonus.dance_scene: Bachata/Salsa: Moderate. Patong has Latin dance clubs (occasional live bands, cover bands). Dedicated bachata/salsa studios sparse; more prevalent in Bangkok. Local Thai dance (Classical, Contemporary) rich but different genre. Patong nightlife venues (e.g., Carlsberg Pub, Dady'O) host DJ Latin nights Fri–Sat. Riza would find social dancing possible but not a scene strength. Suggest connecting via Eventbrite/Meetup 'Salsa Phuket' groups or Bangkok day trips for classes.

concerns: May is off-season (hot, humid, rain, fewer services reduced). Better to shift visit to Nov–Feb.
Denz (top coworking for Riza's workflow) sits on Patong Hill—isolation requires taxi/Grab every trip (kills car-free preference, adds €3–5/day cost).

Phuket's short-stay hotel rates (€45–55/nt peak, €30–40/nt low season) hit budget hard. 7 days = €280–350 accommodation alone (vs €500–600 if monthly). Full €2,000 budget very tight; food/coworking squeeze.
Classical music & tech founder scene both weak—less cultural/professional enrichment than Chiang Mai or Bangkok.
Monsoon season (Jun–Oct) poor for sailing/swimming side interests.
Tourist premium on food/activities vs local price (Phuket 15–30% pricier than Chiang Mai, esp. restaurants).
Scooter/Grab dependence (no public transit integration); Riza car-free preference strained in quieter areas.

summary: <cite index="3-8">Playa del Carmen is one of the best places for digital nomads thanks to its growing infrastructure, increase in tourism, and almost endless amount of things to do during downtime.</cite> For Riza specifically: solid internet for client calls (50+ Mbps in coworking), mobile-friendly coworking with phone booths, tight community, but requires eTA visa clearance pre-trip and faces seaweed/touristy premium pricing. Best for Dec–Apr; a 7-day window fits €2000 budget comfortably.
remote_work_pitch: <cite index="31-5,31-6">Playa del Carmen is a great city for digital nomads, offering a vibrant expat community, affordable cost of living, and plenty of coworking spaces and cafes with a relaxed atmosphere conducive to remote work.</cite> <cite index="6-38">Bunker Coworking claims to have the fastest wifi of all the coworking spaces in Playa del Carmen, at speeds of up to 200+ Mbps.</cite> Meeting rooms and phone booths are available at Nest, Bunker, and Cowork-In for video calls with Vienna/UK/Japan clients; strong indie-hacker presence at meetups and coworking community events.
best_months: December, January, February, March, April

internet.notes: <cite index="31-8,31-9">Internet connectivity in Playa del Carmen is generally good, especially in the central areas and in expat-friendly neighborhoods. The speed is typically sufficient for most digital nomad needs, including video calls.</cite> Coworking spaces offer 100–200 Mbps; cafes 20–30 Mbps. Backup 4G via Telcel recommended for calls.

work_spots.name: Nest Coworking
work_spots.notes: <cite index="3-14,3-15,3-16">Nest is by far the most popular coworking space in Playa del Carmen. Located in the heart of the beach center of the city, it attracts freelancers, entrepreneurs, and start-ups alike. The comfortable space offers meeting rooms, open spaces, and a separate creative wing.</cite> Solar-powered, pet-friendly, unlimited tea/coffee. Two meeting rooms (4–16 capacity). Roost Terrace for calls, Community Garden.

work_spots.name: Bunker Coworking
work_spots.notes: <cite index="3-26,3-27,3-28">Bunker Coworking is definitely a hip spot. Just a few blocks from the beach, this space has shared offices, private offices, and virtual offices for its clients. It's open 24/7, so it fits your schedule no matter what time zone you're working with.</cite> No natural light (bunker-like); very stable/fast wifi; new desks.

work_spots.name: Cowork-In (Playacar Phase II)
work_spots.notes: <cite index="3-29,3-30,3-31,3-32">If you're looking for one of the quietest workspaces in Playa del Carmen, you'll want to head over to WORK-IT. It's cozy, it's professional, and it's the perfect place to put your head down and finish that project. Located across the street from the popular Coco Bongo Club, this coworking space has everything you need for a productive work day. They have three high-speed internet networks to make sure you never lose connection.</cite>

work_spots.name: Choux Choux Cafe
work_spots.notes: <cite index="33-13,33-14,33-15">Choux Choux is probably the most popular digital nomad cafe in Playa del Carmen. At Nest alone, I'd met two nomads who recommended it to me. For me personally, it's the kind of place I'd come for just a coffee or some breakfast as it can get quite busy.</cite> Strong cafe culture; busy but welcoming.

stays.name: Urban Towers – Downtown Contemporary Apartment
stays.why: Central location, kitchen, modern, good reviews (2624), under-budget fit for 7 nights (~€29/nt). Walkable to coworking, cafes, 5th Ave.

stays.name: Hotel Hulku (Boutique)
stays.why: <cite index="15-4">5 Avenida Entre Calle 24 Y Calle 26, Excellent reviews (1812), min price $28 USD/night.</cite> Boutique feel, good service, central, fits within budget (€35/nt est = €245/7 days).

stays.name: Riviera Maya Suites (Serviced Apartment)
stays.why: <cite index="10-1,10-2">Riviera Maya Suites has 12 exclusive apartments of two and three bedrooms, specially designed for family vacations. The apartments are fully equipped with high-quality appliances to fulfill all your needs and exceed your expectations.</cite> Pool, elevator, rooftop solarium, beachfront. Premium option; fits within €2000 total budget (€840 + coworking + food).

visa.details: <cite index="21-3,21-5">Turkish citizens planning to visit Mexico need to obtain an Electronic Travel Authorization (eTA) before their journey. The Mexican Electronic Travel Authorization is a mandatory travel document for Turkish citizens planning to enter Mexico for tourism or business purposes.</cite> <cite index="24-6">Those that have valid Canada, Japan, United States, United Kingdom and/or Schengen visa/living permission exempt from the visa process.</cite> Since Riza holds Slovak Pobyt (EU residence), he should verify if EU residence card exempts him; otherwise, eTA required (apply online, ~€35–40, 72-hr turnaround). Tourist Visitor Permit (FMM) issued on arrival; max stay 180 days. No work permit needed for remote work for foreign clients.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: No major AQI concerns year-round. Tropical coastal air; occasional dust/seaweed smell (seasonal, especially May–Oct).
air_water_safety.safety_notes: <cite index="30-35,30-36,30-37">The police are very corrupt and known to rob tourists walking home from the bar at night. You won't get bothered by gangs, it's the police you have to worry about. It's safe for the most part except for the random cartel hits that happen in broad daylight.</cite> **Practical advice**: Avoid 5th Ave late night (tourist scams, petty theft); stick to Centro/Playacar; use Uber/taxi after dark; no solo night walks to isolated areas. Daytime is safe. Tourist zones (beach, malls, coworking) are well-policed. Solo night safety is low; exercise caution.

ecommerce_events.name: Playa del Carmen Digital Nomad Meetup (Facebook Groups)
ecommerce_events.relevance: Adjacent—networking with ecommerce/software entrepreneurs, indie hackers. Strong expat tech community.

weather.conditions: <cite index="40-3,40-5">Playa del Carmen exhibits a tropical wet and dry climate. Throughout the year, weather in Playa del Carmen consistently exhibits warm to hot temperatures coupled with meaningful seasonal rainfall.</cite>

options.name: Playa del Carmen Beach (Centro & Playacar)
options.quality: <cite index="30-31,30-32,30-33">Playa del Carmen is a super touristy beach town and the catch is, the beach is always full of seaweed, so if you expect to swim in it, have fun. I stayed there for 5 months and only remember the beach being clear 2-3 times. The seaweed stinks so that means the beach stinks.</cite> Water is warm, crystal-clear away from shore, but seaweed buildup is significant year-round (worse Jun–Oct).
options.transit_from_center: Walk 10–15 min from Centro to Mamitas Beach or 5th Ave beach access.

options.name: Jardín del Edén (Garden of Eden Cenote)
options.quality: Natural sinkhole with freshwater, crystal-clear, no seaweed. Excellent for swimming/snorkeling.
options.transit_from_center: ADO bus or taxi ~15 min south of Centro.

options.name: Hotel/Airbnb Pools (Riviera Maya Suites, Nest Coworking area)
options.quality: Private or boutique pools; clean, chlorinated, seaweed-free.

options.name: Gold's Gym Playa del Carmen
options.facilities: Weights, cardio, free weights, cardio machines, group classes, sauna, steam room
options.notes: International standard chain; English-speaking staff; popular with expats and tourists. Good equipment, clean.

options.name: Gym Fitness Playa (Local)
options.facilities: Weights, functional fitness, cardio, classes (Spanish-primary, some English)
options.notes: Local, budget-friendly; quieter than Gold's; basic but well-maintained. Strong community vibe.

options.name: Hotel Gym (Riviera Maya Suites / Acanto / other boutiques)
options.facilities: Weights, cardio, some functional areas, some with pool access
options.notes: Available to hotel guests; day-pass for non-guests. Limited but convenient if staying on-site.

gym.running_options: <cite index="37-41">It's a very walkable city and you can get from one end of downtown to the other in half an hour. Riding bikes is also popular with some bike lanes and a city bike scheme available.</cite> Playacar has a dedicated bike path (near beach, 2–3 km loop). 5th Avenue for strolling/light runs. Colonia Hollywood & inland neighborhoods quieter for long runs.
gym.yoga_pilates: Multiple studios in Centro and Playacar; English-language classes available at Nest Coworking (workshops) and local studios. ~€15–20/class or €80–120/month packages.

day_trips.hiking: Xel-Há (cenote hikes, ~30 min south by taxi), Xcaret park (zip-lines, jungle trails, ~20 min), Aktun Chen cave system (stalactite hiking, ~45 min). ADO buses or taxis recommended (car-free friendly via colectivo).

marinas.name: Marina Xel-Há
marinas.transit_from_center: ADO bus (~45 min) or taxi (~35 min, €40–50). No direct car-free route; coordinate with operator for shuttle.

marinas.name: Cozumel (nearby island)
marinas.transit_from_center: Ferry terminal (15-min walk from 5th Ave). Ferry cost €12–18 each way, 45-min crossing. Cozumel is a major sailing hub.

schools.name: Cozumel Sailing Club / Agua Clara Diving & Sailing

sailing.best_season: November–April (dry, lower hurricane risk, steadier winds)
sailing.notes: Playa itself has no large marina; sailing hub is Cozumel (35 km east) and Xel-Há. Caribbean Sea conditions excellent Dec–April. June–October hurricane season (low risk but possible).

new_hobbies.venue: Playa del Carmen Beach / Laguna Petempich
new_hobbies.notes: Caribbean trade winds (15–20 knots year-round) are ideal for kitesurfing. Riviera Maya is a known kite destination. Winter months (Dec–Feb) have best and most consistent winds. Multiple schools offer 2–3 hour intro lessons.

new_hobbies.venue: Cenotes (Jardín del Edén, Ponderosa) & Cozumel Reefs
new_hobbies.notes: Cenotes are calm, clear freshwater—ideal for beginner freediving training (static apnea, depth). Coastal schools offer AIDA or SSI certifications. Riza swims; freediving adds a meditative, solo skill and accesses unique cenote ecosystem.

new_hobbies.venue: Studio Ritmo (Centro) / Local Dance Schools
new_hobbies.notes: Playa has strong Latin dance scene (Dominican/Puerto Rican influence). Merengue is harder than bachata but fits Riza's salsa interest. Group classes 2–3x/week; drop-in welcome.

new_hobbies.venue: Makemake Artisan Collective (Xel-Há/Centro partnerships)
new_hobbies.neighborhood: Industrial zone (south of Centro) / Collaborating studios
new_hobbies.notes: Caribbean wood-crafting (local hardwoods, small furniture/art). Workshops often community-based; fits indie-hacker DIY mindset. Not widely advertised; inquire at coworking spaces or local craft groups.

new_hobbies.venue: Reef tours (Palancar Reef, Cozumel) / Cenote snorkel trips
new_hobbies.notes: Playa/Cozumel reefs are world-class for marine biodiversity. Underwater photography adds creative dimension; compatible with swimming interest. GoPro or underwater mirrorless setup; guides teach composition + marine ID.

coworking_retreats.notes: No major curated remote-work retreats (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, NomadX, Mokrin House) are based in or regularly operating in Playa del Carmen as of May 2026. The city attracts solo nomads and couples but lacks the structured group-retreat model. Closest is Selina (hostel+coworking, but shared rooms—outside Riza's stay constraints). Recommend Nest or Bunker coworking for daily community events and networking instead.

car_free.walkability: <cite index="30-24">The city has so many conveinces and is very walkable - it's perfect for someone looking to work and enjoy themselves.</cite> Centro is highly walkable (5th Ave, beach access, coworking all within 15–20 min walk). Playacar slightly less walkable but still bikeable.
car_free.public_transport: <cite index="32-8">The city bus and colectivo (shared van) network is limited, so get used to walking, riding a bike or taking a taxi.</cite> ADO buses connect to Cancún, Tulum, Cozumel (fixed routes, ~€3–8). Colectivos (shared vans) run North–South along main avenues (~€1–2). Taxi/Uber standard for longer trips (€5–15 within city). Bike rental widely available (€5–10/day, dedicated lanes on 5th Ave & Playacar).
car_free.airport_to_center: Cancún International Airport (CUN, 50 km north). ADO bus: ~€15, 1.5 hrs. Shared shuttle: ~€25–30 pp. Taxi: ~€50–70 (collective) or €80–100 (private). No car rental needed; Uber/taxi + ADO buses sufficient.

startup_community.scene_summary: <cite index="1-9">The city is home to a large community of remote workers, freelancers, and entrepreneurs who venture here to find the perfect balance of work and play.</cite> Playa has an indie-hacker/solopreneur culture at coworking spaces (Nest, Bunker) and Facebook groups, but lacks venture-backed startups and structured accelerators. Strong ecommerce/SaaS freelancer presence (Amazon consultants, Shopify builders). Tech meetups occur sporadically; AI/Claude API interest emerging but informal.

key_venues.name: Nest Coworking
key_venues.what: Community hub; hosts weekly workshops, networking events, pitch sessions. Most accessible for founders.

key_venues.name: Bunker Coworking
key_venues.what: Professional, quiet vibe; attracts agency owners and productized service founders. Less community-focused but more business-oriented.

key_venues.name: Digital Nomads Playa del Carmen Cancun Tulum (Facebook)
key_venues.what: ~3k+ members; weekly meetups, skill-shares, co-founder introductions. Primary social/professional network.

startup_community.recurring_events: Nest hosts monthly workshops (productivity, marketing, AI tools). Facebook group organizes weekly coffee meetups. Informal tech talks at cafes (Choux Choux, Bendito). No formal demo day or pitch event; scene is grassroots.

bonus.flight_from_vie: <cite index="32-20,32-21">Cancún International Airport, Cancún. Buses run to Playa del Carmen from across Mexico, with easy transfers across the border to the US, Belize, Guatemala and beyond.</cite> VIE→CUN: typically 1–2 stops (Frankfurt, Amsterdam, Madrid), 11–16 hrs total. Direct flights rare. Cost €300–500 return depending on season. Riza would arrive in Cancún, then ADO bus 1.5 hrs to Playa.

timezones.vs_new_york: -1 hour (Playa is EDT+1 when NY is EDT)

bonus.classical_music: **Limited scene.** No world-class concert halls (unlike Mexico City or Guadalajara). Occasional performances at resort venues and cultural centers; mostly tourist-oriented. High-quality classical largely unavailable. If Riza prioritizes live classical, Playa is weak; digital streaming recommended. Cancún or Mexico City (short trips) offer better access.
bonus.dance_scene: <cite index="30-16,30-17">If you're looking to party or shop Playa is a good place. Check out the cenotes, use the ADO to travel up and down the coast.</cite> **Bachata/Salsa**: Strong Dominican community; bachata lessons at multiple studios (€10–15/class). Salsa nights at La Vela nightclub (5th Ave) & Palazzo nightclub. Meringue gaining traction. Live bands Thu–Sat. Not professional-competition-grade but high-energy social scene.

concerns: <cite index="40-2,40-16">Ideal times for visiting Playa del Carmen fall between December and April.</cite> Riza's 7-day stay (unspecified month) risks hurricane season (Jun–Nov) if booked then; recommend Dec–May window.
Seaweed buildup year-round (worst Jun–Oct) detracts from swimming/beach quality unless at private cenotes or hotel pools.
<cite index="49-19">Tap water is not safe to consume;</cite> must buy bottled water (small cost, but environmental impact).
Police corruption reported; solo night walks after bar-hopping risky. Riza must stay in well-lit, populated areas (Centro/Playacar) after 10pm.

<cite index="34-7,34-8">Many recommend avoiding 5th Avenue due to overpriced food, scams, and safety issues. Everything is marked up and full of scams.</cite> Local restaurants away from tourist strip offer better value and authenticity.
Limited classical music venues; if this hobby is critical, Playa falls short. Streaming or day trips to Cancún recommended.
eTA visa (Turkish citizens) must be obtained pre-trip online; allow 72 hrs minimum.
No direct car-free ADO access to some day-trip sites (hiking trails, remote cenotes); may require taxi/coordinated shuttle, adding cost.

summary: The Netherlands' bold, modern port city: striking contemporary architecture, an edgy creative-and-design energy, Europe's biggest harbour, strong internet, English everywhere, and lower costs than Amsterdam. Forward-looking and unpretentious. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Rotterdam is Amsterdam's cheaper, edgier sibling: a rebuilt-modern city of bold architecture and design, a major port and innovation economy, excellent fiber, a big English-speaking community, and good coworking (CIC Rotterdam, StartDock). It's less touristy and more affordable than the capital while keeping all the Dutch infrastructure, and it's same-timezone with home. Dutch weather is grey, and the national housing crunch makes rentals competitive.
best_months: May to September; grey wet winters

internet.notes: Fast fiber (NL averages ~112 Mbps); free wifi widespread

work_spots.name: CIC Rotterdam / StartDock
work_spots.notes: CIC is a startup hub with events; desks from ~EUR250/month

work_spots.name: Markthal & centre cafes
work_spots.notes: Design-forward cafes and the central library

stays.name: Apartment (centre / Kralingen)
stays.why: Cheaper than Amsterdam (1-bed ~EUR1,200); book early given the tight market

visa.details: On your Slovak permit you enter visa-free, but the Netherlands is Schengen, so this COUNTS toward your 90/180.

air_water_safety.safety_notes: Generally safe; mind bikes

ecommerce_events.name: Port/cleantech, design and startup events; CIC community

weather.conditions: Maritime; mild damp summers, grey wet winters

options.name: Hoek van Holland / Scheveningen beaches; pools
options.quality: North Sea beaches ~30-45min away; city pools and a summer urban beach

options.name: City gyms (Basic-Fit, TrainMore)
options.notes: Plus big climbing gyms and harbour watersports

gym.running_options: The Kralingse Plas lake, the Maas riverfront, and the Erasmus Bridge

day_trips.hiking: The Kinderdijk windmills, the Zeeland coast and beaches, and Delft/The Hague/Amsterdam by fast train

sailing.notes: Harbour and river sailing, with the Zeeland delta nearby for coastal sailing

new_hobbies.hobby: Urban kayaking / SUP in the harbour
new_hobbies.venue: Maas and harbour kayak/SUP rentals
new_hobbies.notes: Paddling among Rotterdam's modern harbour architecture is a striking way to see the city from the water

coworking_retreats.notes: City coworking rather than retreats

car_free.walkability: Walkable, very bikeable; metro, trams and great rail; no car needed
car_free.public_transport: Metro + trams + bikes; fast trains across the Randstad
car_free.airport_to_center: Rotterdam (RTM) ~15min, or Schiphol (AMS) ~45min by train (~30 EUR local taxi)

startup_community.scene_summary: A real innovation scene (port-tech, cleantech, design, the CIC hub) with an international, English-first culture and good value. Strong for design/sustainability and ecommerce networking.

key_venues.name: CIC Rotterdam
key_venues.what: Startup hub and events

bonus.flight_from_vie: Direct VIE-AMS ~1h50, then ~45min train (or RTM)
bonus.classical_music: De Doelen, home of the acclaimed Rotterdam Philharmonic
bonus.dance_scene: Good club scene; some salsa/bachata socials

concerns: Less classic-pretty than Amsterdam (modern by design)

sources.name: Citizen Remote Netherlands
sources.what: Rotterdam modern/affordable vs Amsterdam, port economy, English

summary: San Sebastián is a small, walkable Basque coastal city with strong remote-work fundamentals — solid coworking, beach swimming, world-class food, and a real classical/jazz scene — but it's expensive for a 4-day stay and lacks an AI/indie-hacker community. Best as a focused short trip combining work, swimming, and pintxos rather than a deep nomad base.
remote_work_pitch: Compact, car-free city where you can walk everywhere between Teila Fabrika/Impact Hub, La Concha beach swims at lunch, and pintxo dinners. Direct Iberia/Vueling/Volotea connections via Bilbao or EAS keep VIE access reasonable, and Spain shares Riza's timezone — clients in UK/EU/UAE/Japan/Australia stay manageable.
best_months: May, June, September (warm-dry shoulder; July-August are crowded/expensive, October-February are wet)

work_spots.name: Teila Fabrika
work_spots.notes:
High-speed wifi, phone booth (cabina para llamadas), AC, kitchen, meeting rooms, printer
. 
Memberships from €216/month
. 
4.6/5 on Google
.

work_spots.name: Impact Hub Donostia
work_spots.notes:
Located in one of the city's main cultural buildings, multiple work zones for focus, meetings, workshops; community of freelancers, creatives and impact-driven professionals
. Global Impact Hub network access.

work_spots.name: Regus Donostia (Avenida de la Libertad 17)
work_spots.notes:
Renovated contemporary building with classic façade, designer workplaces and collaborative areas
. Most central option for client calls.

work_spots.name: Old Town Coffee
work_spots.notes: Specialty roaster, laptop-tolerant, reliable wifi, power at most tables. Not for calls — busy.

stays.name: Hotel SANSEbay
stays.why:
Located in the old town, 299 ft from Calle Mayor and 820 ft from Constitución Plaza
. 
Apartment units include full kitchen, seating area and living room (note: not all units have AC)
. Walking distance to Teila Fabrika and beaches.

stays.name: Villa Eugenia Boutique Hotel
stays.why:
Quiet location, classy stay, AC, private bathrooms, soundproofing, work desk and mini-bar — ideal for business travelers
. 10-min walk to Anoeta and centro.

stays.name: Hotel Arbaso
stays.why:
Prime location next to cathedral, comfortable modern rooms many with separate sitting areas
. 
4-star, free bikes, restaurant and bar
. Best for client-call-friendly desk + walkable to everything.

visa.details: Turkish citizen with Slovak Pobyt residence permit travels visa-free within Schengen for up to 90 days in any 180-day period. Spain is Schengen — count days toward the 90/180 ceiling along with Vienna base.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Atlantic coast, AQI typically <30. No notable bad-air months.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Very safe city, fine for solo nights. Pickpocketing in Parte Vieja during summer crowds and festivals; otherwise minimal scams.

weather.conditions:
Oceanic, mild and humid; 
weather can get quite rainy, especially October to February

options.name: Playa de La Concha
options.quality: Iconic crescent bay, calm, swimmable May–October

options.name: Playa de Zurriola
options.quality: Surf beach, stronger waves, north side

options.name: Polideportivo Paco Yoldi
options.quality: Public indoor 25m pool, year-round

options.name: Metropolitan San Sebastián (Hotel María Cristina area)
options.facilities: weights, cardio, classes, pool, sauna, spa
options.notes: Premium chain, English-speaking staff

options.name: Altxerri Sport Club
options.notes: Local favorite, Spanish/Basque primary

options.name: Basic-Fit Donostia
options.notes: Budget option, day pass via app trial

gym.running_options: Paseo de La Concha promenade (3km flat seafront); Monte Urgull and Monte Igueldo for hill repeats
gym.yoga_pilates: Bodhidharma Yoga (Gros), Studio Lemon Pilates — most classes in Spanish, some English on request

day_trips.hiking: Monte Ulia coastal trail (start at Plaza de Zaragoza, no transit needed); Pasaia–San Pedro fishing village hike via bus 33 from Boulevard

marinas.name: Real Club Náutico de San Sebastián

schools.name: Navegavela
schools.notes: Two classrooms — one at Real Club Náutico de San Sebastián in the heart of the capital, and one in Irun; offers Blended and Face-to-face PER courses

schools.name: Real Club Náutico San Sebastián Escuela de Vela

sailing.notes: Concha Bay is sheltered; offshore Cantabrian Sea gets choppy. Active regatta calendar through Real Club Náutico.

new_hobbies.venue: Pukas Surf Eskola
new_hobbies.notes:
The northern coast of Spain, including San Sebastian, is famous for its surfing; the main season is September to April, and La Zurriola is the most consistent spot — a beach break with a-frame waves
. Zurriola is right in town, no transit needed.

new_hobbies.venue: Mimo San Sebastián (Hotel María Cristina)
new_hobbies.notes: San Sebastián is the world capital of pintxos and home to the Basque Culinary Center — Mimo runs hands-on pintxo classes in English.

new_hobbies.venue: Lazkaoko Maizpide / AEK Donostia
new_hobbies.notes:
Locals speak the local dialect of Euskera — absolutely not related to Spanish
. Unique to the Basque Country and a real conversation starter.

new_hobbies.venue: Apnea Donosti
new_hobbies.notes: Cantabrian coast has rocky coves and visibility — coastal freediving culture exists alongside surfing.

new_hobbies.venue: Petritegi Sagardotegia (Astigarraga)
new_hobbies.notes: Bus G2 from Donostia. Genuine Basque tradition (Jan–April peak) — not just dinner, but pouring cider straight from the kupela barrel.

options.name: Coworksurf San Sebastián listings
options.notes:
Coworking, coliving, working remotely and surfing in San Sebastian — one of Spain's top surf destinations, suited for remote work and surfing
. Note: these are co-living, not the private stays in 'stays' above.

options.name: The Social Hub San Sebastián
options.notes:
Starting from €900/month
. Hybrid hotel/coworking/coliving — group common areas. Note: not within the 4-day private-stay budget.

coworking_retreats.notes: Limited dedicated curated retreats (Sun and Co/Outsite don't operate here). Surf-coworking is the main scene.

car_free.walkability:
Excellent — 
about 20 minutes to stroll through the heart of the city
car_free.public_transport: Dbus city buses + Topo metro; single ride ~€1.85, day options via Mugi card
car_free.airport_to_center:
From Bilbao Airport (BIO): PESA bus direct to Donostia, ~1h15, €17. From EAS (Hondarribia): bus E21, ~35 min, €2.55. 
From EAS the distance is about 15 km, ~30 minutes by taxi
 (~€35).

startup_community.scene_summary: San Sebastián is not an AI/indie-hacker hub — the local tech scene leans toward food-tech (Basque Culinary Center), gastronomy startups, and academic research at Tecnun. Bilbao is the bigger regional tech node. Few Claude/n8n builders specifically; expect to be the only AI builder in the room.

key_venues.name: Impact Hub Donostia
key_venues.what: Closest thing to a founder hub — events and meetups

key_venues.name: BIC Gipuzkoa
key_venues.what: Public business incubator, Miramon Technology Park

key_venues.name: Tabakalera
key_venues.what: Cultural center hosting innovation/creative events

startup_community.recurring_events: Occasional meetups via Meetup.com (Donostia Developers, Python Donostia); no consistent AI/indie-hacker night

bonus.flight_from_vie:
No direct flights from EAS to VIE; several connecting flights via 1 stopover
. 
Flights take 3h30 up to 5h15 depending on stopover
. ~€200–€350 round-trip via Madrid/Barcelona on Iberia or Vueling. Alternative: fly VIE→BIO direct on Austrian/Vueling + PESA bus.
bonus.classical_music: Quincena Musical de San Sebastián (August festival, one of Spain's oldest classical series); year-round programming at Kursaal and Victoria Eugenia Theatre
bonus.dance_scene: Modest bachata/salsa scene — Dantzaleku and Salsa Donosti run weekly socials; Bilbao (1h by bus) has larger Latin nights

concerns: No direct VIE→EAS flights; minimum 1 stopover or fly into Bilbao + 1h15 bus
Expensive city for 4 nights — boutique hotels €150–200/night eat most of the €900 budget, leaving ~€200 for food + coworking
Wet for ~7 months of the year; October–February gets dreary
Weak AI/indie-hacker community — Riza will be on his own for n8n/Claude conversations
Spanish/Euskera dominant; B1 German useless here, English OK in tourism but limited at gyms/local services
Parte Vieja gets very loud at night during summer — pick Centro or Gros for sleep

summary: Šibenik is a quieter alternative to Split/Dubrovnik with strong coworking infrastructure (Trokut), good internet (46.5–70 Mbps), and lower costs. As an EU citizen with Slovak residence, Riza can stay visa-free under Schengen rules (90/180). The 7-day budget is tight but achievable at €1,400–1,800, leaving buffer for client video calls and weekend sailing.
remote_work_pitch: Šibenik offers Trokut—a purpose-built coworking hub with 12 video/phone booths, FabLab, 24/7 access, and moderate pricing—perfect for Amazon calls. Unlike crowded Split, it's a medieval harbor town with fewer tourists, excellent Adriatic sailing from Marina Mandalina (National Geographic's "world #1 sailing destination"), and Krka National Park day-trips. The timezone spread (Vienna +0, London -1, Tokyo +9, Dubai +2) is manageable for EU/UK/APAC clients.
best_months: May–September (warm, 25–28°C, 8–10 sea hours/day). April–October for sailing. Avoid July–August (peak tourists). Spring (Apr–May) and autumn (Sep–Oct) offer best balance of weather, fewer crowds, and sailing conditions.

internet.notes: Croatia-wide average; Trokut coworking has fiber/LAN. Mobile via T-Mobile recommended. Reliable for video calls; cafes less consistent.

work_spots.name: Trokut Center for Technology & Entrepreneurship
work_spots.notes: 12 dedicated video/phone booths. 2084m² space. FabLab, 3 kitchens, free parking. Ideal for Amazon calls. EU-funded incubator; vibrant startup vibe.

work_spots.name: Banj Beach Waterfront Cafes (cluster)
work_spots.notes: Sea views, outdoor seating. Good WiFi in summer. Less suitable for video calls (ambient noise). Swimming 50m away.

work_spots.name: D-Resort Šibenik – Lobby & Business Center
work_spots.notes: 100+ Mbps WiFi. Quiet, air-conditioned. Day-use may require accommodation booking or guest privileges.

work_spots.name: Medieval Old Town Cafes (general)
work_spots.notes: Narrow alleys, local vibe. WiFi variable. Crowded in peak season. Suitable for reading/admin, not reliable calls.

stays.name: D-Resort Šibenik
stays.why: 4-star boutique, 100+ Mbps WiFi, spa/pools, marina-adjacent. Balcony sea views. Within budget. Fits Riza's car-free profile (walkable to marina, shuttle to Old Town available).

stays.name: Armerun Heritage Hotel & Residences
stays.why: 4-star heritage, kitchenette (some rooms), close to Cathedral. 100m from Banj Beach (swimming). ~€245 under budget. Free WiFi. Garden terrace for morning tea routine.

stays.name: Rooms & Studio Apartments Šibenik (Old Town)
stays.why: Studio with kitchenette, UNESCO Cathedral steps away. Smallest footprint. 7 days = €490. Leaves €1,300+ for coworking/food. Budget choice but central. Car-free (Old Town pedestrian).

visa.details: Turkish citizen with Slovak Pobyt (residence permit) = EU residency status. Under Schengen agreement (Croatia joined 2023), visa-free entry/stay up to 90 days in any 180-day period. No digital nomad visa needed. Note: Long stays (30+ days) may trigger Croatian tax residency; consult accountant if exceeding 183 days/year.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: AQI ~30–50 (Good–Moderate) year-round. Coastal breezes mitigate summer stagnation. Worst months: July–August (tourism, heat). Best: Apr–May, Sep–Oct.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Petty theft in tourist areas (Old Town, waterfront). Avoid isolated alleys at night. Solo travelers safe by day. No violent crime reported. Scams: overpriced café/restaurant bills; ask prices upfront. Well-lit marina area (Mandalina) is safe 24/7.

weather.conditions: Mediterranean. Sunny, occasional rain (Nov–Mar). Mild winters.

options.name: Banj Beach (city center)
options.quality: Clean, protected, sandy/pebble. Calm bay.

options.name: Solaris Beach Resort (south)
options.quality: Resort pool+beach combo, more facilities

options.name: Bazeni Crnica (public pool)
options.quality: Indoor/outdoor pools, well-maintained, local scene
options.transit_from_center: Walk/short bus ride from Old Town

options.name: Krka National Park (day trip)
options.quality: Turquoise pools, waterfalls, pristine
options.transit_from_center: Bus/car to Skradin (~9km, 20 min); then boat or walk down

options.name: Gyms4you Šibenik (Dalmare Center)
options.notes: Modern, Croatian chain (51 locations). Open 24/7. Weights + cardio match Riza's lifting/cardio preference. ~€45/month or pay-as-you-go.

options.name: D-Resort Šibenik Fitness Center
options.notes: Included with hotel stay. If not staying, day-use not typically available. High-end equipment.

options.name: Amadria Park Hotel Jakov (6km south)
options.facilities: Beach club, gym, water sports, pool
options.notes: Day-use possible (€10–15). Good for weekend getaway combining gym + beach + sailing prep.

gym.running_options: Waterfront promenade (3km loop, Banj→Marina→Old Town). St. Michael's Fortress climb (steep, ~30 min uphill for cardio). Krka National Park trails (day trip).
gym.yoga_pilates: D-Resort Šibenik offers spa wellness classes (check if day-use includes yoga). Amosfera coworking (Split, ~50km) known for wellness events; too far for regular. Local studios likely exist but limited English; check Meetup/Lu.ma closer to travel dates.

day_trips.hiking: Krka National Park (20km, bus to Skradin): cascading waterfalls, forested trails, 2–4hr loops. Optional boat to Visovac Island Monastery. Highly rated. Kornati Islands (ferry 1hr): kayak/hiking on remote islands (guide recommended). Paklenica National Park (60km, car rental): rock climbing, canyons; requires car.

marinas.name: Marina Mandalina
marinas.transit_from_center: 5-min walk from Old Town. Superyacht facility, 440 moorings, award-winning (Yacht Harbour Assn London).

marinas.name: Marina Vodice (Mandalina satellite berths)
marinas.transit_from_center: Bus #4 (~25 min) or water taxi from Šibenik harbor.

marinas.name: ACI Marina Skradin (river berths)
marinas.transit_from_center: Boat/car to Skradin, then moorings. Scenic for sailing/motoring Krka.

sailing.best_season: May–October. Best winds & sea temps. May–June, Sep–Oct ideal (warm, fewer crowds than Jul–Aug). Winter (Nov–Mar) possible but cold, changeable winds.
sailing.notes: Šibenik called 'world #1 sailing destination' by National Geographic (Kornati Islands, sheltered bays, 3400+ marina berths). Mild 15-knot thermals, protected channels. Spring regattas; summer flotilla charters popular. Intro lessons €150–250; bareboat charter €200–400/day (Dufour 382). Perfect for Riza's sailing hobby & weekend escapes.

new_hobbies.hobby: stone carving / stonemasonry (related to historic architecture)
new_hobbies.venue: Medieval Old Town walking tour (self-taught exploration) or contact local artisans in Cathedral workshops
new_hobbies.notes: Šibenik's UNESCO Cathedral (St. James) & fortresses are masterpieces of stone craft (15th–16th century). Walking Old Town, observing restoration work, talking to artisans—natural learning curve for someone interested in craftsmanship. No formal 'school' but rich heritage.

new_hobbies.venue: Murter Island wind spots (Dalmatian Maestral winds) – 30km north via bus
new_hobbies.language: English widely spoken at water sports schools
new_hobbies.notes: Šibenik region blessed with consistent Maestral (NW) winds May–Sep. Murter Island is windsurfing hub (Dalmatian Coast). Local schools offer intro courses (€80–150/2–3hrs). Complements Riza's sailing interest; lighter, shorter sessions than full sailing courses.

new_hobbies.venue: Banj Beach or Old Town cafes; self-taught or occasional workshop
new_hobbies.notes: Šibenik's medieval alleys, sea views, and fortress silhouettes are iconic subjects. Buy sketchbook & watercolors (local art shops €25–40), paint from cafes overlooking sea. Meditative, low-cost, no formal instruction needed. Enriches 'work + leisure' blend.

new_hobbies.venue: Ethno House or local craft centers in nearby villages (e.g., Krapanj Island lace-makers)
new_hobbies.language: Croatian-primary, but artisans patient with tourists
new_hobbies.notes: Krapanj famous for hand-made lace. Half-day intro workshops available (€60–100). Car-free: take local boat taxi from marina. Unique to region; connects Riza to local cultural heritage beyond tourists.

new_hobbies.hobby: foraging / ethnobotany walks (wild herbs, coastal plants)
new_hobbies.venue: Krka National Park or guided foraging tours in Dalmatian hinterland
new_hobbies.neighborhood: Krka National Park or villages inland
new_hobbies.notes: Dalmatian coast rich in wild herbs (rosemary, sage, oregano, capers). Some parks & eco-lodges offer half-day foraging walks (€40–80). Pairs well with Riza's interest in tea culture (black tea → herbal tea exploration). Offline, seasonal, sensory-rich.

coworking_retreats.notes: No dedicated co-working retreats (like Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite) currently operating in or near Šibenik (as of May 2026). Trokut is a day-use / membership coworking space, not a residential retreat. Alternatives: Sail Croatia (catamaran coworking in Brač marina, ~50km south—more luxury/sailing-focused, higher cost). For group co-working vibe, Split & Zagreb have more options; Šibenik is quieter, better for solo remote work + sailing.

car_free.walkability: Old Town is fully pedestrian (car-free zone). Waterfront & Banj Beach walkable. Marina area ~ 10-min walk from center.
car_free.public_transport: Local buses (Liburnija): single trip ~€2, monthly pass ~€40. Lines #4 (Solaris/beach), #5 (north), key routes to Vodice, Krka junction. Infrequent (1–2/hr off-peak). Taxis ~€3–5 base (car-free backup). Water taxi from marina to Old Town available in summer.
car_free.airport_to_center: Split Airport (SPU) 57km, ~1hr by bus or €35 taxi. Zadar Airport (ZAD) 70km, ~1.5hr. No direct airport bus; arrange private transfer or catch long-distance bus from airport terminal.

startup_community.scene_summary: Šibenik has an emerging tech ecosystem centered on Trokut (EU-funded incubator with FabLab). Smaller than Split/Zagreb; strong on deep tech & maritime tourism startups. Limited AI/SaaS indie hacker scene compared to major hubs, but Trokut hosts occasional hackathons & pitch nights. Not a nomad hotspot like Split; more authentic local founder vibe.

key_venues.name: Trokut Center for Technology & Entrepreneurship
key_venues.what: FabLab, business incubator, regular tech talks & entrepreneur events

key_venues.name: Šibenik Old Town Cafes (informal meetups)
key_venues.what: Loose gatherings of freelancers & designers; no formal structure

key_venues.name: Dalmatian Startup Club (online presence, occasional Šibenik events)
key_venues.what: Regional network; check Lu.ma or Meetup closer to dates

startup_community.recurring_events: Trokut hosts monthly entrepreneur forums & hackathons (check their website). Summer tech talks at cafes. Digital Nomad Valley (Zadar, 50km away) has more structured events if day-trip viable.

bonus.flight_from_vie: Vienna (VIE) → Split (SPU): ~2 hours direct, Lufthansa/OME/Ryanair. ~€40–120 return. Split → Šibenik: 1hr bus (€10–15) or taxi (€35). Total VIE↔Šibenik ~3–4 hours door-to-door.
bonus.classical_music: St. Michael's Fortress hosts open-air concerts (May–Sep, outdoor amphitheater, international musicians). Cathedral (St. James) occasional organ recitals & sacred music. Šibenik Summer Festival (Jul–Aug) includes classical. Zadar further north (50km) has larger venues. Limited classical scene vs. Vienna, but magical outdoor performances in fortress.

dance_scene.notes: <cite index="14-13,14-14">Summer in Šibenik is festival season, with salsa festivals and open-air dance events from May to September</cite>. Local dance studios exist (salsa/bachata classes check Lu.ma/local listings closer to dates). Nightclubs along waterfront (clubs shift seasonally). Dalmatian folk dance performances at cultural events. Not a major salsa hub like Split, but enough summer events & tourist infrastructure to find partners & classes.

concerns: 7-day stay is short—limited time to settle, establish routine, hit peak productivity. Recommend extending to 10–14 days if schedule allows.
Coworking reliant on Trokut; limited café backup for video calls (WiFi/noise issues in casual settings). Summer season may increase prices & crowding.
Public transport infrequent (1–2 buses/hr off-peak). Walking + taxis/water taxi necessary; adds €50–80 to budget if frequent day-trips.
Limited startup/AI networking scene vs. Vienna/Berlin. Better for quiet execution than community immersion.
Internet: mobile coverage good, but coworking/accommodation WiFi critical for client calls. Test on arrival.
Summer (Jul–Aug) brings peak tourists; cafes & beaches crowded. Spring/autumn preferable for focus.
No digital nomad visa 'deal' (unlike many destinations); standard tourist rules apply—keep documentation if questioned on 'work'.

sources.what: Croatia digital nomad guide, internet speeds (46.5 Mbps fixed, 70.2 Mbps mobile), visa info, coworking overview

sources.what: Coworking spaces in Croatia, Trokut details

sources.what: Trokut coworking space details (phone booths, facilities, pricing)

sources.what: Digital nomad guide to Šibenik, coworking, atmosphere for remote workers

summary: Singapore is a compelling short-stay destination for Riza, offering world-class coworking infrastructure with phone booths, blazing internet (critical for client calls and AI work), and a thriving AI/startup ecosystem. A 7-day visit fits his visa window (30 days visa-free for Turkish citizens) and budget constraints, though accommodation costs and limited sailing/classical music venues are mild drawbacks compared to Vienna.
remote_work_pitch: Singapore hosts Asia's fastest internet, abundant coworking spaces with dedicated phone booths (JustCo, WeWork, The Work Project), and a booming AI/fintech startup scene—perfect for Claude API and n8n builders. The compact island and efficient MRT mean car-free living is effortless. SuperAI 2026 conference (June 10–11) and NEXT Hackathon align with Riza's indie-hacker interests.
best_months: February–April, September–October (cooler, lower rainfall, cheaper hotel rates)

internet.notes: Singapore ranks among world's fastest; fiber widely available. Call reliability excellent for Amazon client video calls.

work_spots.name: JustCo Marina Square
work_spots.notes: Designed lounges, meeting rooms, lively professional community. Monthly membership unlocks all 20+ island locations. Phone booths provide acoustic separation.

work_spots.name: The Work Project - Asia Square Tower
work_spots.notes: Five-star hospitality sensibility. Living botanical wall, refined materials, soundproof phone booths. Attracts serious builders.

work_spots.name: Workspace Grain + Cafe @ Guoco Midtown
work_spots.notes: Work-friendly cafe by Cedele chain. Free guest WiFi, limited power plugs but acceptable for midday work. Good coffee.

work_spots.name: WeWork 21 Collyer Quay
work_spots.notes: 20-floor flagship location, Marina Bay views, soundproofed booths, café on-site. Networking events and wellness center. Premium pricing.

stays.name: Hotel Boss
stays.why: Mid-range 3.5-star, gym + rooftop terrace + pool. Located in business district with MRT access. Stays well under €2000 budget for 7 days + coworking + meals.

stays.name: lyf Funan Singapore
stays.why: Modern hybrid hotel with gym, WiFi, microwave & fridge in common areas. Some units include kitchenettes. Near Telok Ayer MRT; walkable to restaurants & coworking. Kitchen option reduces food costs.

stays.name: YOTEL Singapore Orchard Road
stays.why: Design-focused 4-star, outdoor pool, free WiFi, city-centre location. Tech-forward vibe appeals to Riza. Close to Orchard MRT & shopping district.

visa.details: Turkish citizens enter Singapore visa-free for up to 30 days. Must complete SG Arrival Card online (via MyICA app or website) at least 3 days before arrival. No visa endorsement; digital e-Pass sent via email. Turkish passport must be valid for 6+ months. Proof of onward travel required. Riza's 7-day stay fits comfortably within 30-day window.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: PSI typically 30–60 (Good). Occasional spikes during haze season (June–July from regional fires). May 2026 typically clear.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Singapore is one of Asia's safest cities. Low violent crime, excellent police presence. Solo night travel via MRT/Grab safe. Petty theft rare in tourist areas. No scam hotspots in central districts (Marina Bay, Raffles Place, Orchard). Avoid illegal substances strictly; penalties severe.

ecommerce_events.name: SuperAI 2026
ecommerce_events.relevance: Adjacent—AI/machine learning focus; keynotes on frontier AI, robotics, AI in fintech/commerce. NEXT Hackathon (June 9–11) allows Riza to pitch AI product ideas. Fits his Claude API + n8n builder profile.

ecommerce_events.name: NEXT Hackathon at SuperAI
ecommerce_events.relevance: Adjacent—36-hour AI build sprint. Free admission, meals, drinks, AWS credits, post-hackathon acceleration. Perfect for indie-hacker scene networking.

weather.conditions: Tropical; hot, humid year-round. Afternoon thunderstorms (brief, heavy).

options.name: Sentosa Island Beaches (Palawan, Siloso, Tanjong)
options.quality: Clean, patrolled, good for leisure swimming. Warm water (26–28°C).
options.transit_from_center: MRT to HarbourFront, then cable car or monorail to Sentosa (~20 min total).

options.name: Hotel Boss Outdoor Pool / YOTEL Outdoor Pool
options.quality: Well-maintained, heated. Included with hotel stay.

options.name: ActiveSG Public Swimming Complex (various neighbourhoods)
options.quality: Government-run, clean, 25m Olympic pools. Very affordable.
options.transit_from_center: Multiple locations. Nearest: Clementi, Tanjong Rhu. ~15–20 min MRT + walk.

options.name: Hotel Boss Gym
options.notes: Included with stay. Quality fitness centre.

options.name: ActiveSG Clementi Swimming & Sports Complex
options.notes: Subsidised public gym chain. Extremely affordable. No frills but functional. Popular with locals.

options.name: Crunch Fitness Orchard
options.facilities: Weights, cardio, classes (yoga, Zumba), sauna, modern facility
options.notes: Premium gym chain. Walking distance from Orchard hotels. English classes, friendly multilingual staff.

gym.running_options: East Coast Park (10 km waterfront jogging path, flat, scenic); Gardens by the Bay (garden loop ~2 km, free). Both walkable or 1 MRT stop from CBD.
gym.yoga_pilates: YogaSix (Orchard location), Power Living (Marina Bay). Drop-in classes €20–25. English-taught, beginner-friendly.

day_trips.hiking: Bukit Timah Nature Reserve (164 m summit, 6 km loop trail, 1–2 hours, MRT + 15 min walk from Bukit Timah MRT). Pulau Ubin Island (ferry + mountain biking on dirt trails; car-free by design). Both easily reachable from city centre via public transport.

marinas.name: Singapore Youth Sailing Club
marinas.transit_from_center: MRT to Bedok, then 10 min walk or Grab to East Coast Park.

marinas.name: Changi Sailing Club
marinas.transit_from_center: MRT to Tampines, then bus 2 or Grab to Changi (~20 min).

marinas.name: Sentosa Marina at Marina at Sentosa Cove
marinas.transit_from_center: MRT to HarbourFront, then Sentosa monorail or cable car (~20 min).

schools.name: Singapore Youth Sailing Club - Sailing School

sailing.best_season: December–March (dry, steady winds, calmer seas). June–July monsoon season increases wind but adds rough conditions.
sailing.notes: Singapore's waters are warm (26–28°C) but confined (Strait of Johor north, Strait of Singapore south). More 'inshore dinghy sailing' and racing classes than long-distance cruising. Regattas common Nov–March. Scene is small but active; clubs are welcoming to visitors.

new_hobbies.venue: The WOOD Campus (Ayer Rajah Crescent, One-North district)
new_hobbies.notes: Shared makerspace with wood lathes, hand tools, CNC routers. Beginner classes in English. Riza's AI/tinkering mindset aligns well; woodworking pairs nicely with tech maker culture in Singapore.

new_hobbies.venue: Photo Walk Singapore (Meetup group)
new_hobbies.notes: Free weekly photography walks, beginner-friendly. Singapore's architecture, gardens, cultural diversity offer rich subjects. Good for post-gym/swim relaxation.

new_hobbies.venue: Kite Boarding School Asia (East Coast Park / Sentosa)
new_hobbies.notes: East Coast Park has ideal shallow, windy waters for beginners. Seasonal (Oct–April winds optimal). Pairs well with Riza's sailing interest; higher-adrenaline variant. Active expat scene.

new_hobbies.venue: Pottery & Art Cafe (Tiong Bahru)
new_hobbies.notes: Charming vintage shophouse district. Drop-in wheel-throwing or hand-building classes. Zen counterbalance to intense coworking; aligns with artisanal maker ethos.

new_hobbies.venue: Freedive Singapore
new_hobbies.notes: Intro SSI/AIDA Level 1 courses. Warm tropical waters, minimal cold-water overhead. Complements swimming passion; meditative, technique-driven like classical music appreciation.

coworking_retreats.notes: Singapore's coworking scene is city-based. No major curated co-living retreats (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, etc.) operate in Singapore as of May 2026. Maker communities (BLOCK71, LaunchPad) are day-coworking hubs, not residential. For Riza's 7-day trip, traditional hotels + day coworking is the practical model.

car_free.walkability: Downtown (Marina Bay, CBD, Raffles Place) highly walkable. Tiong Bahru, Chinatown, Little India excellent. Orchard Road is shopping district, mostly pedestrian-friendly.
car_free.public_transport: MRT (Mass Rapid Transit): 6 lines, 142 stations, ~7 min headways. Buses: comprehensive, cheap (S$0.90–2.20 per trip ≈ €0.63–1.50). Monthly travel card: ~S$128 (€91). Among world's best metro systems.
car_free.airport_to_center: SIN–CBD: MRT 30 min, cost €3. Direct train every 10–15 min. Alternatively, Grab/taxi ~€25. No car necessary.

startup_community.scene_summary: Singapore hosts a mature, well-funded startup ecosystem focused on fintech, AI, and logistics—but it's corporate-backed and visa-restricted for foreign solo builders. Founders are typically employed under Employment Pass (EP) or VC-backed locals. Indie-hacker vibe is quieter than Berlin or Lisbon; the scene skews professional/corporate. AI boom (2025–2026 national focus via Budget 2026 National AI Council) is attracting major corporates (Oracle, Johnson Controls, Micron). NEXT Hackathon + SuperAI provide short-term community access for Riza.

key_venues.name: JustCo (network, especially Raffles Place / One-North locations)
key_venues.what: Hub for early-stage founders and freelancers. Community events, monthly mixer events.

key_venues.name: Crane (Joo Chiat, Lavender)
key_venues.what: Indie-leaning community. Pottery workshops, creative panels, networking events. Most 'vibe-aligned' for builders.

key_venues.name: The Great Room (One-North / CBD)
key_venues.what: Enterprise-grade space with Monday breakfast club, networking events, premium community.

key_venues.name: SuperAI 2026 & NEXT Hackathon (Marina Bay Sands, June 10–11)
key_venues.what: Asia's largest AI conference + hackathon. Mentors, investor demos, prize competitions. One-time event perfect for Riza's trip alignment.

startup_community.recurring_events: JustCo hosts monthly founder mixers & talks. Crane runs weekly workshop series (pottery, creative panels, wellness). SuperAI/NEXT Hackathon is *once yearly* (mid-June). Meetup.com has sporadic AI/startup talks. Overall: structured but not daily grassroots energy.

bonus.flight_from_vie: VIE–SIN: ~12–14 hours (typically 1–2 stops via Middle East or Asia hub: Abu Dhabi, Doha, Bangkok). Direct flights rare. Cost: €500–1200 return depending on booking lead time. Major carriers: Lufthansa + partner, Singapore Airlines, Qatar, Emirates.
bonus.classical_music: Singapore has limited classical music venues compared to Vienna. Esplanade – Theatres on the Bay hosts occasional symphony performances and opera (Singapore Symphony Orchestra, resident ensemble). School of the Arts Singapore (SOTA) hosts student recitals. Annual Singapore International Festival of Arts (May/June) includes classical music. Victorian Theatre at Marina Barrage sometimes features chamber music. Overall: classical scene is modest; not a highlight destination for serious classical enthusiasts.
bonus.dance_scene: Bachata/Salsa: Active Latin dance community. Studios: Salsa Republic (Duxton), Tango & Salsa Studio (Clarke Quay), and several casual drop-in classes in Chinatown. Bachata slightly less established than salsa but growing. Friday/Saturday social dance nights at clubs and studios. Expat community is younger and vibrant; beginner-friendly.

concerns: Limited classical music infrastructure compared to Vienna—occasional performances but no world-class orchestras or concert halls.
Coworking day-pass prices are mid-range for Asia (€60–100/day) but cumulative cost for 7 days of drop-in work adds up if monthly membership not practical.
SuperAI 2026 conference (June 10–11) falls outside Riza's current 7-day research window; NEXT Hackathon not guaranteed entry (application-based). Would require separate trip if he wants to attend.
Sailing scene is inshore/racing-focused; limited long-distance cruising or exploratory sailing due to geographic constraints (busy Strait of Johor/Singapore).
Visa-free 30 days means extended stays trigger visa application complexity—suitable for 7-day trips but not long-term residency exploration.
