House by Phaos provides central coworking, and 'Work From Kalamata' partnerships are growing the scene. It's an hour ahead of Vienna and counts toward Schengen. Quiet and authentic rather than buzzy.

summary: France's prettiest Atlantic sailing town: a fortified medieval harbour, white stone arcades, the island of Île de Ré on a bridge, and the country's biggest yachting marina. Schengen (90/180). The single best French city for your sailing interest, with a real mid-size base and small but real tech presence.
remote_work_pitch: La Rochelle is France's sailing capital: a fortified medieval harbour-town of white-stone arcades, the Vieux-Port with its iconic twin towers, Europe's biggest yachting marina (Port des Minimes), and the connected Île de Ré bridge to bike-able vineyards and beaches. For a recon it tests an Atlantic mid-size France that's genuinely a sailing town; same time zone, direct/connecting flights, days count toward 90/180. The mild oceanic climate is the trade-off.

summary: The 'Florence of the South': a small baroque city in Salento with golden stone, low costs, slow living, and beaches on both the Adriatic and Ionian a short drive away. A few real coworking hubs and a small, growing nomad community. CET; counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Lecce is the 'slowmad' pick: a gorgeous, walkable baroque town with shockingly low costs, friendly locals, great food, and Salento's two coastlines within reach. It has a handful of genuine coworking spaces (Officine Cantelmo, Sellalab, Molo 12, Artem) and a small but real community, so you get peace and routine rather than hustle. Same-timezone with home; best as a calm, focused, sun-soaked base.
best_months: May to June and September to October; hot July-August, mild winters

internet.notes: Reliable in the city and coworking; verify rural Salento rentals

summary: Northern France's Flemish-flavored city: brick-and-stone Grand'Place, brasseries, a young student energy, and a strategic location 1h from Brussels/Paris/London by train. Schengen (90/180). Underrated and cheap, with grey weather and a smaller scene as trade-offs.
remote_work_pitch: Lille is the most underrated French big city: Flemish brick architecture around the Grand'Place, lively student-and-brasserie culture, the giant Eurostar/TGV hub putting Brussels (35min), Paris (1h), and London (1.5h) at hand, plus a growing tech/services scene. For a recon it tests northern France with continental reach; same time zone as Vienna, direct flights, and a real value-for-money base. The trade-off is grey, damp, cold weather much of the year.
best_months: May-September; cold damp winter, mild rest of year

internet.notes: Strong fibre/5G; call-reliable

summary: A big, lively Mediterranean resort and sailing hub: a large marina that's a gateway to the Datca peninsula and the Greek Dodecanese, pine-backed bays, beaches and party nightlife. Very touristy in summer, calmer and cheap off-season. Home country, Schengen-free. Does NOT count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Marmaris is a major yacht-charter gateway with a huge marina, pine-clad coves and easy hops to Datca and the Greek islands. As a citizen it's frictionless and Schengen-free, with cafes and a coworking option (eOfis) for work. It's a full-on package-resort town in midsummer (loud and crowded), so it works best in spring and autumn as a sailing-and-sea base rather than a quiet retreat.

summary: Argentina's wine capital at the foot of the Andes: Malbec vineyards, mountain views, sunshine, a relaxed mid-size city. Visa-free 90 days. Beautiful and easygoing with a small work scene — a wine-and-mountains base, best as a southern-summer/autumn escape.
remote_work_pitch: Mendoza is wine-and-Andes country: a sunny, tree-lined city surrounded by Malbec vineyards with Aconcagua (the Americas' highest peak) on the horizon. For a recon it tests a relaxed, scenic Argentine base; the work scene is modest, so pair good cafes with vineyard weekends and mountain trips, ideally in the warm Oct-April window.

summary: The gateway to Sardinia's Costa Smeralda: a practical, year-round Gallura town with coworking, an airport and ferries, set beside some of the Mediterranean's most beautiful coastline and the La Maddalena sailing archipelago. Slow-living island life in a Blue Zone region. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Olbia is the workable base for Sardinia's stunning northeast: a real town (airport, ferries, coworking, restaurants) rather than a resort, right beside the Costa Smeralda's turquoise coves and the La Maddalena archipelago, one of the Mediterranean's premier sailing grounds. Sardinia is a famed Blue Zone of slow, healthy living, costs are lower than the mainland glamour spots off-season, and it's same-timezone with home. Coworking is modest and the scene is seasonal, so spring and autumn suit best.
best_months: May to June and September to October; hot busy July-August, mild quiet winters

internet.notes: Reliable fiber in town; patchier in remote coastal spots

summary: The Azores' main town on São Miguel island: a lush, green, volcanic mid-Atlantic archipelago of crater lakes, hot springs, and whale-watching. Schengen (counts toward 90/180). Stunningly scenic and calm, but cooler, wetter and more weather-changeable than Madeira/Canaries — a nature base, not a sun-guaranteed one.
remote_work_pitch: Ponta Delgada is the gateway to the Azores' otherworldly nature: crater lakes (Sete Cidades, Furnas), thermal springs, tea plantations (Europe's only tea estate), and whale-watching, on a green volcanic island mid-Atlantic. For a recon it tests a calm, deeply scenic Portuguese-island base; it's cooler and weather-changeable (not a winter-sun bet like Madeira), so come for nature and tranquility rather than guaranteed warmth.
best_months: June-September (warmest, driest, whale season); mild but wet and changeable in winter

summary: The Dodecanese's main island: a UNESCO medieval walled town from the Knights of St John, a warm Mediterranean climate, beaches all around, only 30min by ferry from Marmaris/Turkey. Schengen (90/180). A characterful year-round-ish base with a relatively small work scene.
remote_work_pitch: Rhodes is the eastern Aegean's grand island: a UNESCO Old Town that's the largest inhabited medieval walled city in Europe (Knights Hospitaller history), beaches all around, surprisingly mild winters, and a unique 30-minute hydrofoil link to Marmaris on the Turkish coast (a real bonus for you). For a recon it tests an island with both Western-medieval and eastern-Aegean character; same Schengen rules apply, and the work scene is light but the year-round livability is real.
best_months: April-June and September-October (best); hot summer with breeze, mild winter

summary: Transylvania's UNESCO Saxon jewel: pastel piazzas with eyes (windows in roofs), German heritage, 800-year history, mountains 30min south, a polished small-town feel. Schengen (90/180). A beautiful smaller-scale Transylvanian base — calmer, more polished, very small work scene.
remote_work_pitch: Sibiu is Transylvania at its prettiest: pastel-coloured Saxon piazzas (Piaţa Mare, Piaţa Mică), iconic eye-windowed roofs, the Fagaras Mountains 30 minutes south, and a German-Saxon heritage that's genuinely useful for your B1 German. For a recon it's a calm small-town interlude — even more polished than Brasov but smaller and thinner on work scene; same Schengen rules apply.

summary: An ancient, pink-stone capital with a fast-rising tech scene, excellent tap water, very low costs, and a rich classical-music and monastery culture. Safe and welcoming, but landlocked with closed regional borders that complicate travel. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+4.
remote_work_pitch: Yerevan is an affordable, safe, culturally deep base that doesn't touch your Schengen clock: a strong and growing IT scene (PicsArt and ServiceTitan have roots here, with venture cafes and hubs like Impact Hub), famously good tap water, low costs, and Khachaturian's classical legacy. The trade-offs: it's landlocked, the borders with Turkey and Azerbaijan are closed, so you'd fly in via a hub, and as a Turkish citizen you should check current entry rules (a visa/e-visa is generally required and relations are sensitive, though some direct Istanbul-Yerevan flights now operate).
best_months: May to June and September to October (mild); hot summers, cold winters

internet.notes: Average fixed broadband ~59 Mbps; fine for calls and uploads

Armenia allows long visa-free stays (up to ~180 days) for many other nationalities.

summary: New Zealand's biggest city: harbours, volcanoes, a strong tech scene, and great food, on a slender isthmus between two seas. Visitor Visa required for Turkish citizens (not NZeTA-eligible). The country's hub for work-and-network, but expensive, +10h from Vienna, and ~24h+ to fly.
remote_work_pitch: Auckland is New Zealand's commercial, tech, and creative heart: a pretty harbour city of volcanic cones, beaches on both coasts, a real tech ecosystem (Xero, Vend alumni), and a multicultural food scene. For a recon it tests urban NZ; the trade-offs are the same as Australia plus a touch more — Turkish citizens are not NZeTA-eligible and need a formal Visitor Visa, the time-zone is +10-12h from Vienna depending on daylight saving, and the flight is ~24h.

summary: Bali's calm, family-friendly southeast beach town: a gentle, swimmable sea, a long walkable-cyclable beach promenade, and a quieter, more local feel than Canggu. Good fiber and a small but real coworking scene (Genius Cafe, Livit Hub). Non-Schengen; Indonesia gives Turkish citizens visa-on-arrival.
remote_work_pitch: Sanur is the Bali base that actually fits a swimmer who wants calm: a sheltered reef-protected sea ideal for daily swims, a flat beachfront promenade you can walk or cycle (rare for Bali), sunrise over the water, and beach-cafe coworking like Genius Cafe. It is calmer and more grown-up than Canggu, with the airport just 20-30 minutes away. The trade-offs are a smaller scene and the +7h gap from Vienna.
best_months: April to October (dry season); wetter November to March

internet.notes: Fiber (Biznet/MyRepublic) is solid in apartments and coworking; back up with a Telkomsel SIM

summary: Ireland's relaxed, food-loving second city: a compact island-centre on the river Lee with a celebrated culinary scene (the English Market), a solid startup community (Republic of Work), and the rugged south-west coast nearby. Friendlier on the budget than Dublin. Non-Schengen, so it does NOT burn your 90/180; an Irish visa is required in advance. GMT (one hour behind Vienna).
remote_work_pitch: Cork is the easygoing, foodie alternative to Dublin: a walkable river city with a famous culinary culture (the English Market, a wave of great restaurants), a real coworking/startup scene (Republic of Work), and the wild West Cork coast, Kinsale and the harbour close by. It's lower-cost and slower-paced than Dublin, English-speaking, and outside Schengen so it doesn't touch your 90/180. Still pricey by global standards, rainy, and needs an Irish visa in advance.

summary: A breezy, artsy Atlantic port in Morocco: a walkable UNESCO medina, year-round trade winds that make it a windsurf/kitesurf mecca, a creative bohemian scene, surf-coliving spaces, and very low costs. A relaxed, wind-and-waves base. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); WET (one hour behind Vienna).
remote_work_pitch: Essaouira is the wind-sport-and-creativity pick: a laid-back, whitewashed Atlantic town with a UNESCO medina, a strong artist/surfer scene, and reliable trade winds that make it one of the world's great windsurf/kitesurf spots. Surf-coliving and coworking (Noqta Space) plus cheap living make it easy on the wallet, and it's an hour behind Vienna (good for US calls). As a Turkish citizen you enter visa-free for up to 90 days, and it's Schengen-free. Formal coworking is limited and it's small, so it suits a focused, active, breezy stint.

summary: Crete's working capital: a real Greek city with Minoan history (Knossos), a Venetian fortress harbour, less polish than Chania but cheaper and more local, mild Mediterranean climate. Schengen (90/180). A practical Crete base with deeper amenities than Chania, light on tourist sparkle.
remote_work_pitch: Heraklion is Crete's working capital: a real Greek city with the Minoan Knossos site on its edge, a Venetian fortress guarding the harbour, the country's best archaeological museum, and the central position making the rest of Crete (Chania west, beaches east) reachable. For a recon it tests a less-touristy Crete base with deeper everyday amenities; cheaper than Chania, with the same Greek Schengen rules.

summary: The most charming town in Turkish-Cypriot North Cyprus: a fortified Venetian harbour, mountain backdrop, beaches, very cheap. UNRECOGNIZED INTERNATIONALLY — entry for Turkish citizens is easiest of all (via Turkey, no visa), but it's a separate administration with serious caveats around recognition, banking, and EU compatibility. A unique cheap-Turkish-friendly base, but politically complex.
remote_work_pitch: Kyrenia (Girne) is the prettiest town in Turkish-Cypriot North Cyprus: a Venetian-walled harbour, Five-Finger Mountain (Beşparmak) above, beaches and Roman ruins (Salamis) nearby, very cheap living, and the same warm Med climate as the south. For YOU specifically as a Turkish citizen this is the easiest entry of any Cyprus city — just an ID card via Turkey. BUT it comes with serious caveats: the TRNC is recognized ONLY by Turkey, banking/EU links are limited, you cannot legally cross into the EU south using a north-entry stamp, and remote work infrastructure is thinner. Treat it as a niche, very-cheap Turkish-cultural base — not a substitute for the EU south.
best_months: April-June and September-November (best); hot dry summer, mild winter

internet.notes: Decent fibre/4G in town; weaker than the south; verify accommodation, SIM backup for calls

work_spots.name: Kyrenia cafes / small coworking
work_spots.notes: Atmospheric harbour cafes (Turkish-style), laptop-friendly; minimal formal coworking

stays.name: Kyrenia harbour / hillside apartment
stays.why: Cheap accommodation, walk to the Venetian harbour and castle; pools common in residential developments

visa.details: POLITICALLY COMPLEX: Northern Cyprus (TRNC) is recognized ONLY by Turkey. As a Turkish citizen you enter freely on ID card via Turkey (Ercan airport or sea); typical 90-day stays. NOT Schengen, NOT EU (despite the south being EU). KEY CAVEAT: entering Cyprus via the north then crossing to the EU south is considered illegal entry to the Republic of Cyprus — DO NOT mix routes in one trip. For EU-side work/banking/recognition, you must enter the south on a separate trip via Larnaca/Paphos.

air_water_safety.safety_notes: Very safe and relaxed town; normal awareness, watch for sea currents. Most use bottled water

weather.conditions: Mediterranean; hot dry summer, mild winter

options.name: Karaoğlanoğlu, Alagadi (turtle beach), Acapulco
options.quality: Long sandy beaches west and east of town; warm Med, swimmable May-Nov

options.name: Kyrenia gyms / hotel gyms
options.notes: Basic but cheap

gym.running_options: Coastal road, harbour to castle, Beşparmak trails

day_trips.hiking: Beşparmak (Five-Finger) Mountains, St Hilarion Castle, Bellapais Abbey, Karpaz Peninsula (wild turtles and donkeys), Salamis (ancient ruins near Famagusta)

sailing.notes: Kyrenia's iconic Venetian harbour holds small craft; Karpaz Gate Marina (east) is a serious modern yacht marina — sailing is a real feature here, very cheap

new_hobbies.venue: Local Turkish-cultural everything, harbour boats, mountain hikes
new_hobbies.notes: Turkish is the everyday language — easy for you; cheap living for an island Med base; bucket-list scenery (Karpaz, Bellapais)

coworking_retreats.notes: Light; some retiree-British and Turkish lifestyle community

car_free.walkability: Old harbour walkable; the strip and beaches are spread out — a car or many taxis help
car_free.public_transport: Dolmuş minibuses + taxis; car-leaning
car_free.airport_to_center: Ercan (ECN, North Cyprus) ~45min by taxi (~30 EUR); or via Larnaca + Ledra crossing on foot (only one-way, see visa note)

startup_community.scene_summary: None to speak of; tourism, retirees, education (some universities), and Turkish business. Come for cheap Turkish-cultural Mediterranean living, not networking or EU work.

bonus.flight_from_vie: 1-stop via Istanbul to Ercan (ECN), ~5-7h total; Ercan is mainly served from Turkey
bonus.classical_music: Limited; Bellapais Music Festival in summer
bonus.dance_scene: Local Turkish-Cypriot nightlife

concerns: TRNC unrecognized by EU/international community (banking, recognition, EU compatibility all limited)
DO NOT mix north and south entry in one trip (illegal entry risk to Republic of Cyprus)

summary: Arguably Greece's prettiest mainland town: a romantic Venetian-and-Ottoman old town beneath the Palamidi fortress, a seafront promenade, beaches, and Argolis wineries, an easy two hours from Athens. Small and charming rather than a coworking hub. EET (one hour ahead of Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Nafplio is the scenic, romantic Peloponnese base: an elegant old town of neoclassical mansions and Venetian forts on the sea, with beaches (Arvanitia, Karathona), a lovely seaside hiking path up to the Palamidi fortress, and the Agiorgitiko wineries of Nemea/Argolis nearby. English is easy to find and Athens is ~2 hours away. There's little formal coworking (work from cafes and your rental), it's small, and it's an hour ahead of Vienna and counts toward Schengen, so it suits a beautiful, focused stint.

summary: A pretty, low-key town in the eastern Algarve: a Roman bridge and tiled churches, the Ria Formosa lagoon with island beaches reached by boat, an easygoing expat community, and a much calmer feel than the central Algarve resorts. WET (one hour behind Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Tavira is the gentle, authentic Algarve: a charming riverside town with island beaches across the Ria Formosa lagoon, warm calm water, great value, year-round sunshine, and a relaxed expat scene, without the package-resort crowds of Albufeira. It has a handful of coworking spaces and an hour-behind-Vienna timezone. It's small and slow, so it suits a calm, sunny base more than a buzzing one; Faro airport is just 35 minutes away.

summary: Corsica's capital and Napoleon's birthplace: a sunny Mediterranean port city on a stunning bay, French administration with Corsican character, mountains-and-sea on the doorstep. Schengen (90/180). A small French-island base — gorgeous nature, thin work scene.
remote_work_pitch: Ajaccio is Corsica's mild, sunny capital: a Mediterranean port wrapped around an enormous bay, Napoleon's birthplace, the Calanche di Piana cliffs and granite-spired GR20 trail in the back-country, and a French-administrative-meets-Corsican-island feel. For a recon it's a beautiful small French-island base; the work scene is light, prices summer-Mediterranean, and a car or scooter strongly recommended for the spectacular island. France Schengen rules apply.

summary: Sardinia's Catalan-speaking northwest town: a walled medieval harbour, beaches and turquoise coves, a unique Catalan-Italian heritage. Schengen (90/180). A charming small base with thin work scene — best paired with a car for the spectacular north-Sardinia coast.
remote_work_pitch: Alghero is the prettiest small Sardinian base: a Catalan-walled medieval harbour town on the northwest coast (Catalan is still spoken alongside Italian), turquoise coves (Spiaggia delle Bombarde, Cala Dragunara), and the dramatic Capo Caccia cliffs nearby. For a recon it tests Sardinia's charming north; the work scene is light and a car helps for the surroundings — but it's beautiful and English/Catalan/Italian are all in play.

summary: The canal-laced "Venice of Portugal," a flat, walkable, budget-friendly university city 45 minutes south of Porto, with a small but growing nomad scene and the striped beach houses of Costa Nova nearby. Quiet and cheap rather than buzzy. Schengen, so days count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Aveiro is a low-cost, walkable, bike-friendly base with picturesque canals, fast cheap fiber and a relaxed pace, an easy train ride from Porto's bigger scene and airport. It suits a quiet, affordable focus stint with the Costa Nova beaches and Ria de Aveiro lagoon close by. The trade-off is a small community and limited nightlife, classical or dance.

summary: A laid-back northern Aegean olive-oil town of cobbled lanes and old Greek stone houses, facing the island of Cunda and a short ferry from Lesbos. Slow, authentic and affordable home turf, with little formal coworking. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+3.
remote_work_pitch: Ayvalik is the slow, characterful Aegean stint: a historic olive-oil town of narrow stone streets, with the charming island of Cunda linked by bridge, clear swimming coves around the Ayvalik islands, and superb seafood. As home turf it's frictionless and Schengen-free. There's essentially no coworking (you'll work from cafes and your rental), and it's quiet off-season, so it suits a focused, affordable, sea-and-olive-grove stretch rather than a networking base.

summary: Bali's inland cultural and wellness capital: rice terraces, yoga, temples, and a huge nomad community anchored by Outpost and the legacy Hubud. Greener and calmer than Canggu but inland (no beach), traffic-clogged, and not walkable. Non-Schengen; Indonesia gives Turkish citizens visa-on-arrival.
remote_work_pitch: Ubud is the wellness-and-creativity heart of Bali, with a large, easy-to-plug-into nomad community, excellent coworking (Outpost coliving, Hubud, Ubud.Space), yoga everywhere, and jungle-and-rice-field calm. It suits a productive, healthy, social stint. The honest downsides: no beach, traffic and no walkability (you will need a scooter), rising prices, and a +7h gap from Vienna.
best_months: April to October (dry season); wet and humid November to March

internet.notes: Cheap housing can be 12-30 Mbps, but coworking and good villas run 100-300 Mbps fiber (Biznet/MyRepublic); occasional outages, so a backup SIM helps

work_spots.name: Outpost Ubud Coworking
work_spots.notes: Quiet 'library' floor + social/call floor, pools, video-call rooms; ~180 EUR/month, works across all Bali Outpost locations

work_spots.name: Hubud / Ubud.Space
work_spots.notes: Hubud is Bali's original nomad coworking; Ubud.Space and Beluna are good alternatives

stays.name: Monthly villa (Penestanan/Nyuh Kuning)

visa.details: Turkish citizens get Indonesia visa-on-arrival (e-VOA, 30 days, extendable once to 60). Longer stays use a B211A visit visa or the new (income-gated) nomad visa. A Bali tourism levy (~IDR 150,000) is payable. Non-Schengen: does not count toward your 90/180.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Generally clean inland, some traffic fumes and dry-season crop smoke
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Low violent crime; main risks are scooter accidents (very common), monkey theft at temples, and ATM skimming. Strictly bottled water

summary: The lively Adriatic capital of Puglia: an atmospheric old town, the largest expat community in the region, affordable living, high-speed internet, and a string of gorgeous coastal towns (Polignano a Mare, Monopoli) on the doorstep. A practical, authentic southern base. CET; counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Bari is the most practical Puglia base: a real working port city with the region's biggest expat/nomad community, coworking, affordable rents, good fiber, and a seafront old town. The Salento beaches and cliff towns like Polignano a Mare are a short hop, Adriatic ferries reach Greece and the Balkans, and you're same-timezone with home. Authentic and underrated rather than postcard-pretty.
best_months: May to June and September to October; hot July-August, mild winters

internet.notes: High-speed fiber widely available in the city

summary: The Philippines' second city and island gateway: cheaper and more manageable than Manila, English everywhere, beaches and diving (Moalboal, Malapascua) a few hours away. Visa-free 30 days. A practical island-region base.
remote_work_pitch: Cebu balances a real city (coworking, malls, an international airport, BPO economy) with quick access to world-class diving and islands. For a recon it tests the Philippines more pleasantly than Manila: English-first, cheaper, with the sea genuinely reachable on weekends. Internet and traffic are the usual caveats.
best_months: December-May (dry); avoid typhoon/wet season Jun-Nov

internet.notes: Decent fiber in Cebu City/IT Park; verify, SIM backup for calls

summary: Sri Lanka's seaside capital: the island's most developed city, decent internet, a growing cafe scene, ocean-front. Needs a Sri Lanka ETA (online). A practical base for the island, though Galle/Ella are prettier — Colombo is convenience over charm.
remote_work_pitch: Colombo is Sri Lanka's functional gateway: the best infrastructure on the island, oceanfront promenades, a rising restaurant-and-cafe scene, and low costs. For a recon it tests urban Sri Lanka as a base to explore the south coast and hills; it's more practical than beautiful, so many use it as a hub.
best_months: December-March (dry on the west/south coast); avoid the May-September southwest monsoon

internet.notes: Decent fiber in the city; verify, SIM backup for calls

summary: A graceful colonial city in Ecuador's southern Andes: cobbled UNESCO center, rivers, a mild spring-like climate, and one of the world's biggest North American expat-retiree communities. Visa-free 90 days, USD currency. Calm, cheap, and very livable, with a small work scene.
remote_work_pitch: Cuenca is Ecuador's most livable city: a beautiful UNESCO colonial center of domes and cobbles along four rivers, a mild Andean climate, very low costs, and a large, settled North American expat community (so amenities and English are easy). For a recon it tests a calm, affordable, walkable Andean base; the altitude (2,560m) and small work scene are the trade-offs.
best_months: June-September (driest); spring-like year-round, rainier Oct-May

internet.notes: Adequate fiber in the center; reliable enough for calls, SIM backup

summary: A laid-back Red Sea diving town on the Sinai peninsula: world-class scuba (the Blue Hole), warm clear water year-round, a growing nomad-and-coliving scene, desert-and-mountain backdrops, and rock-bottom costs. A diving-and-wellness base. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+2.
remote_work_pitch: Dahab is the warm, cheap, diving-centric escape: a mellow Red Sea town with some of the world's best scuba (the famous Blue Hole), bath-warm clear water year-round, windsurf/kitesurf lagoons, a Bedouin-and-backpacker soul that's grown into a real nomad-and-coliving scene, and astonishingly low costs. It's an hour ahead of Vienna. As a Turkish citizen, Egypt grants visa-free entry to the Sinai resort zone (incl. Dahab) for short stays; for travel beyond Sinai you'd need the standard visa-on-arrival/e-visa. It's remote (long transfer) with patchy infrastructure, so it suits a focused, active, sea-and-desert stretch.
best_months: Year-round warm; especially March to May and September to November (hot midsummer)

internet.notes: Coworkings/colivings have the best wifi; general connections vary, so a mobile-data backup is wise; VPN useful

visa.details: Non-Schengen, so does NOT count toward your 90/180. Verify current rules before travel.

summary: A serene peninsula town where the Aegean meets the Mediterranean: almond and olive groves, clear quiet coves, gulet sailing, and a deliberately slow, low-rise pace protected from mass tourism. Tiny and tranquil, with no real coworking. Home country, Schengen-free. Does NOT count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Datca is the antidote to resort Turkey: a long unspoiled peninsula of clear coves, almond groves and quiet sailing, with a calm, local feel. For a citizen it's frictionless and Schengen-free, ideal for deep-focus mornings and afternoon swims or sails. There's essentially no coworking and it's remote (a long transfer, a car helps), so it's a peaceful sailing-and-writing retreat rather than a networking base.
best_months: May to June and September to October; hot July-August, quiet winters

summary: The Algarve's capital and air gateway: a walled old town beside the Ria Formosa lagoon and its barrier-island beaches, calmer and more local than Lagos, with the region's airport and train hub. Practical rather than glamorous. Schengen, so days count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Faro is the functional Algarve base: the airport and rail hub, a quiet historic centre, the Ria Formosa lagoon and boat-access island beaches, and affordable coworking (Alandra Square). It is less of a scene than Lagos but easier for logistics and cheaper, with the same friendly -1h Vienna offset. Good for a calm, well-connected stint.

summary: Ireland's bohemian cultural heart on the wild Atlantic west coast: a compact, festival-loving, music-filled small city, gateway to Connemara and the Aran Islands, with a friendly nomad-and-student scene. Charming but small, rainy and not cheap. Non-Schengen, so it does NOT burn your 90/180; an Irish visa is required in advance. GMT (one hour behind Vienna).
remote_work_pitch: Galway is the artsy, soulful Irish base: a small, hugely walkable city famous for live music, festivals and craic, perched on the Atlantic with Connemara's mountains and the Aran Islands on the doorstep. It's friendly and creative with a student-and-nomad buzz, English makes it effortless, and it's outside Schengen so it doesn't touch your 90/180. It is small (limited nightlife/scene for some), rains a lot, isn't cheap (~EUR2,600/month), and needs an Irish visa in advance.
best_months: May to September (festivals, longest days); wet much of the year

internet.notes: Reliable fiber in the city; rural west slower

summary: Malta's calmer sister-island: rural, green-ish, fewer crowds, world-class diving, traditional Mediterranean village life. Schengen (90/180). A quieter alternative to Valletta/Sliema — beautiful, but very small with a thin work scene.
remote_work_pitch: Gozo is Malta's calmer, greener sibling: ferry-connected, rural, traditional limestone villages, world-class diving (Blue Hole, inland sea), and a slower pace. For a recon it's a calm Mediterranean village base with English on tap and the same EU nomad-permit framework as Malta; the trade-off is it's small, the work scene is thin, and you'll likely need a car or scooter.

summary: Vietnam's historic capital: French-colonial streets, lake districts, extraordinary food, very low costs, and surprisingly fast internet. A real, dense city with culture and even an opera house, popular with nomads in lakeside Tay Ho. Winter air pollution and chaotic traffic are the trade-offs. Non-Schengen, but Vietnam requires the 90-day e-visa.
remote_work_pitch: Hanoi is the rare Southeast Asia base that feels like a genuine city rather than a beach scene: fast fiber (well over 200 Mbps in coworking), serious coworking brands (Toong, CirCO, COGO), $1-2 meals, and real culture including the Hanoi Opera House. You would base in leafy lakeside Tay Ho among expats. The downsides are bad winter air, no sea, and a +6h gap from Vienna.
best_months: October to November and March to April (mild, drier); avoid the polluted, damp winter and the hot humid summer

internet.notes: Cheap, fast fiber across the city; coworking spaces run gigabit-class fiber, very call-reliable

visa.details: Turkish citizens need a visa for Vietnam but qualify for the 90-day e-visa (single or multiple entry, ~25-50 USD, 3-7 working days). No dedicated nomad visa yet. Non-Schengen: does not count toward your 90/180. Tax residency triggers at 183+ days in a calendar year, so a multi-week base is fine.

summary: A fast-growing Red Sea remote-work hub: year-round sunshine, warm clear water with world-class reefs, very low costs (~$700/month), fast fiber, and a surge of coworking-equipped condos and mid-term rentals. A sunny, affordable diving-and-watersports base. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+2.
remote_work_pitch: Hurghada is the value-and-sunshine Red Sea pick that's quietly become a real nomad hub: fast fiber (Egypt's fixed speeds now top 80 Mbps, the fastest in Africa), 365 days of sun, warm reef-fringed water, kitesurfing, and rock-bottom costs (a comfortable month runs around $700, with 30-90 night furnished rentals near Sheraton Road or the Marina at ~$350-430). It's an hour ahead of Vienna, so European mornings overlap and US clients come online in your afternoon. As a Turkish citizen you enter the South Sinai resort zone visa-free, but Hurghada is on the mainland Red Sea coast, so you'd use the standard Egyptian visa-on-arrival/e-visa (cheap and easy). Schengen-free. It's a resort sprawl rather than a charming town, so base centrally.
best_months: Year-round sun; spring and autumn ideal, hot midsummer, warm winters

internet.notes: Fiber rollout has made Hurghada one of the better-connected spots in Egypt; new condos advertise 1 Gbps and coworking lounges; keep mobile data as backup; VPN useful

Hurghada is on the mainland Red Sea coast (not the Sinai visa-free zone), so a standard Egyptian visa-on-arrival or e-visa is required (single-entry ~$25, easy to obtain). Egypt also offers a longer digital-nomad route. Verify current rules before travel.

summary: South Africa's economic engine: the continent's biggest business and startup hub, high-altitude mild climate, leafy northern suburbs, the best flight connections in Africa. Visa-free 90 days. The work-and-network capital, but car-dependent with real security caveats - less lifestyle-charming than Cape Town.
remote_work_pitch: Johannesburg is Africa's business capital: the deepest startup/VC/corporate ecosystem on the continent, a mild high-altitude climate, leafy affluent northern suburbs (Sandton/Rosebank/Maboneng), and the best air links in Africa. For a recon it tests work-and-network Africa; it's car-dependent and demands security awareness, so it's more a professional base than a lifestyle pick - and like Cape Town, it has zero time-zone offset from Vienna.
best_months: Year-round mild (high altitude); warm rainy summer Oct-Mar, dry sunny winter (cool nights)

internet.notes: Strong fibre in the northern suburbs; many places have backup power. Call-reliable

summary: Germany's Baltic sailing capital: a fjord-side university city that hosts Kieler Woche, the world's largest sailing event, and an Olympic sailing venue at Schilksee. Small, breezy and northern, with a modest nomad scene but a genuine sailing soul. Schengen, so days count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Kiel earns its place purely on the water: it's one of the world's great sailing cities, home to Kiel Week and an Olympic regatta course, set on a Baltic fjord with a sailing university culture. It's small and not a real nomad hub, with limited coworking, grey northern weather and Schengen-counting days, so it's a niche, summer, sailing-focused pick rather than an all-rounder, on your time zone and an easy train from Hamburg.

summary: The Balearics' calmest, greenest island: pristine coves, a UNESCO biosphere, Mahón's deep harbour, low-key village life. Schengen (90/180). The quietest and most authentic Balearic — beautiful, but very seasonal and thin on work scene.
remote_work_pitch: Menorca is the unhurried Balearic: a UNESCO biosphere of stone walls and country lanes, Mahón's enormous natural harbour, and turquoise calas largely without the Ibiza-Mallorca development. summary: East Africa's business and tech capital ('Silicon Savannah'): a high-altitude mild climate, a thriving startup scene, leafy suburbs, and a national park on the city edge. Visa-free for Turkish citizens (Kenya dropped visas; an electronic travel authorisation applies). The continent's #3 tech hub, with safari on the doorstep - but car-dependent with security caveats.
remote_work_pitch: Nairobi is 'Silicon Savannah': sub-Saharan Africa's leading tech/startup hub after Lagos and Joburg, with a mild high-altitude climate, leafy suburbs (Kilimani/Westlands/Karen), strong fibre, and a national park literally on the city edge. For a recon it tests East-African work-and-network; Kenya is now effectively visa-free for visitors (you apply for a quick electronic travel authorisation/eTA online). It's car-dependent with security awareness needed, but the tech scene and safari access are unique.

summary: Greece's lively third city and western gateway: a working port with ferries to Italy, a big student population, the country's first coworking space, a famous Carnival, and easy access to Peloponnese beaches and mountains. Authentic and affordable rather than postcard-pretty. EET (one hour ahead of Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Patras is the authentic, affordable Greek-city option: a real working port and university town (so it's lively year-round, not just in summer), home to POS4work, Greece's first coworking space, with seminars and a startup community. It has direct ferries to Italy, beaches and the Peloponnese mountains nearby, and a famous spring Carnival. It's an hour ahead of Vienna and counts toward Schengen. Less touristy and prettier-on-the-inside than the islands; great for Greek immersion and value.
best_months: May to June and September to October; hot summers, mild winters, Carnival in late winter

internet.notes: Reliable fiber in the city; confirm older-building rentals

summary: A working fishing town and one of Europe's great surf hubs (the Supertubos barrel, Baleal), with low costs, surf-focused colivings, and the Berlengas islands offshore for diving. A budget, focused base for surf lovers; quiet and cool in winter. WET (one hour behind Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Peniche is the surf-and-budget pick: world-class waves (Supertubos hosts a WSL championship event, Baleal is beginner-friendly), a real fishing-town feel without the Lisbon/Ericeira crowds and prices, and surf-coliving hubs (Coworksurf, SecretSurfHouse) plus the cosy coworking spot Largo. The Berlengas islands offer diving offshore. It's an hour behind Vienna (good for US calls); winters are cold, windy and quiet, with limited nightlife.
best_months: May to October (best all-round); good surf year-round, but cold dreary winters

internet.notes: Fast fiber at coworkings like Largo; verify individual rentals

summary: Nepal's serene lakeside town beneath the Annapurnas: a calm, beautiful trekking base on Phewa Lake, paragliding capital, cleaner and gentler than Kathmandu. Visa-on-arrival. Internet is improving but modest — a stunning nature-and-adventure interlude.
remote_work_pitch: Pokhara is Nepal at its most beautiful and relaxed: a lakeside town under the Annapurna peaks, with paragliding, lake kayaking, and the trailheads for Nepal's classic treks. For a recon it's far calmer and cleaner than Kathmandu, suiting an outdoorsy, async stay; the work infrastructure is light but the setting is extraordinary.

summary: Ecuador's high-Andes capital: a spectacular UNESCO colonial old town, spring-like climate, volcanoes all around, the US dollar as currency. Visa-free 90 days. Beautiful and cheap with a small work scene; high altitude (2,850m) and big-city caution are the caveats.
remote_work_pitch: Quito sits in a dramatic Andean valley with one of the best-preserved colonial old towns in the Americas, a spring-like climate year-round, volcanoes on the skyline, and the USD economy (no currency hassle). For a recon it tests high-Andes Ecuador cheaply; the altitude (2,850m) and normal big-city safety awareness are the main caveats, and the modern Mariscal/La Floresta districts are the nomad-friendly base.
best_months: June-September (driest); spring-like year-round, rainier Oct-May

summary: Costa Rica's central-valley capital: the country's practical base and transport hub, mild climate, decent infrastructure, but not especially scenic — most come for the nature beyond. Visa-free (30-90 days). A functional base for a pricey, eco-rich country.
remote_work_pitch: San José is Costa Rica's functional heart: a mild-climate valley city with the country's best infrastructure, coworkings, and connections to beaches and cloud forests. For a recon it tests Costa Rica's hub (most nomads use it as a base between Pacific-coast and jungle trips); the city itself is workmanlike, and Costa Rica is pricier than its neighbors.

summary: Israel's Mediterranean tech metropolis: one of the world's densest startup ecosystems ('Startup Nation'), great beaches, Bauhaus architecture, and legendary nightlife and food. Visa situation for Turkish citizens is currently sensitive - verify carefully. Very expensive, and regional security/instability is a serious live consideration.
remote_work_pitch: Tel Aviv is a Mediterranean-beach tech powerhouse: one of the densest startup/VC ecosystems on earth, with golden beaches, UNESCO Bauhaus 'White City' architecture, world-class food, and famous nightlife, at only +1h from Vienna. For a recon it's a pure work-and-network heavyweight. But it comes with two heavy caveats: it's very expensive, and the regional security situation is a serious, live consideration - plus the Turkey-Israel travel/visa picture is sensitive and must be verified against current advisories before any planning.
best_months: April-June and September-November (warm, pleasant); hot humid summer, mild winter

internet.notes: World-class fibre/5G; flawless for calls

work_spots.notes: Dense premium coworking and startup scene, booths

visa.details: SENSITIVE / VERIFY: Israel has generally allowed visa-free short stays (up to 90 days) for Turkish citizens, but Turkey-Israel relations and travel links have been severely strained, with disruptions to direct flights and entry. You MUST verify the current visa rules, flight availability, and government travel advisories before any planning. Non-Schengen, no effect on 90/180.

air_water_safety.safety_notes: SERIOUS CONSIDERATION: the regional security situation can be volatile (rocket alerts, conflict escalation). Check government travel advisories carefully before any travel. Tel Aviv itself is otherwise a safe, modern city with normal urban caution. Tap water drinkable

summary: Chile's bohemian port city: UNESCO hills of colorful houses, funiculars, street art, sea views, a creative-artistic soul. Visa-free 90 days. Atmospheric and characterful but gritty in parts with a thin work scene — a creative coastal counterpoint to Santiago.
remote_work_pitch: Valparaíso is Chile's artistic, anarchic port: steep hills of rainbow houses linked by century-old funiculars, world-famous street art, and Pacific views, with neighboring Viña del Mar's beaches. For a recon it tests a bohemian coastal Chile base, an easy 90 minutes from Santiago; it's atmospheric but gritty in parts, with a light work scene.
best_months: November-March (warm, dry summer); cool, can be foggy

internet.notes: Chile-grade fiber; verify on the hills, reliable for calls in main areas

summary: New Zealand's compact, hilly, creative capital: government, film/tech (Weta), cafes, harbour, and notorious wind. Visitor Visa required. Smaller and characterful, with NZ's best cafe scene; same brutal distance/time-zone friction.
remote_work_pitch: Wellington is NZ's small, walkable, creative capital: harbour at every turn, government and film (Weta Workshop) and tech driving a real creative-class economy, and arguably the country's best cafe culture. For a recon it tests boutique-city NZ; it's smaller and easier to navigate than Auckland but notoriously windy, and the Turkish-citizen visa/time-zone/flight friction is identical.
best_months: November-April (southern spring/summer); cool windy year-round, especially Aug-Oct

internet.notes: Strong UFB fibre; flawless for calls

summary: A spice-island archipelago off Tanzania: Stone Town's Swahili-Arab heritage, dreamy east-coast beaches (Paje for kitesurf), turquoise water, and a fast-growing beach-nomad scene. Visa on arrival/e-visa (~$50). A tropical-paradise base with island-grade internet; +2h from Vienna.
remote_work_pitch: Zanzibar blends history and paradise: UNESCO Stone Town's spice-trade alleys, and an east coast (Paje, Jambiani) of white sand, turquoise lagoons, and world-class kitesurf, with a rapidly growing beach-nomad community. For a recon it tests Indian-Ocean island living; Tanzania requires a simple visa on arrival or e-visa (~$50). Internet is island-grade (workable, not flawless), and at +2h from Vienna you'll do early EU calls, but the lifestyle is hard to beat.
best_months: June-October and December-February (dry, sunny); long rains Mar-May

internet.notes: Island-variable; Paje/Stone Town nomad spots have workable wifi + Starlink emerging. SIM backup essential for calls

summary: Jordan's hilly capital: a safe, welcoming Middle-Eastern city of ancient sites and modern cafe culture, with a small but real startup scene and a great base for Petra/Wadi Rum/Dead Sea. Visa-free / easy visa. Stable and culturally rich at only +1h from Vienna, though hilly and car-leaning with a modest nomad scene.
remote_work_pitch: Amman is the Middle East's most relaxed, welcoming capital: a hospitable hill-city layering Roman ruins with a thriving modern cafe-and-arts scene (Rainbow Street, Jabal Al Weibdeh), a small but real startup ecosystem, and unbeatable access to Petra, Wadi Rum, and the Dead Sea. For a recon it tests a stable, culturally deep Arab base; it's visa-easy for you and only +1h from Vienna. Trade-offs are the hilly, car-leaning layout and a modest (if growing) nomad scene.
best_months: March-May and September-November (mild, lovely); hot dry summer, cool winter (occasional snow)

summary: An alpine lake town in Argentine Patagonia: snow-capped peaks, glacial lakes, Swiss-style chocolate-box charm, skiing in winter and hiking in summer. Visa-free 90 days. Stunning and outdoorsy but remote with a thin work scene — a nature interlude (note: its 'summer' is Dec-Feb).
remote_work_pitch: Bariloche is Patagonia's lake-district gem: a Swiss-Argentine town on Lake Nahuel Huapi ringed by Andean peaks, with hiking and kayaking in summer and skiing (Cerro Catedral) in winter. For a recon it's a spectacular nature interlude; it's remote with a light work scene, so plan an outdoorsy, async stay — and note the seasons are flipped (warm Dec-Feb, ski season Jun-Aug).

summary: Morocco's biggest city and economic engine: a modern, business-minded Atlantic metropolis with the monumental Hassan II Mosque, real coworking (New Work Lab, The Spot), the country's best air links, and a French-and-Arabic working culture. Less touristy, more functional. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); WET (one hour behind Vienna).
remote_work_pitch: Casablanca is the practical, big-city Morocco base: the country's business and finance hub with the most coworking, the best international flight links, and a modern Corniche waterfront, alongside the spectacular Hassan II Mosque. It's more functional than charming (a working metropolis, not a tourist medina), French and Arabic dominate, and it's an hour behind Vienna. As a Turkish citizen you enter visa-free for 90 days, and it's Schengen-free. Good for connectivity and networking by Moroccan standards.
best_months: April to June and September to November; warm summers, mild winters

internet.notes: Fiber widely available; the best connectivity in Morocco alongside Rabat

remote_work_pitch: Dharamshala blends Himalayan grandeur with Tibetan Buddhist culture and a surprisingly real small nomad pocket (especially nearby Bir, a paragliding-and-coworking village). summary: Kenya's premier Indian Ocean beach: white sand, warm turquoise water, kitesurf, diving, and a growing beach-nomad scene. Visa-free (eTA). A genuine tropical-beach base with surprisingly decent internet and a relaxed vibe - remote-ish (reached via Nairobi/Mombasa), +1h from Vienna.
remote_work_pitch: Diani is one of Africa's best beaches: a long stretch of white sand and warm turquoise Indian Ocean south of Mombasa, with kitesurf, diving, monkeys in the palms, and a small but growing beach-nomad community. For a recon it tests Kenyan coastal living; internet is surprisingly workable, the vibe is relaxed, and at only +1h from Vienna the time zone is friendly. It's reached via Nairobi or Mombasa, so it's a bit remote.

summary: Turkey's youthful, modern university city: canals with gondolas on the Porsuk river, pedestrian-friendly streets, a lively student nightlife, and a reputation as one of the country's most liveable, progressive towns. Small and inland, but fun and cheap. Home country, Schengen-free. Does NOT count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Eskisehir is the surprise lifestyle pick: a clean, modern, walkable city built around two big universities, with riverside cafes, gondolas, and a young energetic vibe at very low cost. As a Turkish citizen it's frictionless and Schengen-free. Coworking is limited and it's firmly inland, so it suits a short, sociable, budget focus stint rather than a long professional base.

summary: Surreal cave-town living among the fairy chimneys of Cappadocia: cave hotels, hot-air balloons at dawn, rock-cut churches, and hiking straight out of town through rose-colored valleys. A bucket-list inland experience with no real coworking scene. Home country, Schengen-free. Does NOT count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Goreme is for the experience, not the infrastructure: wake to balloons over the valleys, take morning calls from a cave room, then hike Rose and Pigeon valleys in the afternoon. As a citizen it's frictionless and Schengen-free, and wifi in central Goreme/Urgup is generally fine for standard remote work. But there's essentially no coworking, coliving or nomad community, and it's landlocked, so treat it as a striking short stay rather than a long base.
best_months: April to June and September to October (mild, best ballooning); hot summers, cold snowy winters

summary: A lantern-lit UNESCO ancient town on Vietnam's central coast, near Da Nang's airport and beaches: calm, beautiful, cheap, and creative, with a maturing nomad scene around An Bang Beach. Slower and smaller than a city base, and prone to autumn flooding. Non-Schengen, but Vietnam requires the 90-day e-visa.
remote_work_pitch: Hoi An pairs a postcard ancient town with An Bang Beach 4km away and Da Nang's airport and bigger nomad scene 45 minutes up the road. It is one of the calmest, most affordable places in Asia to focus while still enjoying daily life, with coworking (The Hub, Hub Hoi An coliving) plus a strong cafe culture. The catches are a small scene, the +6h time gap, and serious flooding in October to November.
best_months: February to May (warm, dry); heavy rain and flooding October to November

internet.notes: Coworking and coliving run fast fiber; some cafe/villa wifi wobbles, and floods can disrupt service in autumn

remote_work_pitch: Lake Atitlán is one of the most beautiful lakes on earth, ringed by volcanoes and Maya villages, with San Marcos La Laguna a long-running wellness-and-nomad enclave (yoga, cacao, holistic everything). For a recon it tests off-grid-ish lake living; it's magical and cheap but remote and boat-connected with village internet, so plan a wellness-forward, async stay.

summary: The wild, windswept southwestern tip of Europe in the Algarve: dramatic cliffs, raw surf and big skies, surf-hostel coworking and an Outsite coliving, and the world-class Rota Vicentina coastal trail. Small, seasonal and tranquil. WET (one hour behind Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Sagres is end-of-the-world tranquility for surfers and nature lovers: clifftop sunsets at Cape St Vincent, consistent Atlantic swell, the spectacular Rota Vicentina/Fishermen's Trail for hiking, and a small but real remote-work scene (Outsite Sagres coworking, Cowork Surf coliving). It's tiny, raw and seasonal with limited services, and it's an hour behind Vienna. Best as a focused, off-grid-feeling surf-and-write stretch rather than a city base.
best_months: April to June and September to October; windy year-round, mild winters, quiet off-season

summary: Albufeira is a touristy Algarve beach resort with one solid coworking space, fast Portuguese fiber, and easy direct Ryanair access from Vienna — a workable 4-day workation if Riza wants sea, sun and a coastal reset. But it's a holiday town, not a tech hub: weak founder scene, sparse car-free transit to nearby beaches, and packed in July/August.
remote_work_pitch: Direct ~3h40m Ryanair from VIE, UTC+0 timezone aligns well with UK clients and is manageable for UAE/Japan/Australia calls, and Albufeira Coworking offers a real community space 5 min from the Old Town beach. The compact walkable Old Town means car-free works for a short stay, and the marina puts sailing day-charters within reach.
best_months: May, June, September, early October — warm sea, fewer crowds than peak July/August, and bus 56 Aerobus from FAO runs full frequency

work_spots.name: Albufeira Coworking
work_spots.notes:
100 sqm open space with 16 ergonomic seats plus a bookable meeting room for up to 4 people; community of expats, locals, and remote professionals
. 
Quiet street in the old town, 5 minutes walk from Fishermen Beach and 5 minutes from the main square
. 
Day passes start at €12, monthly from €150
. No dedicated phone booths — use meeting room for calls.

work_spots.name: HQ Albufeira (Buganvilia Plaza)
work_spots.notes:
Located in a renovated former nightclub in Buganvilia Plaza mall
. 
Business-grade Wi-Fi, cloud printing, meeting rooms, kitchens and breakout areas
. Corporate vibe, private phone-booth-style offices bookable by the hour — best for back-to-back client calls.

work_spots.name: Iguana Café
work_spots.notes:
Listed alongside IKEA Lounge in Loulé and Mar d'Estorias in Lagos as a remote-work cafe option in the Algarve
. Laptop-friendly, decent Wi-Fi, central. Not for confidential calls — take meetings back at coworking.

stays.name: Vila São Vicente Boutique Hotel
stays.why:
3-star Vila Sao Vicente Boutique Hotel is at a 500-metre distance from Praia do Tunel Beach
. Walking distance to Albufeira Coworking and Fishermen Beach; good shoulder-season nightly rates leave plenty of room for coworking + dining.

stays.why:
Two-minute stroll from Praia dos Pescadores
, 
rooftop pool with views, indoor and outdoor pools
. IHG brand reliability for Wi-Fi/desk; spa for post-work decompress. Within budget at €720 total leaving ~€180 for coworking and food.

stays.name: Vila Origens Boutique Hotel (Adults Only)
stays.why:
Small charming property in the heart of Albufeira, a few steps from the beach and the city center
. 
Around a 5-minute walk from Albufeira Old Town Square, with swimming pool and sun terrace
. Adults-only = quiet for calls. Tight against €900 cap — pairs better with day-pass coworking, not multi-day passes.

visa.details: Turkish citizen with Slovak Pobyt (EU residence permit) enters Portugal visa-free under Schengen rules. 90/180 day rule applies but trivial for a 4-day stay. Carry passport + Pobyt card.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Coastal Algarve AQI typically <30 year-round; occasional summer wildfire smoke drift from inland in August.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Very safe by day. 'The Strip' (Areias de São João) gets rowdy with British stag/hen crowds at night; pickpocketing on the Strip is the main scam risk. Old Town is calmer. Solo-walk-safe.

weather.conditions: Mild Mediterranean; mostly sunny year-round, wettest Nov–Feb

options.name: Praia dos Pescadores (Fishermen's Beach)
options.quality: Clean, central, sheltered cove

options.name: Praia da Falésia
options.quality: Long golden-cliff beach

options.name: Hotel pools (Indigo / Vila Origens)
options.quality: Heated/seasonal outdoor

options.name: Fitness Hut Albufeira
options.notes: Portuguese low-cost chain, modern equipment, English-friendly staff; ~10 min by bus from Old Town

options.name: Phive Health Club (Grande Real Santa Eulália)
options.notes: Hotel gym open to externals; pricier but full spa access

options.name: Bodyhit Albufeira
options.notes: 20-min EMS sessions if lifting time is tight

gym.running_options: Praia da Falésia cliff-top trail (~6km flat-packed sand path with sea views) is the standout run; Old Town promenade for short loops
gym.yoga_pilates:
Vila Origens runs weekly scheduled yoga sessions and yoga & sea programs year-round
; English-language classes common given the expat/tourist mix

day_trips.hiking: Seven Hanging Valleys Trail (Percurso dos Sete Vales Suspensos) — start at Praia da Marinha, reachable by EVA/Vamus bus to Carvoeiro/Lagoa then taxi to trailhead; one of Portugal's best coastal hikes

marinas.name: Marina de Albufeira
marinas.location: Várzea da Orada, ~2km west of Old Town
marinas.transit_from_center: Giro bus blue line or 25-min walk along coast

schools.name: Algarve Boat Courses (ABC)
schools.notes:
RYA recognised school teaching motor cruiser skippering, based at Marina de Lagos
 — ~1hr west by train, not Albufeira itself but the regional RYA option

sailing.notes:
Sailo deals start at €250/day; average daily Albufeira charter is €1,100
. 
Most tours depart from Marina de Albufeira in Várzea da Orada
. Mostly skippered motor/catamaran day cruises to Benagil caves; pure sailing scene smaller than Lagos/Vilamoura.

new_hobbies.venue: Algarve Watersports / Surf Experience Albufeira
new_hobbies.notes: West Algarve beaches near Albufeira offer mellow Atlantic beach breaks ideal for first-timers; Portugal's south coast is calmer than the famed Ericeira/Nazaré north

new_hobbies.venue: Hidden Algarve Freediving
new_hobbies.notes: Clear Algarve waters and sea caves make this coast a growing freediving destination; pairs with Riza's swimming background

new_hobbies.venue: Kitesurf Algarve (Alvor)
new_hobbies.notes: Alvor's flat lagoon is the Algarve's prime kite spot; consistent westerly summer winds

new_hobbies.venue: Algarve Photo Tours
new_hobbies.notes: Iconic sea caves and limestone formations make this one of Europe's most photographed coastlines

new_hobbies.venue: CIAL Centro de Línguas (Faro)
new_hobbies.notes: If Riza extends repeat visits to Portugal, basic Portuguese opens doors beyond expat bubble

options.name: Vila Origens Yoga & Sea Fun
options.notes:
Daily yoga and ocean adventures, perfect for all levels, joinable anytime year-round
. Wellness-focused, not pure cowork, but suits a builder wanting structured mornings + work afternoons.

coworking_retreats.notes:
Albufeira itself is light on dedicated co-working retreats; the bigger Algarve scene lives in Sagres (Coworksurf) and Lagos. 
Coworksurf in Sagres and A Good Place in Vale da Telha combine coliving with coworking
 — both ~1–1.5hrs west by car/bus if Riza extends the trip.

car_free.walkability: Old Town core is highly walkable; reaching outer beaches (Falésia, Salgados, Galé) and the marina requires the Giro local bus or taxi
car_free.public_transport: Giro urban buses (~€1.40/ride) connect Old Town, Strip, marina and Caliços terminal. No metro/tram. Day pass ~€4. Bolt/Uber widely available.
car_free.airport_to_center:
Bus 56 Aerobus connects Faro Airport to Albufeira; full frequency May–October, only 2 daily journeys each way in winter
. 
Around €10 per person, ~45 min
. Off-season: Bolt/Uber ~€35–45, or taxi 
€45–60 including A22 toll

startup_community.scene_summary:
Albufeira is a beach resort, not a tech town. The real Portuguese founder/AI scenes are in Lisbon and Porto; Algarve's nomad activity centers on Lagos and Sagres. 
Albufeira Coworking hosts community events ranging from creative to networking and tech-related
 — but it's small-scale.

key_venues.name: Albufeira Coworking
key_venues.what: Only real community hub; hosts occasional tech/networking meetups

key_venues.name: Nomad World Albufeira (annual)
key_venues.what: Annual gathering of digital nomads, remote professionals and entrepreneurs with networking and expert talks on remote work in the Algarve

startup_community.recurring_events: Albufeira Coworking community events (informal); broader Algarve Nomads meetups via Meetup/Lu.ma rotate between Lagos, Faro and Albufeira

bonus.flight_from_vie:
Ryanair direct, ~3h40m, currently 3 flights per week
; 
return fares from ~£57 (~€65) in low season
, ~€150–250 in summer
bonus.classical_music: Minimal in Albufeira itself; Faro's Teatro das Figuras (35 min by bus) hosts classical concerts and Algarve Orchestra season Oct–May
bonus.dance_scene: Tourist-resort club scene dominates (Strip nightclubs Kiss/Liberto's). Dedicated bachata/salsa is thin — closest reliable socials are in Faro/Portimão; check Meetup for 'Algarve Salsa' or 'Bachata Algarve' pop-up nights

concerns: Pure holiday-resort vibe — weak founder/AI community vs. Lisbon or Porto
Bachata/salsa scene is sparse in Albufeira proper; would need to bus to Faro/Portimão for socials
No dedicated phone booths at the main coworking — meeting room must be booked for sensitive client calls
Bus 56 Aerobus runs only twice daily Nov–April; off-season airport transfer essentially requires Bolt/taxi
Classical music scene is essentially nil in Albufeira itself
July/August: heavy British tourist crowds, Strip nightlife noise, hotel prices spike well above €900/4nt budget

summary: Turkey's capital: a planned, business-and-government city with embassies, universities, museums and a dry highland climate. Practical and very affordable rather than scenic, with no beach and a quieter social scene. Home-country base that never costs a Schengen day. Does NOT count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Ankara is the functional, low-cost capital base: reliable coworking (Work Up, Regus, Kolektif/Workinton branches), a serious professional and diplomatic environment, the national orchestra and opera for classical, and rock-bottom living costs. As a Turkish citizen it's frictionless and Schengen-free. It's inland, dry and short on lifestyle sparkle, so it suits work-focused stints over leisure ones.
best_months: May to June and September to October; hot dry summers, cold snowy winters

internet.notes: Solid fiber in Cankaya/Cukurambar; VPN advisable as elsewhere in Turkey

work_spots.name: Work Up
work_spots.notes: Modern space with cabins, booths and cafe; reasonable daily/monthly rates

work_spots.name: Kizilay / Cankaya cafes
work_spots.notes: Central cafes with wifi around the universities and ministries

stays.name: Monthly apartment (Cankaya / Kizilay)
stays.why: Upscale Cankaya near embassies or central Kizilay near the action; cheap

visa.details: Home country (Turkish citizen): full rights, no visa. Outside Schengen, so does NOT count toward your 90/180.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Winter heating can worsen air in the basin
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Very safe, orderly capital. Use filtered/bottled water

ecommerce_events.name: Government/defense/tech procurement scene, university research events

weather.conditions: Dry continental highland; hot summers, cold snowy winters

options.name: City and hotel pools; Lake Eymir nearby
options.quality: No sea; pools and the lakeside Eymir for summer

options.name: City gyms / fitness chains
options.notes: Affordable

gym.running_options: Lake Eymir loop, Seymenler and Kugulu parks

day_trips.hiking: Lake Eymir/Mogan, the Anatolian steppe, and Cappadocia (~3-4h) and the Hittite sites of Hattusa

sailing.notes: Landlocked highland city; no sailing

new_hobbies.hobby: Museum/history deep-dives or Anatolian folk culture
new_hobbies.venue: Museum of Anatolian Civilizations; CSO concert hall
new_hobbies.notes: One of the world's great archaeology museums sits here; pair with the national orchestra

coworking_retreats.notes: Business-coworking city

car_free.walkability: Spread out but with a metro and Ankaray light rail; central districts walkable
car_free.public_transport: Metro/bus, ~22 EUR/month; no car needed
car_free.airport_to_center: ESB ~45min by Havas bus/taxi (~25 EUR)

startup_community.scene_summary: Heavy on government, defense and university research rather than consumer startups; professional and well-connected but not a nomad/ecommerce hub.

key_venues.name: Work Up / Regus
key_venues.what: Business coworking

bonus.flight_from_vie: 1-stop to Ankara (ESB), ~4-6h
bonus.classical_music: The Presidential Symphony Orchestra (Turkey's oldest, new concert hall) and Ankara State Opera; surprisingly strong
bonus.dance_scene: Small salsa/bachata scene

concerns: Inland and dry; no sea or sailing
Cold snowy winters; basin air in winter
Functional rather than scenic; quieter social scene

summary: Europe's least-visited capital: Soviet-era boulevards, leafy parks, the world's largest underground wine cellars (Mileștii Mici), very cheap, friendly. NON-Schengen (90d visa-free, no count). A genuinely off-beat cheap base — gritty, quiet, light scene.
remote_work_pitch: Chisinau is one of Europe's least-visited capitals: Soviet boulevards lined with mature trees, the world's largest underground wine cellars an hour out (Mileștii Mici), an emerging IT-outsourcing scene, and remarkable value. For a recon it's a curious off-beat base — Romanian-speaking like Romania, but **non-Schengen — days do NOT count toward your 90/180**; visa-free 90d for Turkish citizens. Direct flights from Vienna; a real, calm capital that's overlooked.
best_months: May-June and September-October; hot summer, cold winter

internet.notes: Strong fibre/5G; Moldova has surprisingly fast internet

work_spots.name: iHub Chisinau / Tekwill / Generator Hub
work_spots.notes: Real Moldovan tech-hub presence (Tekwill anchor) + Generator Hub

work_spots.name: Centre cafes
work_spots.notes: Soviet-modern centre cafes; specialty coffee scene growing

stays.name: Centre apartment
stays.why: Walkable Soviet-era centre near parks and Stefan cel Mare boulevard; very cheap

visa.details: Moldova is NON-Schengen — Turkish citizens get 90d visa-free, does NOT count toward your 90/180. Note: Moldova-EU candidate status (since June 2022), accession in progress — visa-free for Turkish citizens is straightforward.

air_water_safety.safety_notes: Generally safe; normal awareness. AVOID Transnistria (frozen-conflict breakaway region — special permits, political risk). Tap water variable, most use bottled

weather.conditions: Continental; hot summer (~30C), cold winter (~-3C), distinct seasons

options.name: Municipal pools; Black Sea ~3.5h
options.quality: Indoor pools; Romania's Black Sea coast (Odessa, Ukraine, is currently NOT advised due to war)

options.name: Chisinau gyms (limited modern)
options.notes: Very cheap, basic

gym.running_options: Valea Morilor Park (lake + paths), Stefan cel Mare boulevard, Pushkin Park

day_trips.hiking: Orheiul Vechi (rock-cut monastery — extraordinary), Mileștii Mici wine cellars (Guinness world record), Cricova wine cellars, Saharna monastery, Romania border ~1.5h

sailing.notes: Inland; no sailing

new_hobbies.venue: Mileștii Mici/Cricova wine tours, language schools
new_hobbies.notes: World's largest underground wine cellars; Moldovan wine is genuinely excellent and unknown abroad; cheap immersion

coworking_retreats.notes: Small tech scene; very light DN community

car_free.walkability: Walkable centre; trolleybuses + buses + Bolt; flat city
car_free.public_transport: Trolleys + buses + Bolt; very cheap (~€0.20/ride)
car_free.airport_to_center: KIV ~15min by bus/Bolt (~8 EUR) — superb cheap link

startup_community.scene_summary: Surprisingly real IT-outsourcing scene (Endava, Tekwill, Pentalog), Russian/Romanian/English mix, growing English in tech. Punches above its weight for a country this small.

key_venues.what: Tech community anchors

bonus.classical_music: National Opera and Ballet, National Philharmonic — solid season at very low prices
bonus.dance_scene: Some salsa scene

concerns: Proximity to Ukraine war (geopolitical sensitivity, though Moldova itself remains safe)
Banking with non-EU country has some friction
