summary: Cambodia's gritty, fast-changing capital: very cheap, easy e-visa, a real expat/NGO scene and riverfront, but chaotic, hot, and rougher around the edges than its neighbors. A budget urban base.
remote_work_pitch: Phnom Penh offers rock-bottom costs, a long-standing expat community, French-colonial riverfront, and an easy e-visa, with a surprisingly real cafe scene. For a recon it tests budget urban Cambodia; the tradeoffs are heat, dust, traffic, and a rawer feel than Vietnam or Thailand.
best_months: November-February (cooler, dry); avoid hot season Mar-May

internet.notes: Adequate fiber in central districts; verify, SIM backup for calls

work_spots.name: SmallWorld / Factory Phnom Penh coworking
work_spots.notes: Expat-area coworkings; ask for quiet for calls

work_spots.name: BKK1 specialty cafes
work_spots.notes: Expat cafe district, laptop-friendly

stays.name: BKK1 serviced apartment
stays.why: Expat heart, cafes and walkability, near coworking

visa.details: Cambodia e-visa or visa-on-arrival for Turkish citizens (~$30, 30 days, extendable). Non-Schengen, no effect on 90/180.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Dust and traffic pollution; dry-season haze
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Bag/phone snatching from motorbikes is common — stay alert with valuables; otherwise manageable, solo evenings OK in BKK1

weather.conditions: Tropical, hot; cooler dry Nov-Feb

options.name: Condo/hotel pools
options.quality: Pools in serviced apartments

options.name: Phnom Penh gyms
options.notes: Decent gyms in expat areas

gym.running_options: Riverside, Olympic Stadium

day_trips.hiking: Limited near city; Mekong islands (Koh Dach), Udong hills

sailing.notes: Mekong river boats only; sea is ~4h at Kep/Kampot

new_hobbies.venue: Phnom Penh language/cooking schools
new_hobbies.notes: Cheap immersion in a less-touristed culture

coworking_retreats.notes: Independent coworking, no retreats

car_free.walkability: BKK1 walkable; citywide tuk-tuk/Grab (PassApp)
car_free.public_transport: Cheap tuk-tuks and Grab everywhere; no metro
car_free.airport_to_center: ~30-45min by Grab (~8 EUR)

startup_community.scene_summary: Small but real expat/NGO and emerging-startup scene, some coworking community, fintech growth. English workable in expat circles. Budget urban base, raw but improving.

key_venues.name: Factory Phnom Penh
key_venues.what: Startup/community campus

bonus.flight_from_vie: 1-2 stops (via Gulf hub or BKK), ~15-17h total
bonus.classical_music: Limited
bonus.dance_scene: Small expat Latin scene

sources.name: Seven Corners
sources.what: Cambodia e-visa ~$30 for visitors

sources.name: Nomad List
sources.what: Cost/internet baselines

summary: <cite index="10-8">Podgorica offers the best practical infrastructure in the country for digital nomads</cite>, with affordable accommodation, solid internet, and multiple coworking spaces. However, it's a functional work base rather than a lifestyle destination—expect efficient infrastructure and cheap living, not beauty or cultural scene. The visa situation is currently unstable: <cite index="19-1">Montenegro has restored the visa-free regime for Turkish citizens, shortening the residence permit from 90 to 30 days</cite>, though <cite index="25-1,25-7">citizens of Türkiye could soon also face new visa requirements as Montenegro aligns with EU rules</cite>.
remote_work_pitch: <cite index="6-15">The internet is reliable and fast, which is helpful for remote work</cite>, and <cite index="12-4">average internet speeds reach 116 Mbps</cite>. <cite index="5-21">MTEL Digitalna Fabrika provides all services for free</cite>, making it ideal for client calls and AI tinkering. <cite index="6-13,6-14">Podgorica is a small and very safe city with affordable cost of living and it's less crowded, with plenty of cafes, restaurants, and bars</cite>—perfect for focus work and building side products without distractions.
best_months: April–June, September–October (mild 15–20°C, low rain, ideal for swimming & hiking)

internet.notes: <cite index="9-11,9-12">Montenegro offers reliable internet speeds, with download speeds averaging 40 Mbps and upload speeds of 15 Mbps, good enough for most digital nomad needs, including video conferencing and streaming</cite>. Fiber widely available in city center; verify at accommodation. Phone booth coverage adequate for calls.

work_spots.name: NEST Coworking
work_spots.notes: <cite index="5-3">Each room has a workstation and a working chair, making it perfect for uninterrupted work and video chats, while the on-site coworking area accommodates 8 to 10 people and features lots of natural light and a high-speed fiber connection</cite>. <cite index="4-7">The premises are open 24 hours, with modern audio-video equipment and a professional team</cite>.

work_spots.name: MTEL Digitalna Fabrika
work_spots.notes: <cite index="5-21,5-22">MTEL Digitalna Fabrika provides all of these services for free, making it an appealing choice for local entrepreneurs and digital nomads visiting the city. With its great location, first-rate amenities, and free parking, MTEL Digitalna Fabrika is the ideal place to work, network, and build your business</cite>.

work_spots.name: Hub 385
work_spots.notes: <cite index="12-12,12-13">Hub 385: This coworking space in the city centre offers fast internet, comfortable workspaces, and a community of like-minded entrepreneurs and remote workers. Membership prices start at around €100 (around $110 USD) per month</cite>.

work_spots.name: Nova Café
work_spots.notes: <cite index="12-14,12-15">Nova: This modern cafe in the centre has excellent internet and a great selection of coffee and food. It's a popular spot for digital nomads and locals alike, and is open from 8am-10pm, 7 days a week</cite>.

stays.name: Hotel Hilton Podgorica Crna Gora
stays.why: Boutique 4-star with pool, gym, excellent WiFi. Nightly ~€95 on weekly discount (~€85/night = €595 for 7 days); quoted €665 including service. Safe for solo business travel; restaurant on-site.

stays.name: voco Podgorica (IHG)
stays.why: Mid-range 4-star, central location, pool, fast WiFi. Weekly rate ~€70/night fits budget comfortably. Suitable for remote workers; business center available.

stays.name: Apartment on Booking.com (City Centre, 1BR)
stays.why: <cite index="10-9">Cheapest rents (EUR 300 to 500 for a nice 1BR)</cite> monthly; weekly nightly ~€40 (30% discount). Full privacy, kitchen for tea/coffee prep, fiber internet, near transit.

visa.details: <cite index="19-1">Montenegro has restored the visa-free regime for Turkish citizens, shortening the residence permit from 90 to 30 days</cite>. As holder of Slovak Pobyt (EU residence permit), Riza has 90-day Schengen-equivalent access. For the 7-day stay, no visa required. **Note**: <cite index="25-2,25-3,25-7">Montenegro is aligning visa policy with EU rules; citizens of Türkiye could soon face new visa requirements</cite>. Short stay (7 days) is safe; any longer-term plans should verify current status.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Central Podgorica typically AQI 40–80 μg/m³ (Good). Winter (Nov–Feb) slightly higher due to heating. Summer clear. No major industrial pollution.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: <cite index="6-6,6-13">Podgorica is a small and very safe city with a laid-back atmosphere</cite>. <cite index="43-30,43-31">Podgorica is generally a safe city with low crime rates. Petty crime like pickpocketing can occur in crowded areas</cite>. Solo work/evening movement safe; avoid isolated riverbank areas after dark. No gang activity targeting tourists. Register arrival at accommodation (hotel auto-handled).

ecommerce_events.name: Game Changer Montenegro
ecommerce_events.relevance: Entrepreneurship, startup ecosystem; held in Tivat (1hr drive from Podgorica)

weather.conditions: <cite index="43-20,43-21,43-22">Spring (April-May): 15-20°C, pleasant warmth, decreasing rainfall. Summer (June-August): 25-30°C, frequently over 35°C, very dry. Autumn (September-October): 15-20°C, mild weather, increasing rainfall</cite>. <cite index="42-22,42-23">The city experiences hot, dry summers and mild, rainy winters. During the summer months, from June to August, temperatures can reach up to 95°F (35°C)</cite>.

options.name: Morača Sports Center (Otvoreni Olimpijski Bazen)
options.quality: <cite index="29-20,29-21">Morača City Pool. Equipped with modern amenities, it is ideal for families with children, athletes, and anyone seeking a classic swimming option in the heart of the city</cite>.

options.name: Morača River (Galeb Beach & Millennium Bridge area)
options.quality: <cite index="29-3,29-4,29-5,29-6">The Morača River flows through the very center of Podgorica and offers several popular swimming areas. Locals gather here on hot days, and the most well-known spots are near the Millennium Bridge, Galeb beach, and the area beneath the Blažo Jovanović Bridge. The current of the Morača is strong, the water is refreshingly cold, and the atmosphere is always lively. If you want to experience the city through the eyes of its residents, Morača is the place to be</cite>.

options.name: Skadar Lake (Virpazar beach, 20 km south)
options.quality: <cite index="29-13,29-14,29-15">Just twenty minutes by car from Podgorica lies Skadar Lake, the largest lake in the Balkans. In addition to being a nature reserve and a paradise for birdwatching, Skadar Lake has several organized beaches and pontoons for swimming. The most famous is the beach in Virpazar, where you can also rent a boat and combine swimming with an excursion through untouched nature</cite>.

options.name: Smart Gym
options.notes: Popular chain; friendly staff; English spoken. Good for weight lifting + cardio. Accessible from city center.

options.name: Hotel Hilton Gym & Wellness
options.facilities: Modern weights, cardio, sauna, jacuzzi, pool
options.notes: Premium facility; day pass open to public. Excellent for guest comfort.

options.name: Morača Sports Center (Fitness Wing)
options.notes: Affordable public option; less modern but functional. Paired with pool entry discount.

gym.running_options: <cite index="40-21,40-22">Podgorica is home to many riverfront parks that offer space for kids to play. Gorica Park is especially good for families to visit, with ropes courses and walking trails to explore</cite>. Morača Riverfront promenade (~3 km flat loop) is ideal for cardio. Mountain trails (Gorica Hills, 5 km from center) for hill repeats.
gym.yoga_pilates: Limited formal studios in Podgorica. <cite index="45-24">English is widely spoken in tourist areas, but learning a few basic phrases in Montenegrin can enhance your experience</cite>. Recommend Zoom classes or hotel gym stretching.

day_trips.hiking: <cite index="45-1,45-3">Popular day trip destinations from Podgorica include Lake Skadar National Park, Ostrog Monastery, and the coastal town of Budva</cite>. <cite index="38-6,38-23">Durmitor or Biogradska Gora National Park Trip. Discover the picturesque landscapes and fascinating flora and fauna of Montenegro's national parks</cite>. Hikers: 30 min to Lovćen foothills; 1.5 hrs to Durmitor (UNESCO); 20 min to Skadar Lake trails. Public transport: intercity buses €3–8. No car needed.

marinas.name: Marina Budva
marinas.transit_from_center: 1 hr bus (€5) + 15 min walk to marina

marinas.name: Skadar Lake Boat Rentals (Virpazar)
marinas.transit_from_center: 30 min bus (€3) to town; boat rentals at waterfront

sailing.notes: Podgorica is inland; sailing requires half-day trip to Adriatic coast or Skadar Lake. Skadar is freshwater, lagoon-style, good for beginners. Adriatic (Budva, Tivat) offers Dalmation sailing. Wind patterns: May–Oct stable NW winds; winter unpredictable. No local competitive regatta scene.

new_hobbies.venue: Startup Montenegro coworking (DIY space, informal)
new_hobbies.notes: No formal woodworking studio in Podgorica. Suggest exploring Startup Montenegro community for maker space or visiting Cetinje (35 km, 45 min bus) for traditional wood carving workshops.

new_hobbies.venue: Skadar Lake or Budva Coastal Schools
new_hobbies.notes: Day trip to Skadar Lake (beginners, shallow, calm) or Budva coast (PADI centers). Skadar ideal for Riza's swimming interest + exploring national park. Intro course typically €75–100.

new_hobbies.venue: Self-guided (Podgorica Old Town + riverfront) or Meetup-based
new_hobbies.notes: No formal school needed. Podgorica offers brutalist architecture, river light, and mountain views. Join <cite index="12-26,12-27,12-28">Podgorica Digital Nomads Facebook group (over 1,000 members) and Nomad Coffee monthly meetup (usually held at a different cafe or coworking space each month)</cite> for spontaneous photo walks.

new_hobbies.venue: Lovćen National Park (30 km) or Durmitor (120 km)
new_hobbies.notes: Lovćen & Durmitor offer thermal currents. Summer ideal. Intro tandem flight ~€120. Day trip feasible; book in advance. Beginner-friendly schools in coastal towns (30–90 min from Podgorica).

new_hobbies.venue: Local artisan studios (inquire via Startup Montenegro or tourism office)
new_hobbies.notes: No formal pottery school in Podgorica. Recommend contacting tourism office or Startup Montenegro for introduction to local ceramicists. Cetinje (35 km) has stronger artisan scene. Short stays may not justify multi-week commitment.

coworking_retreats.notes: No curated co-working retreats (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, Sun and Co, NomadX, Mokrin House, Work Wanderers) currently operating in or near Podgorica as of May 2026. Coastal cities (Budva, Kotor) may have seasonal options; not found in 5-query research window. NEST offers coliving (shared dorms/private rooms + coworking) but is residential co-living, not a retreat format.

car_free.walkability: <cite index="42-13,42-14">Podgorica is a relatively small city, so it's possible to reach many places on foot. This is a great way to explore the city and take in the sights at your own pace</cite>. City center compact; 15–20 min walk across downtown. Residential areas (Stari Grad, Zagorič) 10–25 min from center.
car_free.public_transport: <cite index="40-7,40-8">Public transport in Podgorica is provided by local buses with tickets purchased onboard with cash. Podgorica is Montenegro's main hub for intercity buses, which is useful if you want to get to the coast from Podgorica Airport</cite>. Day pass ~€1.5–2; monthly ~€25. Buses run 5:30am–11pm. Frequency: 15–30 min in center, 30–60 min periphery. Reliable for day trips.
car_free.airport_to_center: Podgorica Airport (TGD) 11 km south. Taxi: €12–15 (20 min). Bus line 3: €1.2 (35–45 min, infrequent). No direct shuttle. Ride-share (Bolt/Uber) available.

startup_community.scene_summary: <cite index="12-29">Startup Montenegro: This organization hosts regular events and meetups for entrepreneurs and startup founders, including pitch competitions, workshops, and networking events</cite>. Scene exists but is emerging; dominated by local entrepreneurs and tourism/property startups. AI/SaaS scene weak. Better to connect via Nomad Coffee meetups or online (Telegram groups). <cite index="6-26,6-27">Google "ugliest Balkan capital city reddit" and you see what is the most popular answer. I've been to all the Balkans, and Podgorica is probably the most "meh" and uninteresting city I remember visiting</cite>—cultural buzz is low.

key_venues.name: Startup Montenegro
key_venues.what: Regular pitch nights, workshops, networking for founders

key_venues.name: NEST Coworking
key_venues.what: Community-focused; daily activities, entrepreneur events

key_venues.name: Podgorica Digital Nomads (Facebook)
key_venues.what: 1,000+ members; informal connections, advice, local tips

startup_community.recurring_events: <cite index="12-27,12-28">Nomad Coffee: This monthly meetup is a great way to meet other digital nomads and remote workers in a relaxed atmosphere. It's usually held at a different cafe or coworking space each month</cite>. Startup Montenegro hosts events ~2–4/month (verify on Facebook).

bonus.flight_from_vie: <cite index="40-8,40-9">Podgorica is Montenegro's main hub for intercity buses, which is useful if you want to get to the coast from Podgorica Airport. Although you'll need to take a train or taxi from the airport to first reach the bus station</cite>. VIE→TGD: ~2 hrs direct (Lufthansa, Wizz Air). Cost: €50–150 (budget/full-service). Airport taxis to city ~€15.

timezones.vs_vienna: CET / CEST (same, no offset during summer)

bonus.classical_music: No dedicated concert hall. Orthodox churches (Cathedral of Christ's Resurrection) occasional classical events. Summer: Montenegro classical festivals in Kotor/coastal towns (30–60 km). Recommend live-streaming subscriptions (Berlin Philharmonic, etc.) + YouTube channels for consistent access.
bonus.dance_scene: Bachata/Salsa: Limited formal scene in Podgorica. No regular dance schools or clubs specializing in Latin. Summer (June–Aug) coastal towns (Budva, Kotor) host occasional Latin dance nights. Winter: Informal meetups via Facebook groups. Suggest: practice online + travel to Budva (45 km, 1 hr) for summer season dance socials, or maintain Zoom lessons.

concerns: Visa instability: Turkish citizen visa-free entry recently reduced from 90 to 30 days; EU pressure may trigger further restrictions (monitor 2026 updates).

Aesthetic/cultural appeal low: <cite index="13-10,13-11,13-12">As a tourist destination, Podgorica lacks major attractions or distinctive character. Many people find it uninteresting or generic, especially when compared to coastal cities or the scenic areas in the north. While there are a few things to see such as parks and rivers, visitors looking for cultural or historical experiences may be disappointed</cite>. Not ideal for inspiration/social life.
Dance scene weak: Bachata/salsa clubs virtually absent; nearest reliable scene is coastal (Budva, Kotor), 45 min away.
Classical music limited: No concert hall or regular classical season; reliance on streaming.
Startup/AI ecosystem thin: Better for indie hacking (cheap, quiet, fast internet) than for networking or partnerships.
Summer heat (35°C+) and winter cold (5°C) reduce comfort for outdoor work; spring/autumn best.

summary: A big Adriatic beach city on the Romagna Riviera with a surprisingly rich Roman and Renaissance old town, Fellini heritage, real coworking, and San Marino half an hour away. A summer party-and-beach hub that becomes an affordable, workable city off-season. CET; counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Rimini is two cities: a packed beach-and-clubbing resort in summer, and a calm, affordable working town the rest of the year with a genuine Roman/Renaissance centre, coworking spaces and fiber. Bologna is an hour by train, San Marino is 30 minutes, and the long sandy seafront is right there. Same-timezone with home; best valued and most workable in spring and autumn.
best_months: May to June and September (warm sea, calmer); packed July-August, quiet winters

internet.notes: Coworking spaces run on fiber; general city wifi varies, so a coworking or checked rental is wise for calls

work_spots.name: Rimini Innovation Square / Spazio26
work_spots.notes: Several coworking options (Rimini Innovation Square, Master Coworking, Spazio26, Cooweb) on fiber

work_spots.name: Old-town & seafront cafes
work_spots.notes: Cafes around Piazza Tre Martiri and the Fellini-era seafront

stays.name: Apartment (centre, off-season rate)
stays.why: Cheap off-season (summer spikes hard); centre is walkable and near both old town and beach

visa.details: On your Slovak permit you enter visa-free, but Italy is Schengen, so this COUNTS toward your 90/180.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Coastal air; Po Valley haze possible in winter
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Safe; usual resort-town crowds and nightlife care in summer

ecommerce_events.name: Rimini Fiera trade fairs (a major Italian expo hub); summer events

weather.conditions: Adriatic; hot summers, cool winters

options.name: Rimini and Riccione beaches
options.quality: Long, gently shelving sandy beaches (very crowded and lined with lidos in summer)

options.name: City gyms
options.notes: Plus beach sports and seafront cycling

gym.running_options: The long seafront promenade and the Marecchia river park
gym.yoga_pilates: Several studios and beach yoga in summer

day_trips.hiking: San Marino's Monte Titano (30min), the Montefeltro hills (Urbino, San Leo), and the Marecchia valley

sailing.notes: Adriatic sailing and watersports from the Rimini marina

new_hobbies.hobby: Liscio ballroom dancing and beach volleyball
new_hobbies.venue: Romagna dance halls; Rimini beach courts
new_hobbies.notes: Romagna is the home of liscio ballroom dance (a fun contrast to salsa/bachata); the beaches are Italy's beach-sport capital

coworking_retreats.notes: City coworking and the odd coliving rather than organised retreats

car_free.walkability: Flat, walkable, very cycle-friendly; trains and buses along the coast
car_free.public_transport: Trains (Bologna ~1h), buses and the Metromare rapid bus; bikes everywhere; no car needed
car_free.airport_to_center: Rimini (RMI) ~10min (~20 EUR); Bologna (BLQ) ~1h by train

startup_community.scene_summary: A trade-fair and tourism economy with several coworking spaces and proximity to San Marino's small tech/ecommerce corridor; workable for solo focus, light on tech networking outside fair season.

key_venues.name: Rimini Innovation Square
key_venues.what: Local coworking community

bonus.flight_from_vie: Seasonal direct or via Bologna (BLQ) + 1h train
bonus.classical_music: Modest local season; Bologna and Ravenna nearby for more
bonus.dance_scene: Romagna liscio ballroom; big summer club scene (Riccione)

concerns: Overrun and pricey in peak summer; sleepy in winter
General city wifi varies (use a coworking)
Beach-resort vibe over substance for some

sources.name: Anyplace Rimini coworking
sources.what: Coworking (Rimini Innovation Square, Master, Spazio26), getting around

sources.name: Coworker Rimini
sources.what: Old town (Arco d'Augusto, Tiberius Bridge), Fellini, San Marino base

summary: Roatan is an affordable Caribbean island with adequate coworking infrastructure, reliable internet for calls, and exceptional diving/water activities—but internet reliability remains inconsistent and visa uncertainty exists for 7-day stays. Best suited as a 2-week minimum trip for Riza given workload demands.
remote_work_pitch: Roatan offers Beach Desk coworking with phone booths, laptop-friendly cafes (Bean Crazy, Café de Palo), and Starlink availability—all within a walkable West End hub. Low cost of living (€8–€12 lunch, €15–€25 sit-down dinner) frees budget for coworking + accommodation. However, internet variability and the 90-day visa issue mean short 7-day stays are risky for heavy client work; consider extending to 10–14 days minimum.
best_months: March–May, September–October (dry/warm, lower prices, less crowded)

internet.notes: Fiber patchy in West End; Starlink ~50–150 Mbps available; Tigo/Claro mobile; backup local SIM recommended for video calls. Occasional outages during rainy season.

work_spots.name: Beach Desk
work_spots.notes: Modern coworking with AC, monitors, private phone cabins, free coffee/water, outdoor deck. Central location, reliable WiFi.

work_spots.name: Roatan Cowork
work_spots.notes: AC shared office, free WiFi, backup generator, kitchenette, outdoor deck, parking. Good for hot-desking.

work_spots.name: Bean Crazy Cafe
work_spots.best_for: calls (tested reliable for Zoom), casual work
work_spots.notes: Local favorite, strong WiFi, relaxed vibe, good for 2–3 hr sessions. Popular with expats/remote workers.

work_spots.name: Café de Palo
work_spots.notes: Super chic, ocean breeze, killer lattes. Good for focused 2–3 hr morning sessions.

stays.name: Beach House Boutique Hotel (Half Moon Bay)
stays.why: Spacious suites with balconies, dreamy views, Caribbean design. On water with charm; fits budget for 7 days + coworking + meals.

stays.name: Seagrape Plantation Resort
stays.why: Lush grounds, ocean-view villas, dive center on-site. Peaceful middle ground; fits budget well, room for coworking + food.

stays.name: West Bay Lodge & Spa
stays.why: Budget-friendly, well-maintained, friendly staff. Leaves €500+ for coworking + meals within €2000 total budget.

visa.details: Turkish citizens + Slovak Pobyt holders are EU citizens; Honduras allows EU citizens visa-free entry with automatic 90-day tourist visa. Can extend 30 days (US$20) at immigration office in Coxen Hole if needed. No issue for 7-day stay; declare accommodation on arrival.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Caribbean island, sea breeze keeps air clean. AQI typically 20–50. No major pollution; rainy season (Oct–Jan) slight increase in humidity.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: West End is safe for solo daytime/evening movement; tourist-friendly, expat community. Avoid walking with valuables late night (>11pm). No gang activity in tourist areas. Standard Caribbean precautions apply.

weather.conditions: Tropical, humid. High season (Dec–Apr) dry; May–Sep warm/wet with scattered showers.

options.name: West Bay Beach
options.quality: Excellent – postcard-perfect white sand, calm turquoise water, easy snorkeling off shore
options.transit_from_center: 10 min water taxi (€3–5) from West End

options.name: Half Moon Bay
options.quality: Very good – protected reef, calm waters, excellent snorkeling/diving nearby

options.name: Paya Bay (East Side)
options.quality: Good – secluded, fewer tourists, pristine coral

options.name: Local Gym West End
options.facilities: Weights, cardio machines, some free weights, basic gym
options.notes: No-frills but functional. English-speaking staff. Popular with expats.

options.name: Anthony's Sports Club (Pineapple Villas)
options.facilities: Weights, cardio, some classes, spa on-site
options.notes: More upscale; ~20 min taxi from West End. Pool access.

gym.running_options: West End beachfront promenade, flat; early morning (5:30–7am) recommended due to heat. ~3–5 km routes feasible.
gym.yoga_pilates: Several studios in West End offering drop-in classes (€10–15/class). Instructors English-speaking. Beach yoga also offered at some resorts.

day_trips.hiking: Punta Gorda (East End jungle trails, 30 min taxi); Anthony's Key Resort (jungle/mangrove hike + snorkel combo). Transit: taxi to trailhead (~€20 round-trip), no car required.

sailing.best_season: March–May, September–October (dry winds, calm seas on west side)
sailing.notes: Roatan is famous for diving, less for sailing. West side (West End/Half Moon Bay) calm year-round; east side rough in trade wind season. Island is a sailing/motor-yacht stopover; charter availability good Mar–May.

new_hobbies.venue: Sun Divers Roatan
new_hobbies.notes: Roatan is world-renowned for diving on the Mesoamerican Barrier Reef. Intro dives, PADI certification, and shore/boat dives. Riza swims, so diving is natural fit. Perfect for 7-day immersion.

new_hobbies.venue: Beach Concessions (West Bay, Half Moon Bay)
new_hobbies.notes: Casual alternative to diving; coral gardens and fish schools visible 10–30 ft from shore. No certification needed. Popular afternoon activity.

new_hobbies.venue: Roatan Freediving Club (informal, check Lu.ma/Meetup)
new_hobbies.notes: Growing community on Roatan. Combines swimming + ocean expertise. Scenic, meditative practice in warm Caribbean waters.

coworking_retreats.notes: No curated coworking retreats (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, NomadX, Mokrin House, Sun and Co, Work Wanderers) operate in/near Roatan. The island has ad-hoc digital nomad community but no structured group co-living programs. Beach Desk + Roatan Cowork fill the gap for short stays.

car_free.walkability: West End is highly walkable (restaurants, cafes, dive shops, coworking within 10 min walk). West Bay more car-dependent. Sandy Bay residential; taxi to West End ~5–10 min cheap.
car_free.public_transport: Limited buses/collectivos between West End & Sandy Bay/French Harbour (~€1–2). Taxis abundant, flat rates €5–15 depending on distance. No metro/tram. Local ferries to Utila/Honduras mainland available.
car_free.airport_to_center: Juan Manuel Galvez Int'l Airport (RTB) 12 km from West End. Taxi ~20 min, €15–20. Some hotels offer shuttles. No airport bus, but ferry to La Ceiba available (~€33 one-way).

startup_community.scene_summary: Roatan has virtually no startup/tech scene. Expat community is retirement + dive-shop focused. No tech meetups, hacker spaces, or founder events. IndieHackers/Startup communities extremely thin. Not a viable hub for AI/ecommerce networking.
startup_community.recurring_events: None documented. Meetup.com shows minimal activity. Digital nomad WhatsApp/Facebook groups exist but unstructured.

bonus.flight_from_vie: Vienna (VIE) to Roatan (RTB): ~16–18 hrs with 1–2 connections (typically Miami or Houston). No direct flights. ~€600–900 return. Frequency: daily options via major carriers (United, Spirit, American).
bonus.classical_music: No concert halls or formal classical music venues on Roatan. Expat community holds occasional informal recitals. Best: fly to La Ceiba/Tegucigalpa for serious performances. Roatan is beach/dive-centric, not cultural hub.
bonus.dance_scene: Bachata/Salsa available at casual bars (West End: Sundowner's, Blue Marlin Beach Bar, Roatan Island Brewing Co.). Informal lessons via expat instructors or dance instructors at resorts. Garifuna drumming/dance at cultural events (Garifuna Settlement Day, Sept 19). Not a dance-scene destination like Havana/Santo Domingo.

concerns: Internet reliability: Fiber patchy, mobile variable (15–25 Mbps), Starlink adds equipment cost; backup local SIM essential for client calls.
7-day stay risky: No digital nomad visa; 90-day tourist visa is blunt tool—consider extending to 10–14 days to justify logistics.
No startup/tech community: AI/ecommerce networking non-existent; Riza's indie-hacker/Claude API focus won't find peers here.
Timezone pain: -7 hrs from Vienna means client calls (London 6am, Tokyo midnight) are tough. Best for async/pre-recorded work.
Classical music absent: Roatan is diving/beach-resort town, not cultural destination.
Humidity & rainy season: Oct–Jan = scattered showers, mugginess; May–Sep hot/humid. Not ideal for lifting focus.

summary: Saranda is a beautiful coastal escape with surprisingly good infrastructure for short 7-day stays, but has significant structural limitations: only one coworking space (tight for daily calls), limited startup scene, and weak winter weather. Budget fits €1,600–1,800 comfortably, but the lack of dedicated phone booths and emerging-stage cafe WiFi may challenge Riza's video-call workflow with UK/EU clients.
remote_work_pitch: Saranda offers stunning Ionian Sea views, 300+ sunny days annually, and affordable nightly rates (€60–90/night) that free up budget for quality coworking at Saranda Marketing & Coworking (€25/day, 300+ Mbps, 24/7 access). The single coworking space is newly opened (June 2024), purposefully built for nomads with sea views and community events. However, rely on it—backup cafes have inconsistent WiFi, and no formal phone booth infrastructure means you may need to work from your hotel for sensitive client calls.
best_months: May, June, September, October (warm, dry, low crowds, prices stable). Avoid July–August (peak season, tripled prices) and November–February (rainy, cold).

internet.notes: Coworking: 300+ Mbps fibre. Home: ~20 Mbps (MENO provider, €17/month). Cafes: 10–20 Mbps, variable. Mobile backup: Vodafone/One 4G recommended. Call reliability at coworking: excellent; cafes: risky for video calls.

work_spots.name: Saranda Marketing & Coworking
work_spots.neighborhood: Rruga Gjergj Araniti (5th Street), city center
work_spots.notes: Only coworking in Saranda. 15 hot desks, 480 Mbps download, AC, charging, coffee. Sea views. 24/7 access suits multiple timezones. No private phone booths—open desks only. Monthly networking 'Business after hours' event. Highly reliable for video calls. Book 2+ hours in advance.

work_spots.name: Hashtag2
work_spots.notes: Mix of indoor/outdoor seating. Good WiFi, some plugs. Smoothies, pastries. Relaxed vibe. WiFi adequate for email/async work; avoid video calls here.

work_spots.name: ZUM Cafe
work_spots.notes: Bright, cheerful, staff very accommodating. Reliable WiFi. Good for 2–3 hour sessions. Popular with other nomads. No phone booths.

work_spots.name: Limani (Bar Restaurant)
work_spots.notes: Cornerstone for expats & nomads. Reliable WiFi, outdoor seating with sea views, peoplewatching. Good for working 2–3 hours. Wednesday informal nomad meetups reported. No phone booths.

stays.name: Andon Lapa Hotel & Spa
stays.why: 4-star beachfront, modern, gym + sauna onsite. Excellent WiFi noted by nomads. Private beach. Strong reviews for comfort. Well within budget—leaves €1,405 for coworking (€175) + food (€700+). Opened Feb 2022, well-maintained.

stays.name: Porto Eda Hotel
stays.neighborhood: Main boulevard, 30 m from central square
stays.why: Beachfront, sea-view balconies, central location, good WiFi. Budget-friendly (€1,510 remaining). 3-star, no gym, but steps from everything. All-nighter-friendly (casino/bar in building).

stays.name: Hotel Butrinti & SPA
stays.why: 5-star, fitness center, seasonal pool, restaurant. Continental breakfast. Sea views, balconies. Excellent for calls (quiet rooms, good WiFi). Leaves €1,440 for coworking + food. High-end but within budget.

visa.details: Turkish citizens visa-free for 90 days within 180-day rolling period. Slovak Pobyt (residence permit) is EU/EEA document; Riza does not need separate Albanian visa—Slovak Pobyt counts as valid residence document for some visa-free provisions, though primary entry is Turkish passport (visa-free 90 days). For stays beyond 90 days, Type D visa + residence permit required. 7-day stay: no visa needed. Note: Slovakia is Schengen; Riza can leverage Schengen entry if needed.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Coastal town, sea breezes. No major industrial pollution. Summer (June–Aug): occasional Sahara dust; winter (Dec–Feb): higher PM2.5. Overall AQI 40–60 (good). Best months: May, Sept, Oct.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Low crime, very safe for solo travelers and women. Walking at night safe in city center and waterfront. Avoid remote mountain areas (unexploded landmines near Kosovo border). Scams: taxi overcharging (use Bolt app), ATM theft (use bank ATMs), accommodation disputes (book via Booking/Airbnb direct). No gang activity or violent crime reported. Expat community reports welcoming locals. Solo night walks on waterfront promenade safe.

weather.conditions: Mediterranean: hot, dry summers; cold, wet winters. 300+ sunny days/year.

options.name: Main Saranda Beach (Plazhi Kryesor)
options.quality: Clear, calm waters. Excellent for swimming June–Oct (19–25°C). Safe, family-friendly.

options.name: Ksamil Beaches (day trip)
options.quality: Soft sand, turquoise water, best beach on Albanian Riviera. Multiple beach clubs with loungers.
options.transit_from_center: Bus from Saranda (20 min), €0.40. Car-free option: local bus or taxi (€5–8).

options.name: Plazhi i Ri (New Beach)
options.quality: Sheltered, shallow, calm. Good for morning/evening swims.
options.transit_from_center: 15 min walk north from city center.

options.name: Saranda Petra Gym
options.neighborhood: Mitat Hoxha 69 (hillside, slight climb from center)
options.facilities: Weights (up to 500kg), cardio, boxing area, abs/core zone, showers. Small but very clean. Yoga/Pilates classes available.
options.notes: Highly rated (5★). Owners Zarko & Yelena are sport-oriented, friendly. Stunning beach views/sunsets from gym. Free WiFi. Cleans every 30 min. Sea view = motivating for Riza. Best boutique gym in Saranda. 7-day pass (~€35) reasonable for short stay.

options.name: Silhouette Sport Center
options.facilities: Strength, cardio, HIIT, Cycle, Barre, Row. Experiential lighting & sound. Luxury amenities.
options.notes: Modern, upscale. "Best gym with a view" per reviews. Slightly more expensive but premium vibe. 3x/week monthly = €25. Good for intensive training week.

options.name: Saranda Gym (at hotels)
options.neighborhood: Various (Andon Lapa, Hotel Butrinti, Bougainville Bay)
options.facilities: Varies. Andon Lapa: excellent, modern, sea views, steam room. Bougainville: mixed reviews (some outdated equipment). Butrinti: fitness center, seasonal pool.
options.notes: If staying at Andon Lapa or Butrinti, use hotel gym (included/day pass ~€10–15). Convenient for morning/evening workouts before/after work.

gym.running_options: Waterfront promenade (flat, scenic, 2 km loop). Parks: limited. Best for running early morning (7–8 AM) or evening (18:00+) to avoid summer heat.
gym.yoga_pilates: Saranda Petra Gym offers Yoga & Pilates classes. Instructors English-friendly. Day drop-in €8–10. Studio vibe, small class sizes (5–10 people). Other studios: check Expats in Saranda Facebook group for ad-hoc classes.

day_trips.hiking: Lekuresi Castle (45 min hike from center via marked trail, 260 m elevation). Trailhead: Rruga Butrinti area. Panoramic views. Accessible without car—walk or local minibus (€0.50) to base, then hike. Butrint National Park (15 km south, UNESCO site): bus (€1) + 1.5 hr walk, or minibus (€3). Longer hiking: Saranda–Dhermi coastal trail (4–5 hrs, mixed terrain), bus one-way, walk back or reverse.

marinas.name: Saranda Port / Ferry Terminal
marinas.transit_from_center: 5 min walk. Ferry to Corfu (Greece) 1x daily, 30 min. Boat charters/tours depart here.

sailing.best_season: May–Oct (calm waters, 15–20 knots wind, 19–25°C water). Peak: July–Aug (hot, crowded). Shoulder: May, June, Sept (ideal: warm, fewer boats, stable wind).
sailing.notes: Ionian Sea is protected, shallow, Mediterranean sailing conditions. Full-day boat tours popular (€50–80/person, includes swimming spots like Turtle Cave, Gremina, Kakome). No dedicated RYA/ASA sailing school in Saranda; DIY charters via port operators or Corfu-based Greek schools (30 min ferry). Regattas: local fishing community events (informal). Scene is emerging—mostly tourist tours, not serious sailing club.

new_hobbies.venue: Ionian Sea, Saranda coast (informal; no school)
new_hobbies.language: English, Greek (Greek instructors from Corfu sometimes visit)
new_hobbies.notes: Crystal-clear Ionian waters ideal for freediving. No formal school, but private instructors offer 1-day intro courses (€150–200) through boat operators or Corfu dive centers. Spearfishing community present (local sport). Consider: Saranda is emerging destination for water sports; instructors may require advance booking.

new_hobbies.venue: None formal in Saranda; nearest: Berat pottery workshops (2 hrs by bus)
new_hobbies.notes: Saranda lacks dedicated pottery studios. Berat (UNESCO town, 2 hrs north) is pottery hub—traditional workshops welcome tourists. Day trip: morning bus, afternoon class, evening return. Riza interested in crafts; Berat artisan scene stronger than Saranda.

new_hobbies.venue: Ksamil Beach or nearby Vlore (45 km north)
new_hobbies.notes: Ionian winds 15–25 knots (summer). Ksamil: sheltered, shallow, beginner-friendly. Vlore: more exposed, stronger winds, better for intermediate+. Local operators offer 1-day beginner courses (€100–150). Best season: May–Oct (windy, warm water). Scene emerging; check local beach clubs or ask coworking community.

new_hobbies.venue: Saranda port, local fishing boats, informal
new_hobbies.notes: Saranda fishing culture strong; local fishermen occasionally invite tourists on boat trips. No formal school. Connect via port vendors or Expats in Saranda Facebook. Cost: minimal if included in boat tour (~€50–80). Mediterranean spearfishing skill popular with expat community.

new_hobbies.venue: Ksamil or Saranda main beach (seasonal instructors)
new_hobbies.neighborhood: Ksamil day trip or main beach
new_hobbies.notes: Summer wind patterns (thermals, 12–18 knots) suit beginners. Equipment rental & lessons available through beach clubs in Ksamil (€40–60/day). Vlore (45 km) has more established windsurfing scene. Saranda conditions: moderate, best May–Oct.

coworking_retreats.notes: No curated coworking retreats (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, Sun & Co, NomadX, Mokrin House, Work Wanderers) operate in or near Saranda as of May 2026. Saranda Coworking is single, daily-rate space (not a retreat operator). Nearby: Tirana has coworking (Dutch Hub, Coolab); Durrës emerging. Saranda too small/young for organized co-living retreats. Consider: staying at hotel + daily coworking is current model for nomads.

car_free.walkability: Excellent in city center (compact, 1–2 km). Waterfront promenade walkable. Hilly terrain limits walking to outlying areas. Center easily covered on foot 20–30 min.
car_free.public_transport: Minibuses (furgons) connect Saranda to Tirana (5 hrs, €5–7), Vlore (1.5 hrs, €3), Ksamil (20 min, €0.40). Taxis: unmetered, negotiate fare (~€2 city, €5–8 airport). Bolt app: limited availability, but available. No metro/tram. Walking best for daily needs.
car_free.airport_to_center: Nearest airport: Corfu, Greece (40 km, 30 min speedboat, €15–20 + ferry cost €10–15). Or: Tirana (150 km, 3 hrs bus €7). Saranda has helipad (limited). Speedboat Corfu–Saranda: fastest option for EU travelers (daily service, 30 min). Taxi Corfu airport to port: €15–20. Total Corfu route: €50–70. Tirana: bus + taxi, €15–20.

startup_community.scene_summary: Saranda startup scene is virtually non-existent. No visible founder meetups, demo nights, or tech community. Coworking (opened June 2024) is marketing/general coworking, not tech-focused. Expat community is tourism, digital nomads, lifestylers—not builders. Tirana (capital, 150 km) has Dutch Hub, Coolab, tech events, but requires 3-hr commute. For Riza's AI/Claude API/n8n indie-hacker interests, Saranda offers minimal peer network.

key_venues.name: Saranda Marketing & Coworking
key_venues.what: Only formal coworking. Monthly networking 'Business after hours' but marketing/general biz focus, not tech/startup. ~21 nationalities, but no tech crowd reported.

key_venues.name: Expats in Saranda (Facebook Group)
key_venues.what: ~1,500 members. Lifestyle, travel advice, accommodation tips. Not tech-focused. American Blue Bar mentioned as unofficial expat hub. Wednesday cafe meetups (informal nomad coworking, no business focus).

startup_community.recurring_events: None formal. Saranda Coworking 'Business after hours' monthly (coffee shop next door). No demo nights, pitch events, or AI/indie-hacker meetups.

bonus.flight_from_vie: Vienna (VIE) to Tirana (TIA): 2 hrs direct (Wizz Air, Ryanair, €40–80 one-way). Tirana to Saranda: 3 hrs bus (€5–7). Total: 5–6 hrs, ~€100 return. Alternative: Vienna to Corfu, Greece (2.5 hrs, Ryanair), then 30-min speedboat to Saranda (€30). Total: 3.5–4 hrs, ~€90–120 return. Speedboat route faster, scenic, direct to Saranda.
bonus.classical_music: No concert halls or classical venues in Saranda. Closest: Tirana National Theater (3 hrs, occasional classical events). Saranda: occasional summer outdoor concerts (waterfront promenade, tourism events). Not a classical music hub. Riza may find limited live classical music options.
bonus.dance_scene: Bachata/Salsa: No formal dance schools. Local nightlife: bars, clubs (Jericho, City Lounge, American Blue Bar) play pop, electronic, some Latin (seasonal). Spontaneous Latin dancing at summer beach clubs. Not a dedicated salsa/bachata destination. Nearest: Tirana or Athens (2 hrs ferry to Corfu, then 6 hrs to Athens) for serious dance scenes. Riza would need to practice solo or find ad-hoc groups via Expats Facebook.

concerns: No phone booths or private call rooms at only coworking; open desks only (workaround: hotel room for sensitive video calls).
Weak startup/indie-hacker scene; Riza will feel isolated from AI/tech community (nearest: Tirana 150 km away).
Winter (Nov–Feb) cold, rainy, limiting outdoor activities and swimming (not ideal for lifestyle interests).
Limited classical music events (Riza interested in classical music; Saranda has none).
Cafe WiFi inconsistent (10–20 Mbps); coworking reliance high (must pre-book or risk full capacity).
No formal dance schools for bachata/salsa (must find informal groups or travel to Tirana/Athens).
Peak season (July–Aug) prices triple; budget tight if dates slip into summer.
Car-free travel: minibuses unreliable schedule; day trips require planning (Ksamil, Lekuresi, Berat all 45+ min one-way).
Language barrier: English limited outside tourist zones; German (Riza's B1 level) rarely spoken.

summary: A purpose-built Red Sea resort city at the tip of Sinai: luxury hotels, marinas, and some of the world's best diving at Ras Mohammed and the Straits of Tiran. Polished but tourist-packaged, with patchy coworking. A diving-and-resort base, and visa-free for you within the Sinai zone. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+2.
remote_work_pitch: Sharm el-Sheikh is the polished, dive-centric Sinai base: warm reef-fringed water year-round with legendary sites (Ras Mohammed, the Tiran straits), resort comforts, marinas, and a desert-and-mountain backdrop (Mount Sinai, Dahab 85km north). Costs are low and it's an hour ahead of Vienna. The big plus for you: as a Turkish citizen you can enter the South Sinai resort zone visa-free for short stays. It's a packaged resort city more than an organic nomad town, with patchy coworking (you'll work from hotels, apartments and cafes), so it suits a comfortable, diving-focused stretch.

summary: A lush Black Sea city ringed by green mountains: an ancient Silk Road port with the cliff-clinging Sumela Monastery, the alpine lake of Uzungol nearby, and the Georgian border (Batumi) within reach. Cooler, greener and rainier than the Aegean coast. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+3.
remote_work_pitch: Trabzon is the off-the-beaten-path, green-and-cool home-turf option: a Black Sea city of misty mountains, tea plantations and dramatic sights like Sumela Monastery and the lake at Uzungol, with the Georgian resort of Batumi a few hours away for a change of scene. As home it's frictionless and Schengen-free, and notably affordable. Coworking is minimal and English is less common than on the tourist coast, so it suits an adventurous, nature-rich, focused stay.
best_months: May to September (greenest, mildest); wet much of the year, cool winters

summary: Tunisia's Mediterranean capital: a layered city of a UNESCO medina, the ancient ruins of Carthage and the blue-and-white village of Sidi Bou Said, with very low costs, a small coworking scene (Cogite, The Dot), and a French-and-Arabic culture. Affordable and historic. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); CET (same hours as Vienna).
remote_work_pitch: Tunis is the affordable, history-rich North African base that's same-timezone with home: a layered capital wrapping a UNESCO medina, the ruins of Carthage and the postcard village of Sidi Bou Said, with Mediterranean beaches at La Marsa and Gammarth. Costs are very low, there's a small but real coworking scene (Cogite, The Dot), and as a Turkish citizen you enter visa-free for up to 90 days; it's Schengen-free. French and Arabic dominate and the nomad scene is small, so it suits a budget-friendly, culture-led stint.

summary: A laid-back Caribbean archipelago in northwest Panama: overwater bungalows, surf, snorkeling, a small backpacker-nomad scene. Visa-free 90 days. Idyllic and cheap-ish but remote, with island-grade internet — a sea-and-surf interlude, not a work base.
remote_work_pitch: Bocas del Toro is Panama's Caribbean island dream: a chain of jungly islands with overwater cabins, surf breaks, snorkeling, and a relaxed backpacker-nomad vibe. For a recon it tests island Caribbean living; honestly it's remote with island internet, so treat it as a sea-and-surf escape with async work rather than a serious base.

summary: Tunisia's relaxed beach-resort town on the Cap Bon peninsula: long sandy Mediterranean beaches, a whitewashed medina, jasmine-scented gardens, and very low costs. remote_work_pitch: Hammamet is the easygoing beach option in Tunisia: long sandy Mediterranean beaches, a pretty old medina, gardens and a calm resort pace, all at very low cost and same-timezone with home. Tunis and its coworking are about an hour away. As a Turkish citizen you enter visa-free for up to 90 days, and it's Schengen-free. There's essentially no coworking (you'll work from your rental, beach hotels and cafes) and it's seasonal, so it suits a sunny, restful, swimming-focused stretch rather than a work hub.

summary: Green, historic Bursa beneath Mount Uludag: the first Ottoman capital, with grand mosques, thermal baths, silk bazaars, and Turkey's main ski mountain on its doorstep. Industrial and less nomad-oriented, but uniquely good for thermal-and-mountain living. Home country, Schengen-free. Does NOT count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Bursa pairs Ottoman heritage with nature: the Uludag massif for hiking and Turkey's best skiing, the Cekirge thermal-bath district for daily soaks, and a short ferry hop to Istanbul across the Marmara. Coworking is limited (chain branches like eOfis/Workinton) and it's an industrial city rather than a nomad scene, but as a Turkish citizen it's frictionless, cheap and Schengen-free, best for a thermal-and-mountain stint.

summary: Turkey's gastronomy capital in the southeast: a UNESCO 'City of Gastronomy' famed for baklava, pistachios and kebabs, with a restored old town, a renowned mosaic museum, and exceptionally low costs. An authentic, food-led home-turf base. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+3.
remote_work_pitch: Gaziantep is for the food and the value: a UNESCO City of Gastronomy where baklava, pistachios and legendary kebabs are a daily pleasure, with a beautifully restored old town, the world-class Zeugma mosaic museum, and some of the lowest living costs anywhere (around USD1,700/month covers a lot). As home turf it's frictionless and Schengen-free. Internet can be modest and the formal coworking/English scene is thin, so it suits a focused, deeply authentic, food-rich stretch.

summary: A relaxed Andaman-coast island south of Krabi, anchored by KoHub, one of Asia's original beachy coworking communities. Calm, long sandy beaches, swimmable sea, and a tight remote-work crowd in season. Quiet and partly shuttered in the May to October monsoon. Non-Schengen, so days do not count toward your 90/180.
remote_work_pitch: Koh Lanta gives you a genuine coworking community (KoHub) plus warm swimmable sea and sunset beaches, without Phuket's chaos. It is a strong winter base for shipping work in the morning and swimming or sailing in the afternoon, with onward boats to the Andaman islands. Internet at the good spots is fast; the island simply quiets down in monsoon season.
best_months: November to April (dry, calm sea); May to October is monsoon (rough sea, many closures)

internet.notes: KoHub and coliving spots run fast fiber (BeachHub advertises ~1 Gbps); villa fiber more variable

summary: Western Australia's isolated, sunny capital: superb beaches, the world's most remote major city, a wine region (Margaret River) within reach, mining-money prosperity. Visitor Visa required. The remotest pick in the project — gorgeous but a long way from anywhere, with a small nomad scene.
remote_work_pitch: Perth is one of the world's sunniest, most isolated major cities: long Indian-Ocean beaches (Cottesloe, Scarborough), a Mediterranean climate, the Margaret River wine region 3 hours south, and a quietly prosperous mining-driven economy. For a recon it tests far-western Australia; the same visa/time-zone/flight friction applies as the rest of the country, plus the genuine 'world's most remote major city' tag — but the trade-off is space, calm, and great weather.

summary: China's dazzling global megacity: world-class infrastructure, the Bund, a huge tech/ecommerce economy. The catch for you: mainland China is NOT visa-free for Turkish citizens (needs a tourist visa; only a short transit-visa-free window exists). Censored internet needs a VPN.
remote_work_pitch: Shanghai is China at its most cosmopolitan: stunning architecture, deep ecommerce expertise (the world's biggest market), and elite infrastructure. For a recon it's the hardest-to-access in this batch — you need a proper Chinese visa, the internet is censored (VPN essential for your tools), and payments run on local apps — so treat it as a deliberate, well-prepared trip, not a casual base.
best_months: March-May, September-November (mild); humid summer, cold damp winter

internet.notes: Fast but HEAVILY CENSORED — Google, WhatsApp, many tools blocked; a reliable VPN is essential for your workflow and even then can be unstable

summary: The UAE's cultural capital, next door to Dubai: more conservative and traditional, notably cheaper for accommodation, with museums and heritage. VISA REQUIRED for Turkish citizens (pre-approved e-visa). A budget-conscious Gulf base within commuting distance of Dubai - but alcohol-free, traffic-heavy, and less nomad-oriented.
remote_work_pitch: Sharjah is the UAE's cultural and more affordable emirate, bordering Dubai: rich Islamic-arts museums and heritage, notably cheaper rents, and the same safety and infrastructure. For a recon it tests a budget-conscious Gulf base - some nomads live in Sharjah and use Dubai for work/play. Caveats: it's a dry (alcohol-free) emirate, the Dubai-Sharjah commute is famously traffic-choked, and it's the least nomad-oriented of the three; same Turkish-passport e-visa requirement applies.

concerns: Least nomad-oriented of the three UAE picks

summary: Biggest, cheapest, most chaotic of the group. Direct from VIE (~4h). Visa is the catch: Egypt is e-visa/visa-on-arrival for Turkish citizens (~25 USD), not strictly visa-free. Intense city, weak for car-free living.
remote_work_pitch: Cairo is the wildcard: dirt-cheap, deeply historic, with a surprisingly real Zamalek/Maadi expat-and-cafe scene and direct Vienna flights. But the chaos, traffic, and air quality make it a high-friction recon; it suits curiosity more than a comfortable base.
best_months: October-April (mild, pleasant); avoid May-September heat

internet.notes: Improved fiber in Zamalek/Maadi/New Cairo; older areas slower, verify apartment, occasional throttling

work_spots.name: The District / KMT House coworking
work_spots.notes: Expat-area coworkings with booths and reliable connections

work_spots.name: Zamalek specialty cafes
work_spots.notes: Leafy island district, laptop-friendly cafes, calmer than mainland

stays.name: Zamalek serviced apartment
stays.why: Greenest, calmest, most walkable central district; expat-friendly

stays.name: Maadi serviced apartment
stays.why: Leafy expat suburb, quieter, metro access to center

visa.details: Egypt is NOT visa-free for Turkish citizens: an e-visa or visa-on-arrival (~25 USD, 30 days) is required. Simple to obtain online or at airport. Outside Schengen, does not affect your 90/180 allowance.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Cairo has chronically poor air quality (dust + traffic + industry), among the worst in this group
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Generally safe from violent crime; intense crowds, hustling, and chaotic traffic are the real challenges. Solo evenings OK in Zamalek/Maadi

weather.conditions: Desert, mild winters, very hot summers

options.name: Club and hotel pools
options.quality: Available at sporting clubs/hotels
options.transit_from_center: In-city clubs; Red Sea is a separate trip

options.name: Gold's Gym / sporting clubs
options.facilities: weights, cardio, classes, pools at clubs
options.notes: Good gyms in expat areas, English OK

gym.running_options: Gezira Club, Al-Azhar Park, Nile corniche (early morning)
gym.yoga_pilates: Studios in Zamalek/Maadi with English classes

day_trips.hiking: Limited near city; desert excursions and Saqqara/Giza plateau walks rather than hiking trails

sailing.notes: Nile sailing (feluccas and a yacht club) is a genuine and atmospheric option, though not sea sailing

new_hobbies.hobby: Arabic (Egyptian) language or Nile felucca sailing
new_hobbies.venue: Language schools / Nile clubs
new_hobbies.notes: Egyptian Arabic is the most widely understood dialect; felucca sailing on the Nile is a unique twist on your sailing interest

coworking_retreats.notes: No notable retreat operators; independent coworking in expat districts

car_free.walkability: Zamalek/Maadi walkable internally but the megacity is car/taxi-dependent and traffic is severe
car_free.public_transport: Metro (cheap, expanding) helps; Uber/Careem cheap and essential; walking citywide impractical
car_free.airport_to_center: ~45-60min by Uber/taxi (~12 EUR) depending on notorious traffic

startup_community.scene_summary: Egypt has a genuine and growing startup scene (fintech especially) centered in New Cairo/Maadi, large young English-speaking talent pool. But it's spread across a huge chaotic city, so serendipity is harder than in a compact hub.

key_venues.name: Maadi/New Cairo coworkings
key_venues.what: Startup gathering points

startup_community.recurring_events: Tech meetups in New Cairo, irregular for visitors

bonus.flight_from_vie: ~4h direct (Austrian, EgyptAir), ~200-350 EUR return
bonus.classical_music: Cairo Opera House has a real season (Western and Arabic classical)
bonus.dance_scene: Some Latin/salsa nights in Zamalek/Maadi expat venues, modest

concerns: Severe traffic, weak for car-free living

summary: Senegal's Atlantic capital: a vibrant West-African cultural hub of music, art, and surf on a breezy peninsula. Visa-free 90 days. The same time zone as London (-1h from Vienna) and a true cultural immersion, but a thinner work/nomad scene and a developing-city base - more an adventurous cultural pick than a polished hub.
remote_work_pitch: Dakar is West Africa at its most creative: a peninsula city of Atlantic surf, world-renowned music (mbalax, the Dakar art Biennale), colourful markets, and warm Teranga hospitality. For a recon it's the adventurous, culturally rich end of the spectrum; it's visa-free 90 days and sits at only -1h from Vienna (great overlap), but the nomad/work infrastructure is thinner and it's a developing-city base, so come for culture and surf more than polish.
best_months: November-May (dry season, pleasant); hot humid rainy Jul-Oct

internet.notes: Workable fibre/4G in the good neighborhoods (Almadies/Plateau); verify, SIM backup for calls

work_spots.name: Dakar coworking (Jokkolabs)
work_spots.notes: A small but real coworking scene (Jokkolabs pioneered African coworking); ask for quiet for calls

stays.name: Almadies / Ngor apartment
stays.why: Breezy Atlantic-coast neighborhoods (Almadies/Ngor), near surf and the better cafes

visa.details: Turkish citizens visa-free 90 days (Senegal). Non-Schengen, no effect on 90/180.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Some dust (Harmattan) and traffic; coastal breeze helps
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Senegal is one of West Africa's safer, more stable countries; normal city caution, watch valuables in markets, use taxis at night. Drink bottled water

weather.conditions: Coastal Sahel; pleasant dry Nov-May, hot humid rainy Jul-Oct

options.name: Ngor, Almadies beaches; Ngor Island
options.quality: Atlantic beaches and coves; surf spots; cooler Atlantic water

options.name: Dakar gyms
options.notes: Decent gyms in the affluent coast areas

gym.running_options: Corniche seafront (long coastal road), beaches

day_trips.hiking: Lac Rose (pink lake), Île de Gorée (UNESCO, history), Île de Ngor, Bandia wildlife reserve, Saint-Louis further

sailing.notes: Dakar is a historic Atlantic sailing waypoint (and the original Paris-Dakar finish); some yacht/sailing activity and good surf - relevant to your sea interest

new_hobbies.venue: Ngor/Almadies surf, music schools, French immersion
new_hobbies.notes: Good Atlantic surf; world-famous music scene (learn djembe/sabar); a great place to practise French

coworking_retreats.notes: Pioneering but small coworking scene; few organised retreats

car_free.walkability: Neighborhood pockets walkable; taxis/Yango (ride-hail) for longer hops; new BRT/regional train improving
car_free.public_transport: Taxis + Yango + new BRT and TER train; improving but a bit car-reliant
car_free.airport_to_center: Dakar (DSS) ~50min-1h by taxi (airport is far) (~25 EUR)

startup_community.scene_summary: Small but real (Francophone West Africa's tech is rising, and Jokkolabs began here), French-leaning, a few coworkings. More cultural-adventure than founder-dense; thinner than the East/South African hubs.

key_venues.name: Jokkolabs
key_venues.what: Pioneering African coworking

bonus.flight_from_vie: 1-stop (via Istanbul/Paris/Casablanca), ~9-12h, ~450-750 EUR
bonus.classical_music: Limited; legendary live-music scene (mbalax, jazz)
bonus.dance_scene: Rich West-African dance/music scene; sabar

summary: <cite index="26-11,26-12">Hong Kong is renowned for its high cost of living, consistently ranking among the most expensive cities globally</cite>, which makes the €2000 budget for 7 days challenging but feasible through strategic accommodation and dining choices. <cite index="21-19">With average internet speeds of 217 Mbps</cite>, the infrastructure supports Riza's client calls excellently. The city offers world-class coworking, but only October–December provides optimal climate comfort.
remote_work_pitch: <cite index="1-11,1-12">Multiple coworking spaces feature phone booths essential for client calls, with options in Central, Causeway Bay, and other prime locations</cite>. <cite index="8-4">WeWork has six prime locations across the city, including Central, Sheung Wan, Wan Chai, Quarry Bay, Kwun Tong, and Causeway Bay</cite>, ideal for meetings with UK/EU/UAE clients. <cite index="22-3">Networking opportunities abound through organizations like The Hive, Impact Hub, and numerous industry-specific networking groups</cite>, supporting the indie-hacker AI-builder scene.
best_months: October to December (pleasant weather, less rain, lower humidity); also March–April (spring, milder) though some humidity. Avoid May–September (typhoon season, very hot, high humidity).

internet.notes: <cite index="8-5,8-6">Workspaces include soundproof phone booths, meeting rooms, and high-speed internet with unlimited coffee and beverages</cite>. Mobile networks (3HK, PCCW, SmarTone) are competitive; no call reliability issues reported.

work_spots.name: Banyan Workspace
work_spots.best_for: Focused long work, calls, serene environment
work_spots.notes: <cite index="1-7,1-8,1-9,1-11">Banyan Workspace is tucked along the Quarry Bay waterfront with natural materials, warm textures, and abundant light, attracting professionals and entrepreneurs who value mindful productivity and human connection, with amenities including library for quiet work, meeting rooms, phone booths, event space, and ocean views</cite>.

work_spots.name: WeWork Central (28 Stanley Street)
work_spots.notes: <cite index="3-9">The center offers unique soundproofed call booths on the coworking and lounge floors</cite>. <cite index="8-10,8-11,8-12">Day pass priced at HK$140 includes access to open area and hot desks during office hours, with high-speed WiFi, power plugs, pantry amenities, and pay-per-use options; 3-hour pass is HK$100 and 1-hour pass costs HK$40</cite>.

work_spots.name: Desk-One (Wan Chai or Causeway Bay branch)
work_spots.best_for: Long focus sessions 3+ hours, calm vibe
work_spots.notes: <cite index="2-13,2-14">Desk-One combines a café, library, and workspace into a tranquil and productive environment, with 7 branches across Hong Kong offering reading lounges, quiet co-working zones, private rooms, high-speed WiFi, specialty coffee, independent sockets, printing and scanning services</cite>.

work_spots.name: Garage Society (Sheung Wan or Causeway Bay)
work_spots.hours: Extended hours; private office 24/7, part-time 9 AM–6 PM
work_spots.notes: <cite index="9-3,9-4">Each location features hot desk options for flexible professionals, dedicated desks for those wanting a permanent base, private offices for teams, and event spaces for workshops and networking, with all memberships including high-speed WiFi, meeting room credits, printing facilities, phone booths, and access to member community events</cite>.

stays.name: YING'nFLO Admiralty (Serviced Apartment)
stays.why: <cite index="12-24,12-25,12-26,12-27,12-28">YING'nFLO Admiralty is conveniently located just a 3-minute walk from Admiralty MTR Station and Pacific Place shopping mall, offering stylish accommodation with a kitchen, located a 5-minute MTR ride to Central and 10-minute taxi ride to Airport Express Hong Kong Station, with each apartment offering smart electric door lock, complimentary Wi-Fi, mini-bar, in-room safe, electric kettle, hair dryer, bathroom amenities, slippers, and moveable writing desk, priced at approximately HK$1,000 per day, HK$17,000 per week, or HK$70,000 per month</cite>.

stays.name: Little Tai Hang (Boutique Aparthotel)
stays.why: <cite index="12-9,12-10,12-11,12-12">Little Tai Hang features stunning views of Victoria Harbour with pockets of greens and cosy accommodation in Causeway Bay, only a 5-minute walk from Exit A1 of Tin Hau MTR Station with free WiFi in all areas, a 10-minute walk from Causeway Bay shopping area, and within 15 minutes by public transportation to Central and Wanchai</cite>. Budget-friendly for 7-day stays.

stays.name: Fullerton Ocean Park Hotel (Monthly Rate for 7 days)
stays.why: <cite index="20-8,20-9">Fullerton Ocean Park Hotel Hong Kong offers a special monthly rental package with tiered pricing, with rates starting from HK$24,300 for a 1-month stay</cite>. The pro-rated nightly rate for 7 days approximates €146/night, providing premium amenities within budget.

visa.details: <cite index="21-3,21-15">Turkish citizens can typically enter on a tourist visa, which allows you to stay for up to 90 days</cite>. Riza's Slovak Pobyt residence permit (EU status) provides visa-free or visa-on-arrival access. No work permit needed for remote work (client calls only, no local employment).

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: <cite index="33-1,33-3,33-4">Hong Kong's air quality is good, with an average AQI between 0 and 50, indicating that air pollution is minimal, posing no significant risk to health</cite>. <cite index="47-5">The rainiest period is from May to September when rainfall exceeds 250 mm per month; the least rainy period is from November to January when it drops below 50 mm per month</cite>. Worst months: May–September (haze from mainland China, humidity).
air_water_safety.safety_notes: <cite index="33-18,33-19,33-20,33-21">93 out of 100 residents or visitors say they feel completely safe during the day, and residents and tourists to Hong Kong consistently express a high sense of safety at night, with 85 out of 100 people living in or visiting feeling fully secure during nighttime hours</cite>. <cite index="33-5,33-6,33-7,33-8,33-9">You can drink tap water in Hong Kong without worry—the water supply is treated and monitored to meet safety standards, and many locals and tourists rely on it for daily hydration; if staying in a modern hotel or well-maintained area, the tap water quality is usually excellent</cite>.

ecommerce_events.name: Hong Kong eCommerce Summit (estimated annual event)
Web Summit Asia (if held in HK in given year)

weather.conditions: Currently warm and humid (late May). <cite index="48-3">The current temperature in Hong Kong Island is 30°C, with a feels-like temperature of 40°C</cite>.

options.name: Victoria Park Swimming Pool or Causeway Bay Swimming Pool
options.quality: Clean, well-maintained, affordable
options.transit_from_center: MTR to Causeway Bay or Victoria Park station, 5–10 min walk; day entry ~€2

options.name: Repulse Bay or Deep Water Bay
options.quality: Sandy beaches, lifeguards, safe netting
options.transit_from_center: Minibus or taxi from Central, 20–30 minutes. Best May–October (warmest). Avoid June–September (pollution risk).

options.name: Hotel gym/pool access
options.quality: Available at some serviced apartments and hotels

options.name: Pure Fitness (Central or Admiralty branch)
options.facilities: Weights, cardio, group fitness studios, yoga rooms, personal training, pool (some locations)
options.notes: <cite index="28-18,28-19">Pure Fitness is the dominant premium gym brand in Hong Kong, with locations in Central, Admiralty, Causeway Bay, Quarry Bay, Langham Place, and elsewhere, offering spacious facilities with top-of-the-line equipment, group fitness studios, and yoga rooms</cite>.

options.name: Government Sports Centre (e.g., Causeway Bay or Wan Chai)
options.facilities: Basic weights, cardio machines, indoor courts
options.notes: <cite index="28-16">Government-run fitness centres charge as little as HK$17 per entry</cite>. Very affordable, local atmosphere.

options.name: Anytime Fitness (various branches)
options.notes: 24-hour access available. Good for flexible travel schedules. Day pass ~HK$100.

gym.running_options: <cite index="28-2,28-20">Groups like Hong Kong Trail Runners, Wan Chai Warriors, and various meetup.com groups are free and a great way to meet people</cite>. Popular routes: Victoria Park (loop), Bowen Road (scenic), Hong Kong Trail segments.
gym.yoga_pilates: <cite index="22-8,22-9">Fitness club membership costs just $72.41 USD monthly, with many areas having parks, hiking trails, and outdoor recreational facilities</cite>. Studios in Central and Causeway Bay offer English-language yoga classes (€12–18/class).

day_trips.hiking: <cite index="23-4">Despite its density, there are quiet parks and great hiking trails if you plan ahead</cite>. Popular: Hong Kong Trail (sections 1–3 from Victoria Peak, accessible via tram), Lantau Peak, Dragon's Back trail. MTR + minibus to trailheads; most within 30–45 minutes from Central. Avoid May–September (heat, humidity).

marinas.name: Aberdeen Marina Club
marinas.transit_from_center: Minibus or taxi from Central, 20–25 minutes; minibus 7 or 70 from Central

marinas.name: Sai Kung Yacht Club
marinas.transit_from_center: MTR to Choi Hung, then minibus 92, 94, or 96, ~45 minutes total

sailing.best_season: October–April (cooler, calmer seas, less typhoon risk). Avoid May–September (typhoons, high winds).
sailing.notes: Wind conditions best in winter (November–February). Regatta season varies; check with clubs. Scene is active with expat and local sailors; English widely spoken.

new_hobbies.venue: Flagstaff House Pottery Studio or Independent pottery studios in Sheung Wan
new_hobbies.notes: Hong Kong has a thriving ceramics scene, especially in Sheung Wan's creative enclaves. Classes fill quickly; book ahead.

new_hobbies.venue: Cheung Sha Beach (Lantau Island)
new_hobbies.notes: Best November–April (stronger, more stable winds). Schools operate from Cheung Sha Long Beach. Avoid summer (typhoon season).

new_hobbies.venue: Hong Kong Photography Meetup or independent tours (e.g., via Meetup.com or Airbnb Experiences)
new_hobbies.neighborhood: Various (Sheung Wan, Victoria Peak, Tai Hang)
new_hobbies.notes: Strong local photography community. Golden hour at Victoria Peak or Tsim Sha Tsui waterfront is iconic. Urban architecture and street photography abundant.

new_hobbies.venue: Local freediving schools (e.g., in Sai Kung or Lamma Island)
new_hobbies.notes: Hong Kong has warm waters (October–April ideal). Certification courses run 3–4 days. Excellent viz in summer, but avoid typhoon season.

new_hobbies.venue: Local maker spaces or private workshops (emerging scene in Kowloon East)
new_hobbies.notes: Growing DIY/maker culture in converted industrial areas. Classes typically 2–3 hours; materials extra.

coworking_retreats.notes: No curated coworking retreats (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, NomadX style) identified operating in Hong Kong as of May 2026. The city lacks the 'retreat center' model due to high real estate costs and compact geography. However, WeWork and Garage Society host occasional networking events and workshops for members. Consider mainland SE Asia alternatives (Bali, Chiang Mai, Da Nang) if group retreat experience is priority.

car_free.walkability: <cite index="23-3">Transport is excellent and inexpensive, making it easy to get around</cite>. Causeway Bay, Central, Sheung Wan, Admiralty are highly walkable (flat or tram-accessible). Some neighborhoods (Quarry Bay, Wong Chuk Hang) involve hills; escalators and elevators help.
car_free.public_transport: <cite index="23-3">Transport is excellent and inexpensive</cite>. MTR (subway) is fast, clean, reliable. Octopus card (~€20 deposit, preload €50 credit) covers MTR, buses, ferries, trams. <cite index="12-26">A 5-minute MTR ride to Central and 10-minute taxi ride to Airport Express Hong Kong Station</cite>. Monthly unlimited travel pass ~€45–50.
car_free.airport_to_center: <cite index="12-23">Hong Kong International Airport is 36 km away</cite>. Airport Express train: 24 min to Central (€11.5), departures every 10–15 min. Taxi: ~€35–45, 45–60 min depending on traffic.

startup_community.scene_summary: Hong Kong hosts a mature fintech, venture capital, and logistics tech scene rather than a cutting-edge AI/indie-hacker hub. The city attracts corporate tech talent and multinational R&D but lacks the grassroots indie-builder energy of Berlin or Amsterdam. Founder meetups exist but are smaller and more formal. <cite index="24-9,24-10,24-11">Check Meetup Hong Kong and Eventbrite Hong Kong for groups and events—coding, start-ups, entrepreneurs, vegans, films, puppetry—whatever you are into, HK most likely has a networking group</cite>.

key_venues.name: The Hive (multiple locations)
key_venues.what: Premier coworking network with 23+ spaces across Asia Pacific; strong community events and founder meetups.

key_venues.name: Impact Hub Hong Kong
key_venues.what: Social innovation and sustainability-focused. Regular workshops, pitching events.

key_venues.name: Garage Society (Sheung Wan, Causeway Bay, others)
key_venues.what: Curated events, networking, creative professionals. Monthly founder socials.

startup_community.recurring_events: Startup networking nights (Eventbrite, Meetup.com, IndieHackers Hong Kong). Regular demo days hosted by venture funds. AI/ML meetups emerging but still niche compared to SV or Berlin.

bonus.flight_from_vie: Direct flights: ~13 hours on Cathay Pacific, Malaysia Airlines, or other carriers. Round-trip economy ~€600–850. Connecting flights (via Bangkok, Singapore) add 3–5 hours but can cost €100–200 less.
bonus.classical_music: Hong Kong Philharmonic Orchestra performs regularly at Hong Kong Cultural Centre (Tsim Sha Tsui) and AsiaWorld Expo. Season typically September–June. Western classical concerts occasional; traditional Chinese music more prominent. Ticket €25–100 depending on performer.
bonus.dance_scene: Bachata and salsa: modest but active. Regular dance socials at clubs in Lan Kwai Fong (Central) and Tsim Sha Tsui. Studios in Causeway Bay and Central offer salsa/bachata classes (€12–20/class). Latin nights Fri–Sat at bars like Dragon-i (Central). Community smaller than Barcelona or Miami but present.

concerns: €2,000 budget is tight for 7 days (accommodation alone €770–1,022; coworking + food ~€800–1,000; leaves minimal buffer). Recommend booking accommodation in advance and choosing 1–2 premium coworking days + budget cafes for remaining sessions.
May–September: High heat, humidity (feels like 35–40°C with AC dependence), typhoon risk, and seasonal air quality dips from mainland China. October–December strongly preferred.
Housing is minimal and expensive; rooms are small by Western standards. Budget serviced apartments fit but expect compact layouts.
No dedicated 'digital nomad' visa; tourist visa (90 days) works but renewal requires leaving SAR.
Limited coworking retreat scene (no Feel.travel or Outsite equivalents in HK). If group community-building is important, consider 2–3 day side trip to Bali or Da Nang.
Startup scene is corporate/venture-focused rather than indie-hacker friendly; fewer informal demo nights or early-stage founder meetups vs. tier-1 hubs.

summary: The Philippines' chaotic megacity: English everywhere, a real BPO/startup economy, cheap, but notorious traffic, pollution, and limited charm. Visa-free 30 days. A work-and-transit hub more than a lifestyle base.
remote_work_pitch: Manila's edge is English fluency and a genuine outsourcing/startup economy, at low cost, in a city that runs on global-business hours-ish. For a recon it tests the Philippines as a work base or springboard to the islands; honestly the traffic, flooding, and pollution make most nomads use it as a gateway rather than a home.
best_months: December-February (cooler, drier); avoid typhoon/wet season Jun-Oct

internet.notes: Improved fiber in Makati/BGC; older areas weaker, verify, SIM backup

summary: Ecuador's Pacific surf-and-party village: warm coast, reliable waves, a backpacker-bohemian scene, very cheap. Visa-free 90 days, USD currency. Tiny, rowdy, and basic with weak internet — a budget surf interlude rather than a work base.
remote_work_pitch: Montañita is Ecuador's surf-and-party beach village: warm Pacific waves, a backpacker-bohemian vibe, and rock-bottom costs on the dollar. For a recon it's a cheap coastal-surf interlude; it's tiny, can be rowdy at night, and the internet is basic, so treat it as async surf time, with the calmer nearby Olón as a quieter alternative.
best_months: December-May (warmer, sunnier, better surf for beginners); cooler/grey Jun-Nov

internet.notes: Weak village internet; async only, not for critical calls. SIM backup essential

coworking_retreats.notes: Surf/backpacker scene; no real coworking

car_free.walkability: Tiny walkable village; buses along the coast
car_free.public_transport: Coastal buses, taxis; walkable
car_free.airport_to_center: Guayaquil (GYE) ~2.5-3h by bus/transfer (~40 EUR)

startup_community.scene_summary: None; surf-and-party village. Come for cheap waves and sun, not work. Internet rules out serious remote work.

bonus.flight_from_vie: 2 stops (via Madrid/US hub + Guayaquil) + 3h road, ~20-24h total — remote
bonus.classical_music: None
bonus.dance_scene: Beach party scene; minimal Latin formal
