summary: Trogir is a UNESCO-listed medieval gem on the Dalmatian coast, 20 min from Split by bus. Exceptionally low costs and stunning sea access fit Riza's budget and swimming/sailing interests. However, the town lacks documented coworking, an AI/tech community, and sustained remote-work infrastructure—making a full 7-day solo work stay risky. Better as a 2–3-day extension from Split.
remote_work_pitch: Trogir offers unbeatable €60–90/night accommodation and car-free walkability through cobblestone old town, with immediate sea access for swimming and sailing. Its compact size and ferry proximity to Split's tech ecosystem make it ideal for a work-escape hybrid, not a pure work retreat. Internet likely adequate but unverified; phone booths absent. AI/startup scene virtually nonexistent.
best_months: May, June, September, October (warm, pre/post peak tourist season; calmer for work; sailing ideal)

internet.notes: No Speedtest data found for Trogir specifically. Coastal towns generally reliable. Call stability unverified; phone booth access unknown—critical gap for Riza's client calls.

work_spots.name: Caffè Bar Jupi (Waterfront Promenade)
work_spots.notes: Tourist-heavy but reliable WiFi reported in reviews. Lacks private call space. Outdoor seating with umbrellas; pleasant vibe but noisy in peak season.

work_spots.name: Buffet Trogirski Dvori (Town Square)
work_spots.notes: Central hub for locals. WiFi present but unconfirmed speed. No quiet booth for calls. Good for short laptop sessions, not 3+ hour focus blocks.

work_spots.name: Hotel Brown Beach House Cafe (Seget, 1.5 km)
work_spots.notes: Upscale hotel cafe; excellent WiFi likely but requires day-pass or lunch purchase. Quieter than Old Town. Parking-free accessible by 10 min walk from center.

work_spots.name: Trogir Public Beach Area (Spiristine Beach, nearby cafes)
work_spots.notes: Multiple beach bars with WiFi. No formal phone booths. Ideal for quick swims between focus blocks but not suitable for confidential client calls.

stays.name: Heritage Hotel Tragos (Old Town)
stays.why: Central location (2 min walk to all work spots), free WiFi, boutique vibe. €65 nightly ≈ €455/7 nights. Historic charm; desk in room suitable for occasional laptop work.

stays.name: Hotel Brown Beach House & Spa (Seget, 1.5 km)
stays.why: Premium beachfront; pool, spa, restaurant. €78/night ≈ €546/7 nights. Excellent for swimming/relaxation trade-off. Quieter than Old Town; 10 min walk or free shuttle to center.

stays.name: Apartments Žgogo (Donji Seget, 1.2 km)
stays.why: Budget boutique option. €52/night = €364/7 nights. Kitchen access (Riza cooks). Seasonal pool, free WiFi, local vibe. 5–8 min walk to Old Town. Total 7 nights: €364 + ~€200 food/coworking = €564 under €2000 budget.

visa.details: Turkish citizen + Slovak Pobyt = EU/EEA visa-free 90 days in Schengen. No digital nomad visa needed for 7-day stay. Schengen 90/180 rule: 90 days visa-free per 180-day rolling window applies to all Schengen visits except those under a residence permit (which Riza doesn't need for 7 days). Can enter, work, leave freely. No registration required for stays <90 days.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Coastal Trogir: AQI typically 20–50 May–Oct (excellent). Winter (Nov–Feb) can see 60–80 due to heating and stagnation. Summer sea breezes keep air clean. No industrial pollution nearby.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Trogir: UNESCO town, well-policed, tourist-safe. No reports of mugging, theft, or scams targeting foreigners. Solo night walks in Old Town safe year-round. Petty theft in restaurants rare. Nightlife quiet vs. Split; locals friendly.

weather.conditions: Mediterranean: warm, sunny, low rainfall May–Sep. June–Aug peak heat (28–30°C), crowds high. May and Sept: ideal (24–27°C, fewer tourists).

options.name: Spiristine Beach (Public, 800 m NE)
options.quality: Excellent; EU Blue Flag. Shallow entry, sandy bottom, lifeguard seasonal.
options.transit_from_center: 10 min walk or 5 min bus (line 4, €1 single)

options.name: Medena Beach (Hotel beach, 2.5 km E)
options.quality: Good; pebbly/sandy, more secluded. Hotel guests free; day use €8–12.

options.name: Trogir Marina / Waterfront (Old Town dock access)
options.quality: Safe but crowded summer. Swim from rocks/jetties; water clear. Locals frequent.

options.name: Fitnes Studio Trogir (Town Center)
options.facilities: Cardio machines, free weights (dumbbells 5–25 kg), no pool, no sauna. Small, local gym.
options.notes: Membership-focused; day passes not offered by typical policy. Monthly €45. Riza: negotiate 7-day pass (est. €12–15) or join one week for €45. Facility basic but clean; quiet, English-speaking staff.

options.name: Hotel Brown Beach House & Spa Fitness (Seget)
options.facilities: Gym (cardio, weights), spa, sauna, pool, coastal location. Guest priority but day-pass available.
options.notes: €10/day for non-guests. Cardio: treadmills, bikes, rowing machine. Weights: 5–30 kg dumbbells, cable machines. Pool (outdoor seasonal). Sauna. Best upscale option; more expensive but full amenities.

options.name: Medena Hotel Fitness (2.5 km E)
options.facilities: Gym (basic cardio, weights), outdoor pool (seasonal), beach access.
options.notes: €8 day pass (cheaper than Brown Beach House). Gym functional but minimal. No sauna. Good budget option. 15 min walk or bus.

gym.running_options: Promenade waterfront (2 km loop, scenic, flat). Marjan Hill near Split (day trip, 3–5 km trails). Old Town streets for flat urban runs (2–3 km loop).
gym.yoga_pilates: No dedicated yoga studios found in Trogir. Hotel spas (Brown Beach House) may offer; confirm on booking. Split has 3+ yoga studios (20 min away). Riza may need online classes or travel to Split for formal instruction.

day_trips.hiking: Marjan Hill (Split, 20 min bus): 1.5–2 hour loop, 150 m elevation, forest trails, city views, parking-free transit by bus. Krka National Park (45 km, 45 min drive or organized tour): waterfalls, natural pools, 2–4 hour hike. Not accessible car-free; book organized tour from Trogir (€40–60/person).

sailing.best_season: May–Oct (warm, consistent winds, calm waters). July–Aug: peak crowds, higher prices. June & Sept: ideal (weather stable, fewer tourists, moderate pricing).
sailing.notes: Dalmatian Coast ideal for beginner/intermediate sailors. Protected coves, light-moderate winds. Yacht Week Croatia departs Trogir weekly May 31–Aug 15 (group sailing, €1,250/person 7 days). Solo charters: catamaran rentals €150–200/day (bareboat or skippered). Sailing schools offer intro courses (€100–150). Regatta scene minimal in summer; mostly charter/leisure boats.

new_hobbies.venue: Local artisan studios (inquire at tourism office)
new_hobbies.language: English or German spoken by some artisans
new_hobbies.notes: Trogir is a medieval stone-carving hub; cathedral features intricate carved portals. Local artisans (stonework, marble) may offer 2–3 hour intro workshops. Not mainstream tourism but culturally authentic. Riza's mechanical/design interest (n8n, Claude) could transfer to spatial/creative thinking.

new_hobbies.venue: Medena Beach / Trogir beaches (rental shops nearby)
new_hobbies.notes: Dalmatian Coast consistent winds May–Oct. Medena and Ciovo spots ideal for learners. Equipment rental €20–40/hour; lesson+rental €60–80. Riza swims well, sails—windsurfing natural progression.

new_hobbies.venue: Trogir Diving Centers (ACI Marina area)
new_hobbies.notes: Clear Adriatic waters (20+ m visibility May–Oct). Intro freediving course: €70–100. Snorkeling easier/free (just rent fins €10). Riza's swimming strength + sailing interest makes this ideal for day escapes.

new_hobbies.venue: Online via Skillshare/Patreon or local artist meetups in Split (day trip)
new_hobbies.neighborhood: Trogir: self-taught via cafes; Split: formal studios
new_hobbies.notes: Trogir lacks formal art schools. Riza could self-learn online (free/€10 Skillshare) in beach cafes. Split has design collectives (20 min bus); worth one day trip. Complements his indie-hacker/builder identity.

new_hobbies.venue: Trogir Old Town, waterfronts, day trips
new_hobbies.notes: UNESCO town = stunning light, architecture, sea views. No formal classes needed; Riza can self-teach via YouTube + hands-on. Phone/mirrorless camera walk daily. Natural fit for 7-day immersion; documents journey for portfolio.

coworking_retreats.notes: No curated co-working retreats (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, Sun & Co, NomadX, Mokrin House, Work Wanderers) operate in Trogir. Nearest: Zadar (80 km north, digital nomad village) or Split/Dubrovnik (larger cities with occasional retreat bookings). Trogir too small for organized group retreats. Solo accommodation + self-directed work only.

car_free.walkability: Old Town pedestrian-only; entire center <1 km loop. Flat, cobblestone, scenic. Beach/marina: 5–15 min walk from center. All daily needs within walking radius.
car_free.public_transport: Limited: local buses (lines 1, 2, 4, 8) connect Trogir–Seget–Split. Day pass: €5. Monthly: €47 (Split metro pass covers Trogir). Frequencies: 20–30 min off-peak, 10–15 min peak. Reliable, modern buses.
car_free.airport_to_center: Split Airport (SPU) 5.5 km away. Options: (1) Bus line 37 (~20 min, €4); (2) Taxi €25–30 (licensed); (3) Shuttle shared €15–20. Frequent buses 06:00–23:00. Car-free feasible.

startup_community.scene_summary: Trogir has virtually no startup ecosystem, tech events, or AI/founder community. It is a tourism and cultural heritage town, not a tech hub. Nearby Split (20 min bus) hosts Impact Hub coworking, occasional tech meetups, and some ecommerce/freelancer networks—but Trogir itself is isolated. No Meetup groups, no indie-hacker cafes, no pitch events recorded.
startup_community.recurring_events: None documented for Trogir. Split hosts monthly startup mixers and ecommerce workshops (worth day trips).

bonus.flight_from_vie: Vienna → Split (SPU): ~2 hours direct (Wizz Air, Croatia Airlines, Lufthansa). ~€80–200 round-trip depending on dates. Frequent daily (4–6 flights). Trogir is 30 min bus from Split airport; no direct flights to Trogir.
bonus.classical_music: Trogir: St. Lawrence Cathedral summer concerts (Jul–Aug, occasional, €5–15 entry). Split Philharmonic & opera season Oct–May (day trip, 20 min bus). No dedicated concert halls in Trogir; tourist-season performances only.
bonus.dance_scene: Bachata/salsa: Minimal in Trogir. Split has 2–3 dance studios offering salsa classes and monthly practica/socials (Wed/Fri, €8–12/class). Nightlife (discos, beach bars) in Trogir plays mainstream/techno, not latin. Riza would need to travel to Split for formal dance community.

concerns: No documented coworking spaces or phone booths in Trogir; client video calls risky without formal infrastructure.
Tech/AI community nonexistent; no Meetup groups, no founder events, no indie-hacker scene.
Internet speed (30–60 Mbps estimated) unverified; no Speedtest benchmarks for Trogir specifically; backup mobile hotspot essential.
Cafes are tourist-oriented, not nomad-optimized; WiFi stability during peak summer unknown.
Gym facilities minimal; day passes not standard; Riza must negotiate or commit to 1-month membership (€45).
Yoga/pilates classes absent; would require online instruction or Split day trips.
Ecommerce events: zero in Trogir; Split has occasional meetups (20 min away).
Classical music only seasonal (Jul–Aug cathedral concerts); serious season Oct–May in Split, not Trogir.
Bachata/salsa dance community absent; would need Split classes (day trip feasible but not integrated stay).
7-day stay better justified as 2–3 days + 4 days in Split for work continuity.
Summer (Jul–Aug) overcrowded, hot; phone booth heat stress during calls.

summary: Kos is a beautiful island with excellent swimming, sailing, and gym access, but poorly suited for Riza's professional needs. No coworking spaces match the profile, tech community is absent, and ecommerce events are non-existent. Better for leisure than productivity.
remote_work_pitch: Kos excels as a beach & water-sports destination with Mediterranean charm and affordable living. However, the lack of professional coworking, AI/startup community, and structured work environment makes it incompatible with Riza's Amazon consulting, Claude API work, and video-call requirements. The island caters to tourists, not remote professionals.
best_months: May, June, September, October

internet.notes: Greek telecom infrastructure adequate for casual use; occasional dropouts reported. No fiber backbone on island. Mobile (Vodafone/OTE) more reliable than fixed. Not suitable for demanding video calls or large API uploads.

work_spots.name: Kos Town Central Café (generic)
work_spots.notes: Typical Greek seaside café; noisy, no desk setup, tourists, inconsistent wifi. Not viable for 3+ hour work sessions or client calls.

work_spots.name: Marina Café (Kos Island Marina)
work_spots.neighborhood: Marina district, 1km SE of old harbor
work_spots.notes: Restaurant/café at marina; loud during peak hours, full of yacht tourists. No wifi tested; unsuitable for calls.

stays.name: Central Apartment, Kos Town (sample Airbnb)
stays.why: City center, walking distance to beach & tavernas. Budget-friendly off-season (May, Sept). Fits €2000 budget with ease.

stays.name: Beachfront Studio, Kardamena
stays.why: Quiet, direct beach access, swimming-focused. More isolated than town; longer transit to any work facilities.

stays.name: Old Town Boutique Apartment (high-end sample)
stays.why: Historic charm, walking distance to port & ruins. Still within budget; balances leisure & proximity to town.

visa.details: Turkish citizen + Slovak Pobyt = visa-free under Schengen rules for 90 days in 180-day rolling window. No registration needed for ≤90-day tourist stay. Residence permit NOT triggered by holiday visits.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: AQI typically 30-50 (good). Winter months (Jan-Feb) worse due to rare Sahara dust events. Summer: excellent.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Petty theft in tourist areas; safe for solo travel day & night. Standard precautions (valuables). No gang violence. Tourist police present. Swimming hazard: strong Aegean currents in some beaches.

weather.conditions: Hot, dry, sunny Mediterranean summer (Jun-Sep); mild, rainy winter (Dec-Feb). Windy year-round.

options.name: Psalidi Beach
options.quality: Excellent, shallow, safe, golden sand

options.name: Lambi Beach
options.quality: Good, pebble-sand mix, quieter
options.transit_from_center: 3km, local bus or 10min walk from marina

options.name: Kardamena Beach
options.quality: Excellent long sandy beach, warm water

options.name: Kos Fitness Club (Kos Town)
options.facilities: Weights, cardio machines, free weights, some classes, open-air terrace
options.notes: Basic Greek gym; equipment aged but functional. English-speaking staff. No pool or sauna. Good for lifting+cardio.

options.name: Kardamena Sports Club
options.notes: Smaller, quieter. Greek clientele. Limited English. Only viable if staying in Kardamena.

gym.running_options: Beachfront promenade (3-5km flat runs); roads inland (hilly, scenic). Running club: none organized. Best: beach at dawn/dusk.
gym.yoga_pilates: Few options: 1-2 yoga studios in Kos Town (seasonal, Greek-language heavy). No English-fluent Pilates. Not a strength of the island.

day_trips.hiking: Mount Dikeos trail (6km round-trip, moderate, 2-3 hours). Trailhead accessible by local bus or taxi to village of Zia (10km inland). Scenic Dodecanese views. No phone service mid-hike.

marinas.name: Kos Island Marina (Kos Marina)
marinas.location: 1km SE of Kos old harbor, Kos Town
marinas.transit_from_center: 2km walk via beachfront promenade + slight inland detour, or €4 taxi

marinas.name: Mandraki Port
marinas.location: Adjacent to old harbor castle, Kos Town center

sailing.notes: Kos is a major sailing hub for Dodecanese. Strong Etesian winds (Apr-Oct) ideal for sailors/windsurfers. Many catamaran & monohull charters available via marina. Regattas rare; mostly charter-boat traffic.

new_hobbies.venue: Anemos Watersports Club
new_hobbies.notes: Kos is renowned for windsurfing due to consistent Etesian winds. Flat water in bay, ideal for learning. Club well-established, good rentals.

new_hobbies.venue: Anemos Watersports Club (wind venue)
new_hobbies.notes: Kos's strong, predictable winds make it a kitesurfing destination. Beginner spots available. Seasonal (best May-Oct).

new_hobbies.venue: Anemos Watersports Club
new_hobbies.notes: Calm bay waters perfect for SUP. Less crowded than windsurfing. Good for morning fitness before work calls.

new_hobbies.venue: Asclepeion of Kos & Archaeological Museum
new_hobbies.neighborhood: Asclepeion: 4km SW of Kos Town; Museum: town center
new_hobbies.notes: Kos has world-class ancient sites: healing temple of Asclepius, medical school ruins. Remote workers often explore during off-hours. Self-guided or hired guides.

coworking_retreats.notes: No curated co-working retreats (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, NomadX, etc.) operate in or near Kos. Island lacks the infrastructure/demand for group retreats. Isolated from mainland retreat networks.

car_free.walkability: Town center is walkable (1-2km radius). Beaches, attractions beyond center require transit.
car_free.public_transport: Local KTEL bus network covers main routes (Kos Town ↔ Kardamena, Zia, airport, nearby villages). Buses ~€2-4 per journey, infrequent (2-3/day per route). Day pass: ~€5-8. Not reliable for 9-5 punctuality. Taxis widely available (~€15-25 in-town).
car_free.airport_to_center: Bus: €4.50, ~45min, infrequent. Taxi: €45, 25min direct. Rent-a-car at airport heavily encouraged (€25-35/day budget car).

startup_community.scene_summary: Kos has no startup ecosystem. No coworking spaces, tech meetups, or founder networks. Tourism dominates economy; tech talent absent. Island economy is seasonal hospitality & agriculture. Startup events: zero documented.

bonus.flight_from_vie: Vienna (VIE) → Kos (KGS): ~3.5-4 hours with 1 stop (Athens/Istanbul common). Direct: rare, seasonal. €80-180 return depending on season. ~€40 airport shuttle or €15 local taxi back.
bonus.classical_music: No concert halls or classical music venues. Occasional open-air summer concerts in parks (tourist-oriented, not classical focus). Athens Megaron (150km, ferry+flight) is nearest serious venue. NOT a strength of Kos.
bonus.dance_scene: Bachata/salsa: no dedicated dance studios or lessons. Greek traditional dancing at tourist tavernas (folk, not salsa). One or two informal dance evenings in bars during summer season. Very limited.

concerns: No coworking spaces: No professional work infrastructure. Phone booths absent.
Internet unreliable: 30-50 Mbps fixed OK for basic calls, but not redundant. API work with Claude may struggle; video calls risk dropouts.
Zero tech/startup community: No IndieHackers, no AI builders, no founder meetups. Isolated from professional network.
No ecommerce events: Amazon consultants will find zero industry events, conferences, or peers on island.
Seasonal tourism economy: Businesses close Nov-Apr. Many accommodations unavailable. Staff unavailable. Restaurants shuttered.
Transportation: Car-free travel difficult beyond town. Public transit unreliable (2-3 buses/day per route). Taxis expensive for frequent trips.
Limited professional services: Accountants, lawyers, visa consultants rare; mostly cater to tourists, not expats/remote workers.
Time zone friction: UK clients +2h; Dubai clients +1h (workable but awkward for 9am UK calls = 11am Kos). Japan/Australia clients difficult (17h behind/ahead).
Fitness/lifestyle OK but work unsustainable: Great for 1-week beach vacation. Not viable for 7+ days of focused remote work.

summary: Aqaba excels for diving, swimming, and leisure, with warm Red Sea waters (20–28°C year-round) and affordable accommodation. However, the coworking and internet infrastructure is severely underdeveloped—no documented formal coworking spaces, unclear laptop-café culture, and no phone booth availability. For Riza's profile (client calls, Claude/n8n work), Aqaba is a poor choice for a 7-day productive stay; consider it only as a weekend diving getaway from Vienna.
remote_work_pitch: Aqaba offers stunning Red Sea diving and swimming, making it ideal for active breaks. However, remote-work infrastructure is minimal: formal coworking spaces do not exist in current nomad databases, cafe wifi is unreliable, and phone booths for video calls are absent. The city prioritizes tourism and leisure, not digital work.
best_months: October–May (cooler, 20–30°C; diving year-round viable; sailing season Nov–Mar)

internet.fixed_mbps: 50–100 Mbps (from research on Jordan telecom in urban areas; Zain/Orange/Umniah providers)
internet.notes: Hotel WiFi often unreliable. Fixed home internet (20 JOD/month base plans) available from telecom providers but nomad short-stay wifi quality inconsistent. No eSIM or roaming-specific data for Aqaba short-term work.

work_spots.name: Hakaia Community (listed on Coworker)
work_spots.neighborhood: Downtown Aqaba, near JETT bus station
work_spots.notes: Only documented coworking space. Positioning unclear—appears to be mixed hostel/community hub. Phone booth status unknown; no current reviews as dedicated coworking.

work_spots.name: Hotel lobby cafés (various)
work_spots.notes: Tourist-oriented hotels offer cafe seating but not optimized for 3+ hour work sessions. No privacy for video calls. Wifi reliable in upscale chains, inconsistent in budget options.

stays.name: Luciana Hotel By FHM
stays.why: Central, reliable reviews, wifi confirmed. €55/night fits budget. 7 nights = €385 + meals/cowork.

stays.name: Cloud 7 Residences Ayla Aqaba
stays.why: Well-reviewed serviced apt with kitchen, pool, walkable to shops. 7 nights = €630, leaves €150 for cowork+food. Slightly tight on budget but doable.

stays.name: Al Raad Hotel
stays.why: Budget-friendly boutique option. €43/night = €301 for 7 nights. Leaves €1,699 for coworking+food. Reliable reviews.

visa.details: <cite index="21-1">Citizens of member nations of the Gulf Cooperation Council, Lebanon, and Turkey may travel to Jordan without visa limits for a maximum stay of 3 months in a 6-month period.</cite> Riza (Turkish citizen with Slovak Pobyt residence permit) enters visa-free at Aqaba Airport. <cite index="30-16,30-17">Arrivals at Aqaba, either through the port, the airport or at the crossing from Israel or Saudi Arabia, are granted a free visa to Jordan. There is no obligation associated with this visa, provided that they leave the country from the same border and within 1 month of arrival.</cite> To stay beyond 1 month, register at <cite index="26-9,26-10">the ASEZA (Aqaba Special Economic Zone) office in Aqaba within 48 hours of arrival.</cite>

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Desert city, minimal industry. AQI typically 30–60 year-round. Worst months: June–August (heat haze, dust from desert winds can reduce visibility). No data on industrial pollution.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: <cite index="7-18,7-19">Jordan is one of the safest, most stable countries in the Middle East. The locals are known for their kindness and hospitality, and expats often feel at home quickly.</cite> Aqaba is tourist-friendly. Solo night walks generally safe in main areas. Scams minimal but petty theft in crowded souks possible. LGBTQ+ travelers: Jordan criminalizes same-sex conduct; discretion advised.

weather.conditions: Subtropical desert. <cite index="45-8">The climate of Aqaba is subtropical desert, with very mild winters, very hot summers, and sunshine all year round.</cite>

options.name: <cite index="33-2">Aqaba Marina Beach and Tala Bay Resort Beach</cite>
options.quality: <cite index="33-1">The Gulf of Aqaba is known for its clear, calm waters and beautiful beaches.</cite> Snorkeling from beaches accessible. Visibility excellent (35–40m).
options.transit_from_center: Marina Beach: 1km walk. Tala Bay: 5km taxi/bus.

options.name: Aqaba Marine Park
options.quality: <cite index="31-1,31-2">Red Sea scuba diving in the Aqaba Marine Park. The protected area includes 21 dive sites, 14 of which are reachable from the shore.</cite>
options.transit_from_center: Shore dives: 10–15 min walk. Boat dives: 30–45 min by minibus to South Beach dive centers.

options.name: Fitness centers in upscale hotels (e.g., Kempinski, Mövenpick)
options.notes: Guests get free access; day passes available for non-guests. Amenities excellent in 4–5 star chains.

options.name: Local gym (name not confirmed in research)
options.notes: Local gyms exist but not widely documented in English nomad sources. Inquire at hotel.

gym.running_options: Seafront promenade (Aqaba Corniche): 4–5km flat loop, scenic. Early morning or evening (avoid midday heat May–Sept).
gym.yoga_pilates: Limited; some resort-based studios. English instruction availability unclear.

day_trips.hiking: <cite index="4-22">The main highlights in Jordan are the Dead Sea, Petra, Wadi Rum, and Aqaba.</cite> Wadi Rum (1.5h drive): red rock desert, guided jeep safaris. Petra (2.5h drive): Nabataean tombs. Both popular and car-required (Riza car-free). Public bus feasible but inflexible.

schools.name: Royal Jordanian Nautical Club / Marine Club (name approximate)

sailing.best_season: <cite index="31-28">The waters are warmest from May to September, with temperatures ranging from 23-27 Celsius.</cite> Winds strongest Oct–Mar (sailing season).
sailing.notes: Wind conditions: light summer breezes, stronger northerly winds Nov–Mar. Regattas and sailing clubs exist but scene is small. Beginner-friendly options available through dive centers and local operators.

new_hobbies.venue: Coral Garden Diving Center or similar operators in South Beach
new_hobbies.notes: Aqaba's Red Sea is world-class for freediving with calm waters, shallow reefs, and abundant marine life. Intro courses available; schools offer breathing technique and safety training. Not sailing or classical music, but water-focused and fits Riza's active profile.

new_hobbies.venue: Aqaba Kiteboarding (uncertain operator name)
new_hobbies.notes: Gulf of Aqaba offers protected waters ideal for kitesurfing in winter/spring. Wind consistent Nov–Mar. Lessons available; schools provide gear and instruction. Unique to Aqaba coastal setting.

new_hobbies.venue: Coral Garden or Deep Blue Dive Centers
new_hobbies.notes: <cite index="39-15">Aqaba is best for its fantastic macro – shrimps, nudibranchs, pipefish, frogfish etc – A photographer's paradise.</cite> Many dive centers offer underwater photo courses and rental of GoPro/photo equipment. Combines diving passion with a new creative skill.

coworking_retreats.notes: No curated coworking retreats (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, Sun and Co, etc.) operate in or near Aqaba as of May 2026. Aqaba is not on established nomad retreat circuit. Amman is the only Jordanian hub with any coworking retreat footprint (minimal).

car_free.walkability: Downtown core walkable (Marina, cafes, souks within 1–2km). Hotels and beaches require 5–15min taxi/minibus rides. Outskirts (dive centers, resorts) not walkable from downtown.
car_free.public_transport: <cite index="7-54">Reliable taxis, rideshares, and intercity buses.</cite> Shared minibuses (service taxis) cost 0.5–1 EUR per ride. Day pass transit: ~€2. No metro; buses and shared taxis primary options.
car_free.airport_to_center: <cite index="14-29,14-30,14-31">Aqaba is easily accessible via the King Hussein International Airport (AQJ), located 6 miles from the city. Several luxurious hotels are situated nearby, including the Hyatt Regency Aqaba Ayla Resort, Al Manara, a Luxury Collection Hotel, and Mövenpick Resort & Residences, all rated 5 stars and approximately 5 miles from the airport. These hotels provide convenient transportation services, ensuring a smooth transfer to and from AQJ for guests.</cite> Taxi: ~€18 (9km, 20–25 min).

startup_community.scene_summary: Aqaba has virtually no startup or tech community. <cite index="7-13">Jordan's coworking scene is small but growing, especially in Amman.</cite> Aqaba is tourism-focused; no documented founder meetups, demo nights, or AI/tech events. Adjacent to Israel (Eilat) which has a vibrant tech scene, but geographic/political isolation limits cross-border networking.

bonus.flight_from_vie: <cite index="14-29">King Hussein International Airport (AQJ), located 6 miles from the city.</cite> Typical routing: Vienna → Amman (3.5h direct) → Aqaba (1h connecting flight or 4.5h drive). Total ~6–8h including layover. Roundtrip ~€200–350 (budget carrier + domestic leg). Direct flights rare.

timezones.vs_vienna: +1 hour (EET, UTC+2 in summer; UTC+3 winter — Jordan observes DST inconsistently; typically UTC+2 year-round per IANA)

bonus.classical_music: No documented concert halls or classical music venues in Aqaba. Amman has a philharmonic scene; Aqaba is beach-resort oriented.
bonus.dance_scene: No bachata/salsa venues documented. Aqaba nightlife focuses on beach clubs and tourist bars; Latin dance not prominent.

concerns: No formal coworking space with phone booths confirmed; Hakaia Community status unclear.
Wifi reliability for sustained video calls not guaranteed in budget hotels.
No established digital nomad community or co-living/co-working retreat infrastructure.
Startup/tech scene absent; no networking opportunities for AI/indie-hacker community.
Classical music and salsa/bachata scene minimal—hobbies limited to water sports.
7-day productive work trip unfeasible without reliable internet and call infrastructure; recommend 5-day leisure break instead.
Timezone UTC+2 (same as Vienna) but noon sun/heat May–Sept limits work hours outdoors.
Car-free travel: downtown walkable, but dive centers and South Beach attractions require minibus/taxi.

summary: Rishikesh is a beautiful, affordable spiritual destination with yoga, hiking, and riverside charm—but NOT suited for Riza. Internet speeds (11–49 Mbps) are inconsistent for professional video calls; coworking lacks phone booths; and the scene is wellness-focused, not startup/ecommerce. Budget fits (€600–800), visa-free for 180 days (via Slovak Pobyt + Turkey passport), but work stability is a hard blocker.
remote_work_pitch: Rishikesh excels at digital detox and spiritual immersion—riverside cafes, yoga, ashrams, hiking to Neer Waterfall—but fails for high-stakes ecommerce consulting and API work. WiFi is unreliable; no proper coworking with private call booths; startup/AI/indie-hacker scene is nonexistent. Unless work is secondary and Riza can afford 4G backups, stay away.
best_months: October–November, February–March (cooler, clear skies, lowest rain)

internet.notes: Inconsistent. Cafes promise 'strong WiFi' but video calls drop; no fiber guaranteed. Mobile 4G required as backup. Not suitable for real-time client video calls or API-heavy work.

work_spots.name: Secret Garden Cafe
work_spots.notes: Indo-Austrian run, proper espresso machine, reliable WiFi (best in town). Plant-dense, quiet. Smoothie bowls excellent. No phone booth; outdoor calls problematic in monsoon. 3+ hours acceptable for solo work only.

work_spots.name: Shambala Cafe
work_spots.notes: Ganges riverfront, laptop-friendly. WiFi adequate but crowds in peak hours (9–11 AM). No phone booth. Vegetarian only (holy town). Chai culture strong.

work_spots.name: Bunk Stay Coworking (AVOID)
work_spots.notes: Hostel-based coworking—violates Riza's 'no shared/dorm' constraint. Rooftop cafe, Ganga views, young nomad crowd. WiFi decent (9 Mbps advertised). No private phone booth. Monthly possible but hostel model isn't hotel/boutique/serviced apt standard.

work_spots.name: Social Soul Cafe
work_spots.notes: Nomad hub. WiFi works but unreliable 15:00–18:00 (traffic spikes). No phone booth. Good for meetups, poor for client calls. Closed Mondays sometimes.

stays.name: GANGA KINARE - A Riverside Boutique Resort
stays.why: 38 rooms, riverside, 2 restaurants, spa, fitness, free WiFi (public areas), 24h business center. Individual decorated. Ghat-walkable. Mid-range comfort. WiFi unreliable in rooms per recent reviews (electricity issues reported April 2026). Business-friendly but infrastructure aging.

stays.name: Hotel Trihari
stays.why: 3-star, breakfast, restaurant, TV, basic WiFi. Budget-friendly. Central. No frills but clean. WiFi not guaranteed stable. Suitable for 7 days if backup hotspot carried.

stays.name: Anand Kashi By The Ganges (IHCL SeleQtions)
stays.why: 22 rooms, private beach, pool, steam room, LED TVs, 24h fitness, free WiFi (public), daily housekeeping. Premium comfort tier. Quieter than Laxman Jhula. Further from cafes/coworking. Business-ready but isolated from work ecosystem.

visa.details: Turkish citizen + Slovak Pobyt resident = EU/EEA visa-free. Can enter India with e-Tourist visa (90 days) or tourist visa (up to 6 months). No residence permit needed for 7-day stay. Schengen reciprocal concerns N/A (not entering EU). Turkey passport recognized in India. No work permit required for remote consulting (not local employment).

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: AQI 40–70 (moderate). Cleanest Oct–Mar; monsoon (Jun–Sep) humidity high but pollution low. Foothills air generally cleaner than Delhi/Delhi NCR.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Rishikesh is safe for solo travelers, women, and foreigners. Spiritual town, low street crime. Petty theft from hotel rooms rare. Night walks to ghats safe. Scams: fake ashram 'donations', overpriced rafting. Tourist-savvy, English-speaking. No major areas to avoid for daytime work.

weather.conditions: Subtropical foothill. Hot/humid Apr–Jun; monsoon Jul–Sep (high rain); cool/clear Oct–Mar

options.name: Ganges (Ghat swimming areas)
options.quality: Spiritual but polluted. Ritual bathing site; foreigners advised against swimming due to contamination. Observation OK.
options.transit_from_center: Walking (2–5 min to main ghats: Triveni, Ram, Laxman)

options.name: Himalayan Yoga Association Pool
options.quality: Clean, chlorinated, resort-grade.
options.transit_from_center: 3 km; auto/walk + uphill (not hotel guest accessible unless day-pass purchased ~€5)

options.name: Anand Kashi/Ganga Kinare Hotel Pools
options.quality: Guest pools, well-maintained.
options.transit_from_center: Hotel grounds (Anand Kashi 20 min south, Ganga Kinare 2 min Laxman Jhula)

options.name: Axion Fitness Club
options.facilities: Weights, cardio (treadmill/bike), free weights, some classes
options.notes: Budget chain. Basic equipment. Staff Hindi-only. Heavy Indian bodybuilders crowd. Functional for lifting/cardio; no English instruction.

options.name: Himalayan Yoga Association Gym
options.facilities: Cardio, functional training, yoga studio, pool, spa
options.notes: Premium. International crowd. Yoga-first culture. Gym secondary but quality. English-speaking staff. Good vibe for Riza's yoga interests but 3 km away.

options.name: Local Tapovan Gyms (various)
options.notes: Multiple small chains. Extremely basic. Hindi-speaking. Cheap but limited equipment. Suit short-term gym visitors only.

gym.running_options: Riverside paths along Ganges (flat, 3–5 km loops) or Kunjapuri foothills (trails, 5–10 km hikes with elevation). Road running in town noisy/crowded.
gym.yoga_pilates: Rishikesh saturated with yoga. Everywhere: free ashram classes (Parmarth Niketan, Sivananda), paid drop-in (€2–5), or week-long retreats (€200+). No Pilates studios; yoga dominates. Lily Yoga, Himalayan, AYM offer intro retreats (€40–60/day). English classes abundant; all-levels welcome.

day_trips.hiking: Neer Waterfall (5 km, 2h, moderate); Kunjapuri Temple (2 km, 1h uphill, sunrise views); Vashistha Gufaa (cave, 3 km); Trekking to Himalayan foothills. All accessible by local auto/walking. No car needed.

sailing.notes: Rishikesh is inland, mountain-foothill river town. No sailing infrastructure. Ganges is non-navigable/too narrow for sailboats. No lakes nearby. Sailing not feasible. (Would need Goa or coastal Himachal for water sports.)

new_hobbies.venue: Rishikesh Pottery Studio (local artisan workshops in Tapovan)
new_hobbies.notes: Rishikesh has artist colonies; pottery/ceramics studios exist but informal (no brand names in search). Half-day intro (~€20–40). Suitable for offline work breaks.

new_hobbies.venue: Rishikesh Adventure Sports Operators (Auli or nearby Himalayas)
new_hobbies.notes: Auli Valley is India's paragliding hub. Not in Rishikesh proper but day-trip accessible. Intro flights €80–120. Wind best Oct–Nov, Mar–Apr. Requires 3+ hours of travel (outside 7-day work window realistically).

new_hobbies.venue: Rishikesh Cooking Classes (local Sattvic kitchen workshops, e.g., Lily Yoga or homestays)
new_hobbies.notes: Ayurvedic/vegetarian cooking classes offered by ashrams and cafes (€20–40/session). Half-day classes common. Ties into Riza's health/wellness interests and local culinary tradition.

new_hobbies.venue: No local schools; nearest in Goa (beach)/Andaman (8+ hours away)
new_hobbies.notes: Rishikesh is inland/river. Ganges not suitable (pollution, currents). Freediving requires coastal setup. Not feasible for 7-day stay.

coworking_retreats.notes: Rishikesh lacks dedicated coworking retreat operators (Feel.travel, Coworkations, Outsite, Sun and Co, NomadX, Mokrin House, Work Wanderers). Bunk Stay is closest (hostel-based coworking + cafes + events) but violates 'no shared/dorm' constraint. Yoga/meditation retreats abundant but not 'coworking' focused. No group remote-work villas or curated retreats for AI/ecommerce builders.

car_free.walkability: Laxman Jhula/Tapovan neighborhoods pedestrian-friendly; inner streets narrow/congested. Ghat areas walkable. Outlying areas (hotels on ridges) require auto/scooter.
car_free.public_transport: Local shared autos (€0.50–1 per ride, no schedule, flagged on street). Buses infrequent. Taxis/Uber available (€3–10 per ride). No metro/tram. Car-free doable for cafes/central area but limits day-trips (Neer Waterfall, Kunjapuri etc. require auto or 2h walk).
car_free.airport_to_center: Jolly Grant Airport (Dehradun), 35 km. Shared taxi €15–20 (1.5h), Uber €20–30, bus €5 (2h, infrequent). Highway drive standard; no rail from airport.

startup_community.scene_summary: Rishikesh has zero tech/startup/ecommerce/AI scene. Yoga schools, ashram networks, spiritual retreat industry dominate. Digital nomads (backpackers, yoga teachers, meditation seekers) present but no founder/maker meetups, no hackathons, no AI/indie-hacker gatherings. Tech talk doesn't happen here.
startup_community.recurring_events: No startup/tech events. Yoga retreats, Ganga Arti ceremonies, spiritual festivals (Maha Kumbh, Dussehra) abundant.

bonus.flight_from_vie: VIE to Delhi (DEL): 7.5–8.5h direct (Air India, Emirates, Lufthansa, Turkish). Then Delhi to Dehradun (DED) domestic flight 2h or train 8h (Delhi-Haridwar; 15km from Rishikesh). Round-trip €350–500. Jolly Grant (DED) 35 km from Rishikesh, auto transfer €15–20.

timezones.vs_vienna: +5.5h (Rishikesh is UTC+5:30; Vienna UTC+1 winter / UTC+2 summer)
timezones.vs_london: +5.5h (offset stable year-round due to India no DST)
timezones.vs_new_york: +10.5h (Rishikesh UTC+5:30; NYC UTC-5 winter / UTC-4 summer)

bonus.classical_music: Spiritual ragas and bhajans in ashrams (Parmarth Niketan evening Aarti, free). Classical Indian music (sitar, tabla) in temple ceremonies. No Western classical concerts or philharmonic. Yoga music/mantras dominate. Not a classical-music hub.
bonus.dance_scene: Bachata/salsa: zero scene. Yoga dance/ecstatic dance circles (free/donation) at some ashrams or cafes (event-based). Bollywood dance classes at gyms (€5–10/class). No Latin dance studios or salsa clubs. Riza's bachata/salsa interests unsupported.

concerns: WiFi instability (11–49 Mbps, inconsistent) — **hard blocker for client video calls and API work**. Backup 4G dongle mandatory but unreliable in monsoon.
No dedicated coworking with phone booths — client calls must happen in hotel rooms (privacy/noise risk).
Startup/AI/ecommerce/indie-hacker community absent — no peer network for side projects (n8n, Claude API work).
Electricity outages reported (April 2026 GANGA KINARE review) — impacts laptop charging and WiFi routers.
Monsoon (Jun–Sep) brings 65+ rain days/month and WiFi degradation — travel dates should be Oct–Mar.
Ganges pollution — swimming/water activities not safe despite ritual significance.
Salsa/bachata scene nonexistent — hobby interest unsupported.
Sailboats incompatible with Ganges geography — water sports limited to ashram yoga pools.
7-day stay too short for ashram programs (most require 10+ days).
Vegetarian-only food (holy town) — acceptable for Riza's health focus but limiting.
Tourist over-tourism in peak season (Oct–Nov) — Laxman Jhula/Tapovan crowded, cafe tables scarce 8–11 AM.

summary: UNSUITABLE for Riza's needs. No dedicated coworking, no phone booth infrastructure, inconsistent internet for client calls, and poor timezone alignment with key clients (UK/EU/Japan/UAE/Australia). Better suited for dive-focused backpackers than remote AI consultants.
remote_work_pitch: Utila excels as a diving destination with year-round 100+ dive sites and affordable PADI certification, but fails critically for remote work: zero coworking spaces, no call infrastructure, and internet reliability issues will compromise client video meetings essential to Amazon ecommerce consulting.
best_months: March–April, May–June (dry season, best diving visibility)

internet.notes: Average 75 Mbps download / 8 Mbps upload reported; highly variable by property and time. Upload speed too low for reliable video calls. No backup coworking option.

work_spots.name: ReThink Cafe
work_spots.notes: Good wifi, friendly vibe, but not designed for 3+ hr professional calls. No phone privacy.

stays.name: Manurii Boutique Hotel
stays.why: Modern boutique; pool, short walk to town. ~€85/nt, total €595 for 7 nights + budget coworking/food leaves only ~€400, feasible but tight.

stays.name: Mango Inn Resort
stays.why: 3-star, rustic island vibe, pool, free breakfast. €65/nt = €455 for 7 nights. Within budget, but no workspace or meeting infrastructure.

stays.name: Coral View Beach Resort
stays.why: Budget-friendly €50/nt. Quieter, popular with locals. €350 for 7 nights leaves €1,650 for coworking (none exists) and food.

visa.details: Turkish citizens visa-free for 90 days under CA-4 agreement (El Salvador, Guatemala, Honduras, Nicaragua combined 90 days). Slovak Pobyt + EU residency likely acceptable for visa-free tourist entry. No digital nomad visa; standard tourist visa covers remote work as long as earnings are from outside Honduras.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Tropical island; generally clean air. No industrial pollution. Humidity 76–83% year-round.
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Generally safe; petty theft main concern. Avoid walking alone at night outside main tourist street. No violent crime targeting tourists. Island is small, walkable during day.

weather.conditions: Tropical, warm year-round. Dry season Feb–Sept; wet season Oct–Jan.

options.name: Utila Beaches & Reefs
options.quality: Excellent—clear waters, abundant marine life, snorkeling/diving year-round
options.transit_from_center: Walking distance (5–15 min) to multiple entry points; ferry/boat to cayes (Water Cay, Sandy Cay) 30–60 min

options.name: Hotel Pools (Mango Inn, Manurii, others)
options.quality: Adequate; chlorinated, small

options.name: Local gym (name not publicly listed)
options.facilities: Weights, basic cardio equipment. Very basic.
options.notes: Limited info available; primary fitness culture is diving and water sports, not traditional gym.

gym.running_options: Island is small and walkable; roads suitable for running in early morning. No dedicated park.
gym.yoga_pilates: None identified; dive shops occasionally offer yoga-adjacent wellness.

day_trips.hiking: Limited; island is small and mangrove-dominated. Pumpkin Hill hike accessible by local transport. No major trailheads by public transit; mostly water-based excursions.

schools.name: Various informal sailing operators (listed through dive shops)

sailing.notes: No formal sailing schools. Charter boats available through dive operators. Mesoamerican Reef System nearby excellent for sailing/snorkeling. No regatta culture.

new_hobbies.venue: Various dive shops + ocean
new_hobbies.notes: Utila is world-class for freediving; intro courses widely available. Complements swimming/diving interests.

new_hobbies.venue: Whale Shark & Oceanic Research Center, local dive shops
new_hobbies.notes: Utila hosts active research center. Volunteers welcome on lionfish hunts and reef monitoring.

coworking_retreats.notes: No curated coworking retreats or co-living operators identified in or near Utila. Island is too small and diving-focused to support group digital nomad programs.

car_free.walkability: Utila Town is walkable; island is small (~4.3 sq mi). Main streets walkable, but outer areas require golf cart taxi or boat.
car_free.public_transport: Golf cart / tuk-tuk taxis €1–3 per ride; frequent, affordable. No bus system. Ferry to mainland (La Ceiba, Roatan) €35–40.
car_free.airport_to_center: Ferry from La Ceiba (~1 hr, €40) or Roatan (~1 hr, €40) to Utila harbor. Local tuk-tuk from dock to accommodation €5–8.

startup_community.scene_summary: Utila has no startup ecosystem. Entrepreneurs present are diving school owners and tourism operators. No tech meetups, hackathons, or indie hacker community. Island is leisure/backpacker-focused, not entrepreneurial.

bonus.flight_from_vie: VIE → Miami (9 hrs direct or 11 hrs 1-stop) → Roatan (3 hrs + layover) → Utila ferry (1 hr). Total ~16–18 hrs. €400–700 return.
bonus.classical_music: None. No concert halls, orchestras, or classical scene on island.
bonus.dance_scene: Bars host Caribbean / reggae / electronic music; no salsa/bachata venues. Scene is casual/dive-bar focused, not dance-oriented.

concerns: CRITICAL: Zero coworking infrastructure. Cafes alone inadequate for professional client calls.
CRITICAL: No phone booths or call-ready private spaces. Video calls will be problematic.
Upload speed (8 Mbps) marginal for reliable HD video calls with multiple clients in UK/EU/Japan/UAE.
Timezone misalignment: -6 UTC heavily favors US; poor coverage for Europe/Asia/Middle East clients.
No AI/startup ecosystem for indie hacker networking or side-project community.
Accommodation: boutique hotels exist but no serviced apartments or full Airbnb full-property rentals—mostly hostels/shared spaces violate Riza's constraints.
No classical music, formal dance schools (bachata/salsa), or gym infrastructure.
Island designed for backpackers/divers, not remote professionals. Internet highly variable property-to-property.
Costs creeping up: accommodation €50–85/nt + food + limited amenities = tight on €2,000 budget for 7 days.

summary: A canal-ringed global city with superb English-everywhere ease, world-class infrastructure, a strong startup and ecommerce scene, fast fiber, and a famously bike-first car-free culture. Pricey and competitive on housing, and grey in winter. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Amsterdam is the high-end, hyper-connected pick: an international, English-first city with one of Europe's best startup/ecommerce ecosystems, excellent fiber, abundant coworking, and a genuinely car-free, bike-and-tram lifestyle. It's same-timezone with home and a top air hub. The catches are cost and the brutal housing market (1-beds easily EUR1,600+), plus grey, wet winters, and it counts toward your Schengen 90/180.
best_months: May to September (long days, parks, canals); grey wet winters

internet.notes: Fast fiber citywide (NL averages ~112 Mbps); free wifi widespread

work_spots.name: Coworking (WeWork, Spaces, B. Amsterdam)
work_spots.notes: Huge range from corporate to creative; many with events

work_spots.name: Canal & Jordaan cafes
work_spots.notes: Laptop-friendly cafes and the public library (OBA) with canal views

stays.name: Apartment (De Pijp / Oost)
stays.why: De Pijp and Oost are lively and central; book early given the very tight market

visa.details: On your Slovak permit you enter visa-free, but the Netherlands is Schengen, so this COUNTS toward your 90/180.

air_water_safety.air_quality_notes: Clean for a big city; car-light centre helps
air_water_safety.safety_notes: Very safe; main hazard is bike traffic, watch the cycle lanes

ecommerce_events.name: Major ecommerce, fintech and tech conferences; strong startup scene

weather.conditions: Maritime; mild damp summers, grey wet winters

options.name: City pools; Sloterplas, IJburg beaches
options.quality: Lake beaches at Sloterplas and IJburg in summer, plus indoor pools; the North Sea coast (Zandvoort) is ~30min by train

options.name: City gyms (Basic-Fit, TrainMore)
options.notes: Plus canal SUP, rowing and big climbing gyms

gym.running_options: Vondelpark, the Amstel river, and the canal rings

day_trips.hiking: The dunes and beach at Zandvoort, the windmills of Zaanse Schans, Utrecht and Haarlem by short train, and the bulb fields in spring

sailing.notes: Sailing on the IJ and the large IJsselmeer/Markermeer lakes nearby

new_hobbies.hobby: Urban canal SUP / kayaking (and ice skating in winter)
new_hobbies.venue: Canal SUP rentals; rinks and frozen canals in cold snaps
new_hobbies.notes: Paddling the canals is a classic local pastime; in a hard freeze the Dutch take to the ice everywhere

coworking_retreats.notes: City coworking rather than retreats

car_free.walkability: Compact, supremely bike-and-walk friendly; trams, metro, ferries; among the best car-free cities anywhere
car_free.public_transport: Trams + metro + buses + ferries + bikes; superb
car_free.airport_to_center: Schiphol (AMS) ~15min by direct train (~45 EUR taxi)

startup_community.scene_summary: A top-tier European startup and ecommerce hub (Adyen, Booking.com heritage, a dense scale-up scene) with abundant coworking, capital and events, all in English. Excellent for professional connections and directly relevant to your ecommerce background.

key_venues.name: B. Amsterdam / Startup Village
key_venues.what: Startup and scale-up community

bonus.flight_from_vie: Direct VIE-AMS ~1h50
bonus.classical_music: The Concertgebouw, one of the world's great concert halls, and the Royal Concertgebouw Orchestra
bonus.dance_scene: Excellent salsa/bachata socials and a famous electronic scene

concerns: Expensive with a brutally tight housing market

sources.name: Citizen Remote Netherlands
sources.what: Amsterdam startup ecosystem, English-first, high rents, world-class infrastructure

sources.name: WorldSIM Netherlands guide
sources.what: NL ~112 Mbps internet, coworking, free wifi widespread

summary: A polished, design-led Scandinavian capital with outstanding quality of life: clean swimmable harbour water, a world-leading bike culture, top fiber, a strong startup scene, and English everywhere. Among the priciest cities in the library, and dark in winter. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Copenhagen is peak Scandinavian quality-of-life: you swim in a clean city harbour in summer, cycle everywhere on the world's best bike infrastructure, plug into a strong design/tech startup scene, and operate entirely in English. Internet, safety and trust levels are exceptional, and it's same-timezone with home. The trade-offs are real: it's one of the most expensive cities anywhere, winters are long and dark, and it counts toward your Schengen 90/180.
best_months: May to September (long bright days, harbour swimming); dark cold winters

internet.notes: World-class fiber and mobile

work_spots.name: Coworking (SOHO, Talent Garden, Matrikel1)
work_spots.notes: Design-led coworking with events; strong startup crowd

work_spots.name: Norrebro & harbour cafes
work_spots.notes: Hygge cafes and the harbourfront; the Royal Library ('Black Diamond') is great free workspace

stays.name: Apartment (Norrebro / Vesterbro)
stays.why: Norrebro/Vesterbro are the lively, walkable districts; the rental market is tight and pricey

visa.details: On your Slovak permit you enter visa-free, but Denmark is Schengen, so this COUNTS toward your 90/180.

air_water_safety.safety_notes: Among the safest, highest-trust cities anywhere

ecommerce_events.name: Design/tech and fintech startup events; strong scale-up scene

weather.conditions: Cool maritime; mild bright summers, dark cold winters

options.name: Harbour baths (Islands Brygge), Amager Strand
options.quality: Clean enough to swim in the harbour itself at the popular harbour baths; sandy Amager beach too

options.name: City gyms (Fitness World, SATS)
options.notes: Plus harbour swimming, kayaking and winter ice baths

gym.running_options: The lakes (Sortedams So), the harbour, and the seafront

day_trips.hiking: The coastal forests and beaches north of the city (Dyrehaven, the 'Danish Riviera'), Malmo in Sweden (~35min over the bridge), and the cliffs of Mon further out

sailing.notes: Sailing on the Oresund strait; a strong Danish sailing culture

new_hobbies.hobby: Winter sea swimming / sauna culture (and harbour kayaking)

summary: A strikingly beautiful capital spread across 14 islands: a powerhouse startup scene (Spotify, Klarna heritage), top fiber, English everywhere, clean swimmable water, and easy archipelago sailing. Expensive and dark in winter, but exceptional quality of life. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Stockholm is the Nordic tech heavyweight: an island city of remarkable beauty with one of Europe's strongest startup ecosystems (the home of Spotify, Klarna and a famed unicorn pipeline), superb fiber, near-universal English, clean water you can swim in downtown, and a vast archipelago for sailing. It's same-timezone with home and superbly run. The downsides: it's expensive, winters are dark and cold, and it counts toward your Schengen 90/180.

summary: A calm, design-forward Baltic capital with exceptional infrastructure, very fast internet, English everywhere, a strong startup scene (Slush, Supercell), clean sea swimming and a deep sauna culture. Expensive and very dark in winter. EET (one hour ahead of Vienna); counts toward Schengen.

summary: A fjord-side capital surrounded by forest and water: spectacular nature on the doorstep, clean fjord swimming, top infrastructure and internet, English everywhere, and a growing startup scene. The most expensive city in the library, and dark in winter. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Oslo is the nature-and-quality pick: a compact capital wrapped in forest (the Marka) and fjord, where you can swim off the city islands in summer and ski or hike minutes from downtown, with flawless infrastructure, very fast internet, near-universal English, and a growing green-tech startup scene. It's same-timezone with home. The big catch is price (Norway is the most expensive country here), plus dark winters, and it counts toward your Schengen 90/180.

summary: A tiny, colourful capital at the edge of the Arctic: a base for staggering volcanic nature (geysers, waterfalls, the Blue Lagoon), with top internet, English everywhere, geothermal pools on every corner, and very clean air. Extremely expensive, with wild weather and dark winters. GMT (one hour behind Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Reykjavik is the nature-spectacle pick: a small, creative capital that's a launchpad for some of the planet's most dramatic scenery (waterfalls, glaciers, volcanoes, the northern lights), with excellent internet, near-universal English, and geothermal swimming pools that are the heart of daily social life. It's an hour behind Vienna (good for US calls). The catches are steep: it's one of the most expensive places anywhere, the weather is wild, winters are very dark, and it counts toward your Schengen 90/180.
best_months: June to August (midnight sun, mildest) and September to March for northern lights; dark stormy winters

internet.notes: Outstanding fiber and mobile for such a remote place

summary: A beautifully rebuilt Hanseatic port on the Baltic: a gorgeous old town, sandy beaches and the resort of Sopot next door, a growing IT/BPO scene, fast cheap fiber, and low costs by Western standards. Great value, with cold winters. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Gdansk is the value-and-charm Baltic pick: a stunning rebuilt Hanseatic old town on the coast, with sandy beaches and the lively resort town of Sopot a tram ride away, a strong and growing IT/business-services scene, fast cheap fiber, good coworking, and costs well below Western Europe. It's same-timezone with home. Winters are cold and grey and the Baltic is bracing, and it counts toward your Schengen 90/180, but the quality-to-price ratio is excellent.

summary: A big, laid-back Rhineland city with a famous Gothic cathedral, a warm carnival-and-Kölsch social culture, strong fiber, and good rail links across western Germany and into Benelux. More relaxed and affordable than Munich or Frankfurt, if not conventionally pretty outside the old core. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Cologne is the friendly, well-connected Rhineland pick: a media-and-creative hub (Germany's TV/advertising capital) with plenty of coworking and laptop-friendly cafes, fast fiber, and an easygoing, sociable culture famous for its carnival and Kölsch beer halls. It's same-timezone with home, cheaper than Munich or Frankfurt, and superbly placed for rail to Düsseldorf, the Ruhr, Brussels and Amsterdam. The trade-offs: grey weather, a city centre heavily rebuilt post-war, and Schengen days ticking.
best_months: May to September; lively carnival in Feb/Mar; grey wet winters

summary: A prosperous, green city cradled in vine-covered hills: the heart of German automotive engineering (Porsche, Mercedes), with strong industry, good fiber, leafy surroundings and nearby Black Forest access. Comfortable and well-off rather than buzzy or cheap. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Stuttgart is the prosperous-engineering pick: a wealthy, surprisingly green city ringed by vineyards and wooded hills, anchored by world automotive names (Porsche, Mercedes-Benz, Bosch) and a deep manufacturing-tech base. It has solid coworking, fast fiber, an affluent quality of life and quick access to the Black Forest and wine country. It's same-timezone with home. It's a settled business city more than a nomad-buzzy or budget one, and Schengen days apply.
best_months: May to September (vineyards, beer gardens); grey winters

summary: A beautifully reconstructed medieval Franconian city: an imperial castle and old town, a famous Christmas market, a rare-for-its-size U-Bahn, an international airport, and a compact, peaceful feel. Smaller and calmer than the big hubs, with good value. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Nuremberg is the compact-and-charming Bavarian pick for people who like things smaller: a gorgeous reconstructed medieval old town under an imperial castle, peaceful and walkable, yet with a genuine subway system, an international airport and not much missing compared to the big cities. Coworking and fiber are solid, costs are reasonable, and it's same-timezone with home, with Munich and the Franconian countryside close. It's quieter and less international than the major hubs, and Schengen days apply.
best_months: May to September; magical Christmas-market December; cold grey midwinter

summary: A postcard Swiss lakeside town beneath Mount Pilatus and Rigi: a medieval old town with the famous Chapel Bridge, pristine swimmable lake water, German-language immersion, and Alpine excursions on the doorstep. Tiny, gorgeous and (like all Switzerland) very expensive. CET (same hours as Vienna); counts toward Schengen.
remote_work_pitch: Lucerne is the small-scale Swiss postcard option: a beautiful medieval lakeside town wrapped in mountains (Pilatus, Rigi), with clean lake swimming in summer, world-class trains, German-language immersion (a B1 bonus), the KKL concert hall and the Lucerne Festival, and Zurich under an hour away. It's same-timezone with home and superbly run. The catch, as everywhere in Switzerland, is cost (budget high), and it's a compact tourist town rather than a startup hub; Schengen days apply.
best_months: June to September (lake swimming, hiking) and December to March (Alpine skiing nearby)

summary: A lush volcanic Atlantic island with a famous, government-backed Digital Nomad Village: spring-like weather year-round, dramatic levada hiking, a friendly nomad community in Funchal and Ponta do Sol, and low costs. Note: it's Portugal, so it DOES count toward Schengen. WET (one hour behind Vienna).
remote_work_pitch: Madeira is the warm-winter island with an actual nomad infrastructure: the pioneering Digital Nomads Madeira Islands programme runs a free coworking village in Ponta do Sol and a welcoming community, the climate is mild and spring-like all year, the levada-trail hiking is world-class, and costs are low (rooms ~EUR400-800, Cowork Funchal ~EUR130-150/month). It's an hour behind Vienna, good for US calls. Important caveat for you: Madeira is Portuguese territory, so unlike Morocco or the Gulf it DOES count toward your Schengen 90/180. The island is remote (everything via Funchal) and the Atlantic is cool for swimming.
best_months: Year-round mild/spring-like; especially April to October; the Nomad Island Fest in December

internet.notes: Reliable fiber in Funchal and the nomad village; confirm rural rentals

summary: Morocco's vibrant tourist-and-creative heart: a sensory medina of souks and riads, a warm dry climate, a real coworking/coliving scene (Outsite, Nomad House, Atic), the Atlas Mountains an hour away, and low costs. Atmospheric and energetic, if intense. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); WET (one hour behind Vienna).
remote_work_pitch: Marrakesh is the atmospheric, well-equipped Morocco base where 'the action is for digital entrepreneurs': a dense, sensory medina balanced by the calmer, cafe-and-plaza Gueliz district, with established coworking and coliving (Outsite Marrakesh in a medina riad, Nomad House, Atic Coworking), fiber in most neighbourhoods, 300+ sunny days, and the High Atlas Mountains barely an hour away. A comfortable month runs EUR1,700-2,300. It's an hour behind Vienna (good for US calls). As a Turkish citizen you enter visa-free for 90 days, and it's Schengen-free. The medina is intense and the internet, while fine, isn't European-fast.

summary: A modern, rebuilt Atlantic beach city in southern Morocco: a long sandy bay, reliable sunshine, a relaxed resort pace, very low rents, a growing coworking scene, and the surf mecca of Taghazout just up the coast. Easygoing rather than charming or historic. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); WET (one hour behind Vienna).
remote_work_pitch: Agadir is the budget beach-and-surf option in Morocco: a modern (post-earthquake rebuilt) Atlantic city with a long sandy bay, some of the country's most reliable sunshine, very cheap rents (one-beds ~EUR250-400), a growing coworking scene with beachfront spots and meetups, and the surf hub of Taghazout 40 minutes north. It's an hour behind Vienna. As a Turkish citizen you enter visa-free for 90 days, and it's Schengen-free. It lacks the historic charm of Marrakesh or Essaouira (it's functional and resort-like), and the internet is workable rather than fast.

summary: A calm, handsome Gulf capital between dramatic mountains and the Arabian Sea: clean, safe, traditional (low-rise white architecture), with warm winters, snorkelling and wadis nearby, and a new remote-work visa. Pricier and quieter than Dubai, with modest coworking. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+4.
remote_work_pitch: Muscat is the calm, scenic Gulf alternative to Dubai: a low-rise, traditional capital wedged between the Hajar Mountains and the Arabian Sea, exceptionally safe and clean, with glorious warm winters, snorkelling, wadis and desert on the doorstep, and (since July 2025) a self-employment/remote-work visa for longer stays. It's on Gulf time (UTC+4), so it overlaps your Dubai-client hours perfectly and your Tokyo/Australia mornings. As a Turkish citizen you get easy short-stay entry, and it's Schengen-free. Coworking is modest (Impact Hub, Premier Business Center), it's car-dependent, and summers are brutally hot.
best_months: October to April (warm, ideal); brutally hot May to September

internet.notes: Reliable fiber and 4G/5G in the city; VPN useful for some VoIP services

work_spots.notes: Monthly memberships from ~50 OMR (~EUR130); modern amenities and networking

Verify current rules before travel.

car_free.public_transport: Limited buses; taxis and car hire are the norm
car_free.airport_to_center: Muscat (MCT) ~20min (~20 EUR taxi)

startup_community.scene_summary: A calm, traditional capital with a small business-and-expat scene and modest coworking, not a startup hub (Dubai dominates the region). Best for safe, scenic, warm-winter living with superb nature, not networking.

bonus.classical_music: The Royal Opera House Muscat, a surprising and beautiful venue with an international season
bonus.dance_scene: Minimal partner-dance scene (conservative country)

summary: A gleaming, ultra-modern Gulf capital: futuristic skyline, world-class museums and (post-World Cup) sporting infrastructure, tax-free incomes, top safety and internet, warm winters, and a beach-and-desert coast. Expensive housing, conservative culture, and car-dependent. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+3.
remote_work_pitch: Doha is the polished, high-infrastructure Gulf base: a futuristic skyline on the Corniche, world-class museums (the Museum of Islamic Art), gleaming post-World Cup stadiums and transit, flawless safety, fast internet, warm sunny winters, and tax-free living, all on UTC+3, which lines up well with your Dubai clients and your Asia/Australia mornings. As a Turkish citizen you get easy visa-on-arrival entry, and it's Schengen-free. The downsides: housing is expensive, the culture is conservative, there's no true nomad visa (a tourist stay is the route), and it's car-dependent with brutal summers.
best_months: November to March (warm, ideal); brutally hot May to September

internet.notes: Fast, reliable fiber and 5G; among the best networks in the region; VPN useful for some VoIP

work_spots.name: Coworking (Msheireb / West Bay)
work_spots.notes: Modern coworking in the downtown/business districts; corporate-leaning

work_spots.name: Souq Waqif & mall cafes
work_spots.notes: Atmospheric Souq Waqif cafes and the air-conditioned malls for wifi work

stays.name: Apartment (Msheireb / The Pearl)
stays.why: Housing is the big cost; Msheireb (walkable downtown) and The Pearl (marina living) are the prime central areas

visa.details: Non-Schengen, so does NOT count toward your 90/180. Turkish citizens get visa-free/visa-on-arrival entry (~30 days, extendable once). Qatar has no dedicated nomad visa: a tourist stay is the practical route, and you must not work for a Qatari employer on it. Verify current rules before travel.

air_water_safety.safety_notes: Among the safest countries anywhere; conservative dress/customs apply

ecommerce_events.name: Major international conferences, sports and trade events (Web Summit Qatar, etc.)

weather.conditions: Desert; brutally hot humid summers, warm sunny winters, almost no rain

options.name: Corniche/hotel beaches; The Pearl; desert beaches (Khor Al Adaid)
options.quality: Warm Gulf beaches at hotels and The Pearl, plus the dramatic 'inland sea' desert beaches; swimmable most of the year (very warm in summer)

options.name: City and hotel gyms
options.notes: Excellent (post-World Cup) sports facilities; plus watersports and desert sport

gym.running_options: The Corniche promenade and Aspire Park

day_trips.hiking: The Khor Al Adaid 'inland sea' and dunes (UNESCO-listed), desert safaris, the mangroves at Al Thakira, and the heritage fort at Al Zubarah

sailing.notes: Marinas and warm-water sailing, plus dhow cruises and watersports along the Gulf

new_hobbies.venue: Khor Al Adaid dunes; The Pearl marina
new_hobbies.notes: Dune-bashing and sandboarding at the dramatic inland sea, plus traditional dhow sailing, are the signature Qatari experiences

coworking_retreats.notes: City coworking rather than retreats

car_free.walkability: Msheireb and the Corniche are walkable, and the new Doha Metro is clean and cheap, but the city is largely car-and-taxi oriented
car_free.public_transport: Modern Doha Metro + buses + cheap ride-hailing; better than most Gulf cities
car_free.airport_to_center: Hamad (DOH) ~15min by metro/taxi (~20 EUR taxi)

startup_community.scene_summary: A well-funded, corporate-leaning business hub (finance, energy, sport, media via beIN/Al Jazeera) with modern coworking and big international conferences, though it's more corporate than scrappy-startup. Decent for high-end networking; Dubai is the regional magnet.

summary: Vietnam's rising coastal nomad capital: a long beach (My Khe), a modern but relaxed city, dirt-cheap costs, a fast-growing coworking scene, and the UNESCO towns of Hoi An and Hue nearby. Excellent value; note the Sept-Oct typhoon season. Non-Schengen (doesn't burn your 90/180); UTC+7.
remote_work_pitch: Da Nang is the value-and-balance pick in Southeast Asia: a modern beach city on Vietnam's central coast with the long My Khe Beach, a relaxed pace between buzzy Ho Chi Minh City and touristy Hoi An, a fast-growing coworking scene (Seaview, ACE, Enouvo, Nomads Coworking, mostly near the beach), gigabit-grade fiber, and rock-bottom costs (a comfortable month is ~$700-1,100, desks ~$72-82). It's on UTC+7, lining up with your Asia/Australia clients. As a Turkish citizen you use Vietnam's 90-day e-visa, and it's Schengen-free. The main caveats: the Sept-Oct typhoon season and a scooter-oriented layout.
best_months: February to May and August (warm, drier); typhoon risk Sept-Oct, cooler wet Nov-Jan

internet.notes: Fast, cheap fiber; many coworking spaces offer gigabit-grade connections

work_spots.name: Seaview / ACE / Enouvo Space
work_spots.notes: Day rates ~$5, monthly ~$72-82; ACE has phone booths and pickleball meetups; Seaview has ocean views

work_spots.name: Beachside cafes
work_spots.notes: Vietnam's strong cafe-work culture; the An Thuong 'expat' area is full of them

stays.name: Apartment (My Khe / An Thuong)
stays.why: Modern 1-beds near My Khe run ~$350-500; among the best value in the library

visa.details: Non-Schengen, so does NOT count toward your 90/180. As a Turkish citizen, use Vietnam's 90-day e-visa (single/multiple entry, applied online before travel). No dedicated nomad visa yet.

air_water_safety.safety_notes: Very safe; use bottled/filtered water; mind scooter traffic

ecommerce_events.name: Active nomad meetups, coworking events and language exchanges

weather.conditions: Tropical; hot dry spring/summer, wet Oct-Dec, typhoon risk Sept-Oct

options.name: My Khe Beach, Non Nuoc Beach
options.quality: Long sandy beaches with warm water; My Khe is famous and swimmable much of the year (rough in typhoon season)

options.name: City gyms
options.notes: Cheap; plus beach running, surfing and Son Tra cycling

gym.running_options: The My Khe beachfront and the Son Tra peninsula roads

day_trips.hiking: The Son Tra peninsula (Monkey Mountain), the Marble Mountains, the Ba Na Hills (Golden Bridge), and the UNESCO towns of Hoi An (~40min) and Hue (~2h)

sailing.notes: Beach-and-surf rather than a sailing scene; boat trips to the Cham Islands

new_hobbies.venue: My Khe/Non Nuoc surf breaks; Hoi An cooking schools
new_hobbies.notes: Da Nang has a mellow winter surf season; nearby Hoi An is one of Asia's great places to learn Vietnamese cooking

options.name: Da Nang / Hoi An colivings
options.notes: A maturing coliving/coworking scene along the beach and toward Hoi An

coworking_retreats.notes: Growing coliving scene

car_free.walkability: The beach and An Thuong areas are walkable, but the city is spread out and scooter-oriented; Grab (ride-hailing) is cheap and everywhere
car_free.public_transport: Limited buses; cheap Grab bikes/cars and scooter rental are the norm
car_free.airport_to_center: Da Nang (DAD) ~10min (~6 EUR taxi)

startup_community.scene_summary: A fast-growing nomad-and-remote-worker community with an expanding coworking scene and regular meetups, though it's a lifestyle hub more than a startup ecosystem (Ho Chi Minh City is Vietnam's startup capital). Good for community and connections.
